## 8. 5 Blind Spots Holding Your Creative Agency’s Growth Back

Every creative agency has talent, and
that's not what slows growth. What
actually holds agencies back are the
blind spots you didn't even realize were
there. These blind spots quietly weaken
your work, slow your team down, and cap
your growth, even if everything feels
mostly fine. In this video, I'm going to
show you the five blind spots I see in
many creative agencies, from the small
teams to the multi- sevenfigure teams.
Fixing these will elevate your work and
remove the friction that's been holding
you back from sustainable growth. First
blind spot, having vague or unclear
briefs. So, it's actually from what I've
seen the number one cause of
internal revision. Having to go back to
drawing board, having to spend long
nights or over the weekend having to
redo work internally before it's even
presented to the client. And I'm not
talking about client revisions. I'm
talking about internal revisions.
It's the reality that the team
is guessing instead of executing because
out of clarity of where it is that they
need to be. What can fix this is having
one clear source of truth for this
project. reference examples, creative
brief, understanding and having the team
really truly understand the big idea and
articulating this in a really meaningful
way and how the big idea directly
translates to the client's objectives
and how it's going to help them
accomplish that. Without the team
understanding where they are headed and
what is going to be needed on this
project, you are going to end up in a
place where you have to directly be
involved at every single stage of the
project or you're going to end up with
revisions. Then you have slow or
inconsistent creative decision-m is the
second blind spot. And ultimately most
often this is a bottleneck from the
founder or a bottleneck from the
creative director. Most often again the
founder and this creates for the endless
back and forth because the team didn't
understand where things were. The
reality is actually kind of falls into
two different camps. Overcommunication
and undercommunication. In
overcommunication, they're getting too
much feedback too frequently, causing
lots of confusion or undercommunication,
they didn't have the information that
they needed and they just went off the
rails and things that weren't understood
and they're not getting feedback in
time. The fix is having
decision-m criteria of where the project
actually is going and having appropriate
check-ins. So the reality of how I see
this from a creative leadership
perspective, you have, let's look at the
timeline of a project, you want to
invest quite a lot at the beginning, a
little bit in the middle, and a little
bit more at the end. If you think about
this as a percentage perspective, you're
going to spend about 60% of the total
creative director strategy time at the
beginning of the project, right at
the launch between that first
30-ish total percent of design time.
Then you check in around halfway through
the project and then you're checking in
when it's most mostly complete
75% complete of the actual work the
final deliverable just to push that work
just that little extra to get it to the
final component. The reality is is that
if you start the project completely
offkilter, so let's say that we are
supposed to be going from San Diego to
New York City, you need to make sure
that you have the right direction that
we are headed because if you start the
project headed in the wrong direction,
it takes a lot longer for you to be able
to course correct it. And if you're able
to get it really locked in right at the
beginning, it makes things a whole lot
easier long term. Then [snorts] the next
bottleneck is a misalignment of what
done looks like. So, and this is
a the failure to communicate on
management's part, but the team needs to
have a full clear picture of what
completed looks like. If they have
different expectations of what the
completed version looks like, then they
are not going to really truly be able to
deliver upon it. And it's having unclear
quality standards. So, here's
the thing that I want you to be able to
understand is that dependent on how it
is that you are delivering the creative
direction or the delegation of what it
is that you're assigning to your team.
And this is across the entirety of the
team, not just creative and strategy,
but the way that you actually give them
authority. So, what does complete look
like? And there's multiple different
stages of authority and completion. So,
I'm going to use a very simple context.
So, let's say that we looking at
researching something. And so, I want
you to do the research and bring me back
to just the raw research or the next
stage of authority. I want you to do the
research. I want you to summarize it
and come to me with the
conclusion that you came from that
research. Or I want you to go do the
research. I want you to come to me with
the conclusion. I want you to determine
exactly what the next step is going to
be to solve for this problem. Or I want
you to do all of that, come with a
solution, come to me just for the yes or
no. Or I just want you to do it and then
let me know what happens. It's really
important that we have delivery clarity
and visual alignment and that the team
is under able to understand that they
have to share rationale. If you are not
sharing rationale or they are not
sharing rationale, it creates a whole
lot of confusion. Then the [snorts] next
piece is the team not actually owning
the outcome. So the founder ends up
becoming the safety net and this is
mostly our fault for doing this. So
tasks get completed but the problems
remain unressed or there was a checklist
that was incompleted or there was
spelling errors or there was issues
things that were not understood and
there should be true accountability
rules SOP checklists that really create
for a lot of understanding. But here's
the number one thing that
is causing for this issue is as the
founder, we ultimately know that we hold
responsibility for what we said we were
going to do. And if we don't deliver, we
have an ownership ourselves of it. And
so because of that, we end up putting on
our Superman or Superwoman cape
and we end up going in and saving the
day. This actually incentivizes and
creates for the wrong dynamic on your
team. You need to not be the superhero.
You need to not be fixing other people's
mistakes. You need to allow for them to
fix them themselves. The reality is is
that for me, and I'll speak from
a first-person perspective, is that I
grew a lot in my career, in my
understanding, in my skill level, by not
actually having someone else fix my
problems. I had to fix them on my own.
And so, it allowed for me to learn
rapidly. It allowed for me to iterate
faster. It allowed for me to be able to,
frankly, in some instances just suffer
the consequences of my own failures to
understand or being able to meet that
specific moment. And it allowed for me
to grow quite a lot. And you are doing
two things. One, you are suffering
yourself for coming in and being the
superhero. and two, so is your team.
Stop being that person and allow for
them to take accountability and
ownership. And then finally, there's a
lack of creative and project process.
So often I see that agencies are
arguing that every single project is
different. It's bespoke. It's a special
snowflake. And so there's no rhythm.
There's no operational memory. The
reality is is that there are patterns
for every single one of your projects.
There are workflows that you should be
having set up as templates. There are
steps. There is a rhythm. And you need
to set them up so that your team is
following this rhythm, this system, this
process, this the step-by-step system
that your agency should be operating
under. And if you're not following some
kind of system, no wonder there's chaos.
And no wonder you're stuck with your
face planted firmly against the glass
ceiling. It is creating for a lot of
bottlenecks. Once you identify these
five blind spots, you'll start noticing
how much faster your team moves, how
much clearer your work becomes, and how
much less you're needed in every
project. But fixing blind spots is only
the start. You also need a delivery
system strong enough to support better
creative work. And that's why I
recommend you watch my next video where
I break down exactly how top agencies
structure their delivery infrastructure
to operate smoother and grow sustainably
without chaos.

## 9. 5 Costly Hiring Mistakes That Are Killing Your Creative Agency

If you manage a team, you know how
incredible it can feel when you hire an
A player. But, we also need to take
responsibility when things don't go to
plan. Most agency owners don't lose out
because of bad clients or bad work. They
lose because of bad hires. And the real
cost of a bad hire isn't just the
salary. It's the time, the morale, the
clients affected, and the months that
you spend cleaning up the mess. So, in
this video, I'm going to walk you
through the five most costly hiring
mistakes that creative agency owners
make, and most importantly, how you can
go about fixing them. So, the first
mistake is hiring too quickly in order
to fill the role. So, desperation drives
bad decisions. And there's so many
instances where we end up hiring the
wrong fit or the wrong skills or the
wrong values because we are rushing. And
that happens over and over and over
again. And the reality is is that we end
up being back to square one, not now,
but when we end up getting into the
place where we have a mess in order to
clean up. So, the reality is is that we
actually need to slow the process down.
And it's incredibly important that we
make sure that we are very pragmatic
about how we go about hiring. We need to
be thinking about things far enough in
advance so that we have a pool of
candidates that allow for us to be sure
that we are hiring the right person. So,
we need to be evaluating based on the
true culture of the agency. We need to
make sure that we have a clear
understanding that they can do the
actual role. But, here's one of the most
important factors that I see that will
make the most meaningful difference is
having them do a real-world task. So,
you can have a freelancer or a team
member to be able to bridge this gap
that you have while you're going through
this process to make sure that you're
hiring a true A player. But, here's the
thing is that in this task, you want to
be able to evaluate for not only the
ability to do the job, but you want to
be able to evaluate for the thinking.
So, there's tests within the actual task
that you're going to put in front of
them. So, as an example, if you have a
strategist, you are going to put some
things in front of them that allow for
them to make the appropriate actions.
So, you maybe you have a link to a call
that was what with a client or a brief
that's going to give you the insight or
a an ability for them to actually go and
actually do some research. Depending on
the amount of time that is going to take
them to complete this, you may need to
con- compensate them for this, but that
it's so much cheaper to pay for multiple
candidates to go through this this
specific test or task before
actually hiring the wrong person because
the hiring of the wrong person is
incredibly more expensive. Number two is
failing to see behind the mask. So,
reality is is that when you're going
about hiring, you're actually seeing the
absolute best version of this person.
And your job is to find the real person
behind this mask and making sure that
you're very clear. And so there, again,
like I said, they are the best version
of themselves in this interview process.
So, if there's any red flags, there's
any issues, this is your moment to back
away and ultimately continue your
search. So, you do need to go through
the tough questions. You need to go
through them more than once. You need to
make sure that you're going about having
an incredible amount of detail. So, the
reality is is especially for
senior-level team members is that
when you are asked about a
specific client scope of work or
something that you've done, you're able
to go into detail in the most
infinitesimal version of it. And you
need to be able to have your team
members do the same thing. They need to
be able to explain these items allowing
for you to be able to understand the
level of detail that they are going to
actually understand what it is that
you're asking for and how they're going
to actually go about this in practice.
One of the things that I do find really
important is being able to get
this conversation into what feels a bit
more casual. And so the reality is is
that interviews can feel quite taxing
for the candidate as well. So, making
sure that you bring them into what can
feel a bit more like a casual setting.
So, you may start with something small,
understanding a little bit about them,
that sort of thing that allows for them
to feel a little bit more comfortable
and making sure that you have this
understanding. The piece that I want you
to really understand here is you are
going to need to do a couple of things
to make sure that you're not doing this
in the wrong fashion. So, the first is
you do want to make sure that you call
references. You do want to make sure
that you ask that reference about their
character, not just about the
performance, about who they are as a
person. How do they interact with other
team members? How do they interact with
clients? How do they manage things under
pressure? And how do they go about
solving for these specific components
that will come up inevitably in their
role. And so, making sure that you
understand these facets of that
candidate is incredibly important.
Number three is misaligned
expectation. So, this is one that I see
come up a load and it just it's very
fraught in the industry is that we end
up having ambiguity for new hires and
they don't end up having the
understanding of what they need in order
for them to be able to be successful.
And so, good people do not stay
where they feel lost or they feel
confused or they don't know how to be
successful in the role. So, you need to
have really meaningfully defined roles,
responsibilities, and ultimately exactly
what success looks like. I think it's
vital that you actually have some
non-negotiables around this role and
exactly black or white whether the
specific item was met. A players want to
succeed in their role. They want to
actually not only just achieve what is
necessary, but they want to go above and
beyond. So, it's vital that they
understand what that looks like and that
they're meaningfully able to actually
put that into practice. What you need to
do in order to be able to allow for this
to happen is that you should have 30,
60, 90-day milestones throughout the
actual onboarding process. Allow for
them to understand exactly what it looks
like to be successful in this role. Get
them to agree. Get them to be able to
reiterate what exactly these
expectations are so that they can turn
it into meaningful commitment allowing
for them to be the best versions of
themselves at your agency. Number four
is failing to communicate the company
culture.
So, you should be interviewing first
off, interviewing by company culture.
Bluntly, skills does not actually mean a
culture fit. So, you can have an
absolute great execution person on the
team, but ultimately if there's
misalignment in culture, it actually
creates an incredible amount of friction
in client engagement, in internal
collaboration, and it ends up killing
morale, and it hurts the output of not
only this person, but of every single
person that they interact with. And so,
this cannot be overstated. This is an
incredibly important component of hiring
a really meaningfully impactful
player is actually having them have a
great culture fit. So, you need to
communicate culture early. You need to
be interviewing them based on culture,
and actually having a reasonable set of
questions that are being asked for every
single person, and making sure that
you're onboarding and reaffirming the
culture of the agency. And so, here's
something that's really important is
that I largely I believe that you should
have clarity around what your culture is
and what your values are, and you should
be interviewing based on those. And that
every single person should go through
these stages is generally speaking, you
have a culture interview that's the
first interview to just make sure before
they actually get to hiring manager, and
then you have hiring manager going
through interviewing this person. You
then have a task that they are going to
go about, and then you have the final
interview where I generally recommend
that the job offer is made. But, you're
going to go through these interviews to
ensure that this person actually hits
all of the qualifications not only in
the individual contributor components,
but also in culture. And then finally,
five, which is one of the
biggest gaps that I see happening is
hiring without meaningful documentation.
So, it actually ends up creating a load
of chaos from day one. And the reality
is is that every single person comes
into your agency, and then they end up
having a perception around who you are.
And if they feel lost, they're unsure,
they feel like they can't do a good job,
it actually creates a whole lot of
tension for this specific person. And
so, the existing team is going to be
continually interrupted, so they don't
end up having a good experience with
this new person, and it ends up creating
a dynamic where someone is going to want
to leave because they are struggling
either interacting with new hires, your
existing team members, and so they end
up leaving because they are struggling
onboarding new people, and so they end
up feeling taxed by that while they're
still having to do their individual
roles or you end up having this A-player
that comes in unsure about how to go
about actually integrating into your
agency and then they end up leaving for
the same reason. So you should be
documenting how things go about being
done, your workflows, your onboarding,
your SOPs of exactly what it means and
what it means to be complete and the
work they're ultimately doing. And so
every hire should get the same
information so that they can hit the
ground running faster, better. But
throughout that onboarding process, you
now have the ability to be able to
provide them with the collateral, the
knowledge, the information that they
need in order to be successful. The
reality is is that you have a really
meaningful force multiplier. So you can
either be 100% behind an employee or
you're not. And that moment that
trust wavers, you subconsciously start
to just pull support. And so you need to
either be fully behind your team, really
understanding where they are. So no
matter where they are, you're able to
actually meet them appropriately, being
able to trust them, provide them the
full support, and set them up so that
they're more successful. But if you
don't do that and you start to pull
support, they are set up to fail. So you
need to be able to go all in, give them
the tools, the guidance, the knowledge,
the support, the mentorship so that they
have every single chance to succeed. But
the moment that that trust wavers, you
need to make a change and not have that
person continue. Every bad hire doesn't
just cost you money. It costs you
standards, it costs you morale, and time
that you're just never going to get
back. But hiring the right people is
only half of the equation. Once they're
in, how you manage them based on their
capacity and commitment is what
determines whether your team performs
meaningfully or they're going to
plateau. So watch this next video on how

## 34. Client Acquisition Isn’t Hard for Creative Agencies. It’s Misunderstood.

Client acquisition can feel hard for
most creative agencies. And it's not
because it's difficult, but because
mostly it's misunderstood. Most agencies
try to perform marketing. They are
posting consistently. They're chasing
tactics. They're experimenting with ads.
And they follow whatever is ultimately
trending, and they wonder why it ends up
feeling inconsistent. But client
acquisition isn't about performing for
attention. It's about building a system
that allows the right clients to raise
their hand in the right moment. So, in
this video, I'm going to show you why
acquisition feels harder than it should
and how it should shift from marketing
activity to structured acquisition
systems that compounds over time. Let's
jump into it. So, let's start with why
it ends up feeling so hard is that we're
end up kind of posting at random. We're
doing things in the moment. We are
creating for so much kind of ad fatigue.
If we are running ads, we run into cold
outreach burnout that we're doing so
much and feeling like we're kind of
selling to someone that hasn't
interested in us talking to them. It
ends up creating this like very feast or
feminine oriented mentality and it's
hard because it's entirely reactive
based on the fact that I need revenue
now and I don't have revenue for
tomorrow. So we are in this like
consistent cliff oriented feeling of
survival mode and the performance trap
that ends up creating is most agencies
are are chasing reach. They're chasing
engagement. They're looking at the
algorithms. They're trying to chase
these different trends of this agency
over here is doing X, Y, and Z. And they
end up confusing visibility with
acquisition and marketing activity
should actually equal an acquisition
system, not just having eyeballs looking
at you. And so we want to start to shift
from performance to system. So how can
we go about that like self- selection
acquisition framework that allows for us
to have the ability to bring people in
when we need them, when we want them
instead of trying to convince cold
audiences. We want to create mechanisms
that allow pre-qualified prospects to
signal interest. So let's talk about the
three layers of this real acquisition
system. So the first step vital
component the foundation of everything
is the position clarity. Who is it for?
What is the problem? What is the
outcome? So who am I working with? What
industry? What profile? What ICP? What
is the persona? What is the problem?
That one specific challenge that we are
solving for and then what is the outcome
that they are looking to accomplish. It
is core to everything that you are doing
and you need to have this really well
understood in a simple format. If you
don't this becomes problematic going
into any of the others. Spend the
appropriate amount of time here because
it is incredibly vital. Then we need to
understand the signal mechanism. What
causes the right prospect to raise their

understanding of what type of place do
they find themselves? So thinking about
it this way frankly are they problem
aware? Are they solution aware? And
where are they in those kind of phases
of making decision-m criteria? Are they
actively looking for agencies because
they are solution aware and they know
that they need to be talking with
agencies or are they problem aware and
there's a little bit of of an
understanding of they need to understand
what is even possible from a solution
perspective to allow for them to make a
determination that they're looking for
an agency to solve this problem. And
based on where they are, it allows for
you to understand the type of mechanism
that you should be using. Are you
talking about a case study that allows
for them to understand here's the agency
because they are looking for an agency.
Are you looking for content that allows
for you to educate because they are
problem aware or maybe not even problem
aware but they're definitely not
solution aware that allows for you to
start to educate them a little bit
towards the place where they understand
that they're needing an agency. And
again another example in solution aware
like a direct offer here's exactly what
we can ultimately be doing that is like
a very kind of outbound oriented
direction and ultimately that like clear
true problem framing. So is it like a
book? Is it a white paper? like what
exactly is it that we are doing that
allows for them to understand to be able
to take that next step. And then the
third layer ultimately relatively simple
but once they signal what do you do? Are
you putting them to a call? Is there a

Are they booking a call and then filling

grow your business. If client

should, it's not because you need more
tactics. It's usually because there are
blind spots inside your agency that are
quietly weakening your positioning,
slowing down your decision-m and
diluting your signal in the market. I
break those down in detail in my video
on the five blind spots holding back
agency growth. Go watch that next
because once you remove those blind
spots, your acquisition system becomes
much easier to build and far more
effective.

agency mentor who can provide you with a
fresh perspective. Let's figure out the
best steps for your agency's growth
together.

## 37. Creative Agencies： Stop Asking Clients What They Think And Do This Instead

Every creative agency owner knows this
moment. You've done the work, you're
proud of it, and you send it over to the
client, and then you wait, and what ends
up coming back is something along the
lines of, "I'm not sure," or, "It
doesn't really quite feel right," or,
"Can we try something different?" And
suddenly you're on revision for, the
team is incredibly frustrated. The
deadline has slipped, maybe even way
past, and nobody can really explain why
or what happened. The reality is is the
problem actually isn't the work. It's
the question you asked and how you
framed it. It's the what do you think?
It is the most expensive question that
this industry asks. And so in this
video, I'm going to show you exactly
what to ask instead. So, let's start
with the why. What do you think is end
up killing your projects? The reality is
is that clients are not trained to give
creative feedback. And so, these
open-ended questions actually end up
just creating a whole lot of anxiety for
them. And so, they end up either doing
one of two things. They end up getting
incredibly vague feedback because
they're not exactly sure how to share
it. And so the cycle is just like
continually going because they're not
exactly sure how to share the
information or they end up avoiding you
entirely and then it goes weeks, months
without any feedback and the exact same
thing results and it's like a project
that was supposed to have been done 3
months ago is still ongoing. And so
every extra revision, every round, it
costs time, it costs money, it cost
morale, it cost the fact that you're not
able to build a client the remaining
part of this project because you hasn't
even started on the next phase of it.
And what it ends up doing is it ends up
making the agency feel or look like
they're incompetent even when the work
is actually great. And the client
actually didn't really have a problem
with it, but you're asking for feedback,
so they're trying to give it to you.
They're not exactly sure how to do it
because this is the first time they've
worked with an agency. This is the first
time they've given creative feedback.
They're not a creative director and yet
we're trying to put them in the position
to be one. So, is that our fault or is
that theirs? And so, let's talk about
how that actually truly ends up costing,
what that ends up doing, this
unstructured feedback process. what that
actually does to the agency. So these
revision cycles, these delays and
projects actually end up meaningfully
eating into your profit margin lines. So
like the the reality of the world is
like going through loads of client data
that on average every single week that a
project goes past due, you're actually
losing about 6% of your net profit
margin. 6%. And for those of you that
are operating in a very tight margins,
that is incredibly problematic. And then
it ends up causing a whole lot of team
motivation because the reality is is the
vast majority of creative people are ADD
or at the very least high input people
and they like when things are a little
bit more dynamic and now you have them
on a project that they're continually
having to pick up and stop and pick up
and stop and then redo work and then
they're being questioned of whether they
even did the right thing in the first
place. So they end up questioning
theirelves and their own ability. So, it
ends up causing this massive drop in
morale and motivation and then the
confidence of the client in your agency
ultimately erodess over time because
they're continually asking you to make
changes. Now, they're actually feeling
like they know what they're doing
because now we've given them that
pedestal to actually provide this
feedback and it ends up creating
incredible problems. And so, projects
are overrunning, it's affecting how
their client work because now you can't
actually start other client work. So, it
ends up even further impacting your
margins. And so one bad feedback loop
can cost an entire client relationship
and even cascading into other
relationships because now it's affecting
other work too. And so here's the shift
that we need to make is the reality is
is exactly as I was trying to articulate
earlier is that you're the expert, not
them. I'm going to let that sit there
for a moment is that you are the expert.
They are not. So the reality is is that
clients have hired you because you know
how to do this. They have no idea how to
do it. So if they were able to provide
feedback in the context of the way that
you're asking it, what do you think? How
should I do this? What direction should
I go? Then ultimately they could be
doing your job or at the very least
doing the CD job, right? And so you need
to be guiding them in the decision that
they're making. How exactly what exactly
are they providing feedback on there's
very specific components of the work
that you're asking for feedback on. But
even before that, there's structure to
the way that you're actually presenting
the work that allows for them to
actually understand how to provide
feedback. what feedback should look like
and what is even the feedback to be so
that you don't end up with well I like
purple. The structure is not
controlling. It's just you being
professional. It's you guiding the
client and ultimately what they paid you
to do in the first place. The best
relationships that creative agencies
have are led by the agency and not the
client. And the reality is is they came
to you because they want your strategic

so that they can have the outcome that
they're here to get. So you need to own
the process from brief to approval. And
if you're not, I'm sorry, but maybe we
should exit stage left. So now let's
talk about what and how we need to be
going about this review framework
differently. So I want to talk through
the actual stages, but the reality is is
that you should never be presenting work
without context. That is incredibly
vital. They need the information in
order to actually make a choice in the
first place. Remind the client of the
brief, the objective, the audience, the
details, and we're going to walk through
the actual stages of them so that they
understand the lens. There's a filter by
which they should be looking at this
work. So first thing is remind them
where are we in the project depending on
where you are and what type of work
you're doing. There's different stages
to the project. So web there might be
wireframes, lowfidelity design, mockups,
final design. In brand work, there may
be different design reviews. There may
be initial concept work. Then there
might be lowfidelity design, high
fidelity design. It might be just in the
logo marks. It might be in strategy

are we in the work? What stage are we?
So that it allows for us to frame what's
going on. Then we need to reframe for

ones that articulated this to you but
often people forget and so we need to
remind them here's the northstar that we
are using that came from you to achieve
your goal. Then we go into information
and the basis that we used for this
project. What information did we
research? What information did the
client provide? What consumer or
stakeholder interviews did we actually
take? So what was some of the takeaways
that we had from that information that
allowed for us to meaningfully be able
to take extract insights from that
information? Then what client thinking,
the actual client that we're now talking
to, what information was taken into
account that they have shared with us
because ultimately people also still
want to see that they had their
thumbrint on the work that is being put
in front of them. After all, they did
hire you to do work and in a lot of
instances on their behalf. And so they
actually want to see that they had some
impact to the work that is being
outputed. So what was the thinking that
they shared that ended up inspiring you?
A little insight, a little piece of
feedback if this is not the first
component or or design review that
you're going through. And then here's
one really important piece that I think
that is so incredibly important that I
very very very rarely see people do is
that the work that you're showing to
them is not the first piece of work that
you worked on. There was loads of
iterations, little changes, little
adaptations, little things that you
decided. And the reality is is that
journey does a couple of things. It ends
up showing the client that you actually
were thoughtful and intentional in the
way that you went about things. You made
lots of creative decisions throughout
the actual work purposefully so that you
can have the most meaningful outcome.
You decided to discount something
because of x y andz reason. You decided
that this worked better because of this
specific data point. You decided to go
here because of this specific component
of a client feedback that allowed for
you to be able to get and arrive where
you are. And so you don't have to show
every single piece of work that you've
done, but allows for them to understand
by the time that they actually see the
final reveal of the work that you've
gone through lots of thinking and
iterations and considerations that
ultimately there was no other reasonable
choice to have made in the situation
that you ended up landing at the advice
that you ended up providing. The other
thing that it also ends up doing is it
allows them for them to see there was a
lot of work that went on behind it
because in a lot of instances people
have no idea what that actually takes to
produce and so the more that you
actually put this in front of them, the
more ideal that it ends up becoming. Now
the question now that we've gone through
this entire prelude to sharing the work
we are going to ask questions now and we
need to be very specific about them not
this open-ended question of what do you
think but again remember we have to
consider the stage of the project. So
let's say that we're in a wireframe
stage of a web project. The reality
would be is you know we've taken all of
these considerations. Here's all the
information that's currently on your
side. Here's the information that is on
typically on other competitor sites.
Here's some of the information that we
learned from consumer interviews and
stakeholder interviews that we'd like to
include on this specific page. Here's
the layout. Is there anything that you
feel vital in order for a user to make a
buying decision that also should be
included on this page that we have not
considered? Very different question than
what do you think? It's very specific,
very narrow. Actually, no. I think that
we've well covered every single piece of
information that now needs to be
included. Let's say that we are on
lowfidelity concept work, right? The
mockupsoriented side of things. We may
say, you know, these we've gone a little
bit more masculine in the way that we
have drawn these bolder lines. Are we
okay with the masculine kind of feel of
the way that this works? We try to
soften them by doing X. We try to soften
them by doing Y. But does this actually
embody your brand and feel like it
represents you? Well, again, very
different question than what do you
think? And that's how you actually need
to be thinking about it. You need to be
making and asking very specific
questions to get very specific outcomes
and results. That allows for you to be
able to push the work a little bit more.
Allows for the client to actually have
their fingerprint in the work, but also
not so open-ended of what do you think?
Because the reality is what they think
doesn't really matter because they're
often in most instances not the ideal
client of the person that's going to
need to consume this work in the first
place. What actually matters is their
consumer and who's going to be utilizing
this work or who's going to be drawn by
this campaign or the brand or whatever
it is that you're working on. What you
need to do is be able to get the work to
be as best as it possibly can so that it
achieves the client's outcome. The way
that you do that is very specific
oriented questions. So there are
instances where you may have clients
that have this kind of idea of you know
I'll know it when I see it. And here's
something that I want to push back on
because this is a common thing that ends
up coming up. And the reality is that
this is actually a brief problem and not
really a feedback problem. So we
actually kind of need to go back to the

specific that actually ended up creating

how we ended up launching a creative

have gone through it with the client, we

enough for the client to be able to have

The I'll know it when I see a client
really is the client that just doesn't
know what even to say. They're feeling

They're not even sure how to articulate
the fact that they feel uncomfortable.
You know, having been married to a
British person, completely understand

that you need to be able to lift that

be able to have had very clear
objectives, directives, ideas that
allows for them to be able to understand
what's actually going on. Now, in the

an important piece of advice here that
you need to make sure that you have
fully systemized is that you need to
have a formal approval stage in every
project. There should be some component
of a written signoff for each milestone.
Concept, low fidelity, final work, high
fidelity. There shouldn't be verbal
approvals because let's face it, if
we've gone all the way through the
actual cycle, we are now on highfidelity
work, but we now need to actually go and

actually likes concept B rather than
concept A. Now, I'm sorry, but that

concept A was better, no longer matters.
It is completely out the window.
However, if you had it in an email, you
had it written down, it doesn't
meaningfully change things. It allows
for you to charge for us going in a
different direction. So you in order to
be able to limit those rounds, you need
to be able to meaningfully be able to
have that articulated and written down
and you need to control your client a
bit more. So what ends up changing when
you actually fully own the feedback
process is that revisions drop
dramatically and in lots of instances
completely disappear. Approvals happen
faster because clients feel more
confident in the work that is being
delivered because you are leading them
the thing that they wanted you to do in
the first place. The team delivers
better work because they feel clearer in
the direction. Profit margins on on
every project end up improving because
you're not cycling through revision
after revision after revision.
Structured feedback doesn't just reduce
revisions. It builds the kind of client
trust that keeps people coming back. And
when clients keep coming back,
everything changes. The stress of
finding new business every month starts
to fade. But for most agencies, that
stress just never goes away. And there's

of the foundational high-value work that

can often feel like a fuzzy kind of
abstract oriented process. Whether

actually scalable, this episode is going
to be for you. Welcome to the show,
Anastasia.
>> Thank you, Robert. Glad to be here.

when we're talking to them.

particular type of client in that

Honestly, those lists are probably going

## 39. Creative Agencies： The ONLY 6 Questions You Need to Close Deals

Deals aren't lost in the close. They're
lost in the first 10 minutes of the
conversation. Not because the offer is
wrong, not because the pricing is too
high, but because the call turns into an
explanation instead of a diagnosis. So,
if you're doing most of the talking,
you're trying to persuade, and
persuasion is a weak position. So, in
this video, I'm going to walk you
through the six questions that structure
a call properly, so the prospect builds
the case for working with you
themselves. Let's talk about the purpose
of a sales call in the first place. The
call's not to explain the agency. The
objective here is for you to start to
guide the thinking, the problem solution
evaluation direction for the prospect.
And frankly, the prospect must reach the
conclusion themselves. They must feel
comfortable with that direction. And
frankly, sales is emotional sequencing
and not information transfer. There is
information that needs to be transferred
throughout it, but it's predominantly
emotional sequencing and understanding
how do you actually go through that
story arc of a conversation that allows
for them to make the best decision
possible for themselves. And know that
there are multiple different types of
decisions. They can decide to work with
you. They can decide to work with
someone else. They can decide to do
nothing. And the reality is is that each
one of those different things are
important that you have to have an
understanding that those are the
potential outcomes. And you're not
trying to guide the decision of where
they're going. You're trying to actually
allow for them to feel comfortable in
the decision that they're ultimately
making. And knowing that those are
ultimately possible. So, here's the
thing. The emotional structure of the
high converting call is going to fall
into essentially three different
categories. You've got the background,
current circumstance, the goals where
they want to go, and then the challenges
of all the things that they've tried or
the context around what's going on in
order for them to actually solve the
actual problem and get to the goal. If
one is skipped, the call is going to
feel flat, and the deal is going to
stall. You need to hit each one of
those, and it's vital that you actually
go through them in a reasonable amount
of detail. So, let's start with the
background and exactly what it is that
we're doing. We're looking to understand
their current circumstance. Where are
they currently sitting? And so, their
operational reality. You want to get
specific. You want to get numbers. You
want to get frequency. You want to get
systems. And you want to move away from
the vague. You want to get to fully
measurable. At this point you're staying
neutral, you're getting curious, you're
just wanting to have an understanding.
And so,
question one, you want to understand
where are you right now? And that could
be revenue, that could be leads, that
could be margin, that could be team
size, that could be systems, that could
be brand recall, but make it measurable.
Not opinions. You want facts, true
bonafide facts, black and white numbers,
measurable detail of exactly where they
are. Question two, how long has that
been the case? So, time frame creates
weight. The duration of the time that
they've been in a specific place
actually allows for them to understand
the actual risk profile of actually
continuing to do things the same. And
that being in that same place is an
inevitability if they don't actually do
something differently. And largely for
you, also understanding the longer that
they've been in a place, the greater the
motivation they actually have to change.
It moves for them to the surface, the
seriousness for them of exactly how
important actually is for them. Within
the background, you're actually doing a
couple things. When you're starting to
put things into the context of real
bonafide numbers, you're actually
starting to establish more authority.
Being able to allow for you to be the
diagnoser and actually having clarity as
to what's going on. And then, you're
able to then take that information that
you have to where they're looking to go
that we're going to utilize in a
contrast later in the conversation that
is incredibly important. Once we have
those informations, we can now go on to
the next piece.
And so, for goals, the objective here is
that we need to be numbers oriented
again, but we need to under- and time
bound outcomes. What exactly are they
looking to get to? And even within it,
you might want to understand the
emotional drivers here behind the
numbers. So, question three, where do
you exactly do you want to be? And
again, numbers driven, be a specific
outcome. Where do you want to be with
revenue? Where do you want to be with
leads? Where do you want to be with
brand recall? Where do you want to be
with total case volume? Where do you
want to be with this specific thing that
is in contrast to where they're
currently standing from a background
perspective. And you want to remove the
vague answers. And if they are not sure
what that is, it becomes very difficult
for you to actually put a plan together.
And you're going to need to actually be
able to pull it out. And maybe you give
them some direction of like, here's
what's actually reasonable. What happens
a lot here, and I know the pushback that
ultimately is being shared is like,
well, maybe they don't know, or maybe
they don't have the insight. Well, then
you need to make sure that you bring the
person that has that insight that is
able to get it. But they do know. They
have a direction here. They have some
kind of understanding. And it's vital
that you actually get there. If you
don't get here, you are in a losing
battle. Just bluntly, you're in a losing
battle. And frankly, so are they. Then
you want to know, why is this important
right now? Why does this actually have
an importance to what's going on? And
there's the timing driver, right? Like,
what exactly is that timing trigger for
them? Of, hey, you know, in order for us
to be able to get into this next round
of funding, in order for us to be able
to reduce our cost of goods, in order
for us to be able to make this next
hire, in order for me to be able to do
X, Y, and Z. That allows for you to
convert that information into an urgency
piece of like, now they're able to admit
to themselves, as well as to you, of
exactly why they need to make this
change. Making sure ultimately, here's
the thing that we want them to actually
understand now is, here I am now, here's
where I want to go, and there's this gap
in between. That allows for them to
fully understand how far of a leap is
it, and what exactly is reasonable and
possible. Like, how achievable is this
thing, and how much help and assistance
am I going to need? But here's the
actual important piece here is that they
need to admit to themselves, not
necessarily to you, but they need to be
able to admit to themselves that they
need support here. And then ultimately
understand how important is this to
them, without applying pressure. They
are articulating it to you. You're not
trying to put in urgency and false
urgency, which a lot of people try to
do, but the actual true urgency that
they feel, without applying pressure.
Then finally, here is an a key important
piece.
Now, we're going to talk about the
challenges. What have they tried? What
hasn't worked? What has been preventing
success? What are the blockers in their
way? What What is the cost of staying
the same? Of what's actually going on?
And you want to get specific examples.
You want the understanding exactly what
is the context of that specific problem
and exactly how that manifested, whether
that's financial, whether that's
strategic, what's actually going on,
what are the consequences of it. So,
very simple question five is what have
you tried? What have you tried to do for
go from X to Y? What have you tried with
with regard to this specific tactic?
What have you tried in specifics to this
specific item? Here's the key. It
actually allows for you to understand
the effort that they've actually put
into solving this problem. It's an
incredibly important piece here. And
then another thing that you want to be
able to do is what's preventing you from
getting there on your own? Question six.
It's that allows for them to actually
identify the constraint that they
actually do need support here and
creates a need and invites ownership of
someone else into this. The reality is
is that each person is like a bit
protective of the sphere around them,
right? Really important component that
allows for you to be able to
meaningfully manage this appropriately
and be able to drive this self-awareness
for them that they need support and that
they are actually motivated to make this
change and that the time is now to do

of the cost of inaction. They need to
see it very clearly to themselves of
exactly what happens if they don't do
it. And the reality is throughout this
conversation what you're doing is you're
positioning the agency as the bridge,
not a persuader. Here's the process by
which we actually follow. Here's an
example of something that we've
ultimately done. Here's a way that we

## 41. Creative Agency Owners： How to Fire Toxic Clients (The Right Way)

So, ever wish you could send that
breakup text message to a client that
it's not you, it's me text and actually
really mean it? Every agency runs into
those clients that just make life
harder? Knowing the right way to break
up with them can save your reputation,
save your sanity, and actually
boost your business. I've worked with
tons of agency owners on this, and I'll
share the five steps and real scripts
that they've used to be able to handle
it smoothly. So, if you're tired of
difficult clients holding your agency
back, stick around. I'll show you
exactly how to turn that tough breakup
into the smartest move that you've ever
made. So, the first step is to recognize
when it is actually time to end a
relationship. So, here's the most true
fire way to know 100% that it is time to
change things. And it's the client that
you receive an email or a text message
or a Slack message or however it is that
you communicate with clients and you see
the message and you think that you
immediately sigh. That client that is
doing that and you have that feeling,
you are in a relationship that is
causing more of an energy drain to you
than positive momentum. And it's that
type of client is the ones that we want
to be looking at. But it's also the
client and typically that's the end
result of them overstepping your
boundaries, giving you lastminute things
that have a really tight
turnaround deadline that you feel like
you're completely scattered. You see the
patterns where they are asking too much
of you. They're not paying you for it.
You end up dreading calls. You end up
losing sleep. You end up working till
the late night. You end up feeling
disrespected. They're showing up late
demanding that you stay longer. And it
just shows that you're it's time to move
on. But you can also do this in a very
mathematical way in actually looking at
what is your effective bill rate or
average hourly earning with that given
client. So taking revenue divided by the
number of hours that you spend on that
client. Most often what I find is the
ones that are on the lowest end of that
chart that you build are going to be the
ones that you like the least. Most often
that's true. The next pieces to decide
on your approach. Do you want to just go
through and eliminate all the ones that
you want to get rid of now or do you
want to do this in a more gradual
oriented approach? And there's kind of
two distinctions from it, right? is that
do you have enough opportunity and
pipeline that's coming in right now that
you just need to create a lot of space
and opportunity for you to be able to
bring on new clients and being able to
even have the time to have those sales
calls and etc. And so you can do
this in the very direct and quick
oriented approach where hey I'm just
eliminating these five clients that's
going to create the capacity for me to
bring on these opportunities that I
currently have. Or you can take the
gradual approach where you are letting
go of clients over time and you select
and the way that had clients do this is
rate clients from A to F and the
worst clients being at the bottom and
ultimately having those clients there at
the bottom being the ones that you focus
on letting go of first. And the typical
mechanism is that you've brought on
revenue that's two to three times the
amount of revenue that that client means
and then you let them go so that you're
doing it in a financially secure place.
So it depends on where you currently are
financially, but ultimately deciding on
the approach is a vital step. Now the
piece is actually to prepare the message
and how it is that you're going to go
about it. The thing that I want to make
sure here is that you do this in the
most seamless way possible. And you
don't want to just, you know, stop
answering emails and just ghost them
because that is incredibly
unprofessional. You actually need to
make sure that it's clear, that it's
polite, that it's done in the most
meaningful way and stay respectful and
focus on fit and not blaming them. And
so you can have a true easy way of going
about this. And so it would be something
like this. you know, hey John, we've
been taking a look at our agency and
where it is that we've been focused our
attention and we decided that we're
making a bit of a shift and we are
headed in this direction and we
realized that with that augmentation
that means that we are not really
focused in the direction and not best
suited to help you with X goal. And so
we want to make sure that we are working
with people that we are really truly
able to have a lot of impact for and we
are not the best fit for you. And so we
want to make sure that you're working
with someone that is set to be able to
help you accomplish your goals and is
going to be the most impactful for you.
And so to help you with this transition,
we have a list of suggested agencies.
Here's two of them. We can either be
part of the conversation and help
transition the conversation or you're
welcome to have a conversation with them
directly or anyone else and we can make
sure that we'll transfer everything over
in a seamless way of possible. And so
understand that you're doing this in a
respectful and professional way so that
you're able to actually save your
reputation. And then you want to make
sure that you offer the right kind of
exit plan. So the reality is is you
should do this. Do you really owe them
anything? Is there something that you
really need to be doing here? But I
generally prefer doing this in the most
graceful way possible. So by offering
referrals as I mentioned by providing
them with the files that they need and
offering a transitionary period that it
doesn't just happen immediately. And the
reality is is that for most of you
contractually you have to give them some
kind of notice period. And so making
sure that you do so in the most
meaningful way. And finally, and most
importantly, this is happening generally
speaking because the client has
overstepped your boundaries. And you
need to make sure that you set those
boundaries with them in a really
programmatic way. So, will they be
upset? Are they going to argue? All
those sort of things. We need to make
sure that we have a framework allowing
for us to be able to manage it as well
as possible. Stay calm. Understand that
this is a shift and change for them.
That you're springing this on them. They
were unaware. And you want to
make sure that you are contractually
sound, that you are doing the things
that you need to, that you are ensuring
that you're doing and not
leaving the person in a worse off place,
but you're actually being most
thoughtful about the process that you're
doing, but also making sure that you are
letting them know where and where you
begin and end. So, here's the official
date that we will need to transfer
everything over. Here's the plan of
action that we would have for this
transition. And hold and stick to it.
remind them of when these things are
coming up that hey, we need to know who
it is that we are passing off this
responsibility to. Is it someone
internally? Is it someone externally?
The thing that I also want you to be
thinking about is how can you reflect
and make sure that you don't end up in
the same place again? Learning from
these different scenarios from these
different clients so that you're able to
bring on better clients. What is it that
we did during the onboarding process?
What is it that we did during the
proposal process that allows for us to
once see the red flags and avoid it next
time? It's vital that we learn from
every circumstance within the business,
allowing for us to improve at every
different stage. And that's how you can
finally break free from the toxic client
relationships without drama. But here's
the thing. Cutting that negativity is
only half the story. Because once the

## 43. Creatives Fail At Time Management & Productivity, Until They Fix This

So, in my experience, creative teams
aren't bad at productivity. They're just
using a system that was never designed
for them. That same brain that allows
someone to be able to think differently,
see patterns other people miss, and
create bold new ideas is the same brain
that ultimately ends up struggling with
structure, focus, and a lot of times
time management. And then, what we end
up doing is we give that creative brain
things like Slack. We give them
notifications, and then we give it
constant context switching, and we try
to call it productivity. Most
productivity systems are built for
operational roles and ultimately just
not creative ones. So, in this video,
I'll show you why creative teams fail at
time management and the structural shift
that actually unlocks consistent output.
So, let's let's start with the kind of
creative brain paradox. The reality is
that what makes a creative person
incredibly powerful, but ultimately ends
up making them also structurally
vulnerable, is that they have a high
sensitivity to stimulus. They are
non-linear thinkers. They are great deep
pattern recognition people, and they
have a lot of kind of idea fluency kind
of going in and out of them, where it's
just like a constant piece. These traits
are often linked to ADD and ADHD people
and patterns. And here's why I'm
bringing this up is that creative
people, most often, are interest-driven.
They're not urgency-driven. They're
looking at here's the pieces that make
my brain tick and have interest to me.
They operate really in bursts of energy.
But, the reality is is that focus is
finite, so if you're like trying to
constantly be able to leverage these
bursts of energy, it ends up being
incredibly draining. So, traditional
productivity assumes steady linear
attention on a constant basis. And
frankly, just the creative attention,
generally this is not true for every
single person, but generally speaking,
is not linear. And that's actually a
feature of you as a creative, not a
drawback. It's actually a feature. It's
what makes you great at your job. But,
when you try to fit it into this
construct of what we have society has
built as productivity and in linear
attention, it actually just doesn't work
for you. And so, here's the thing that
we end up creating is this like
productivity lie. So, we end up trying

framework around the ability to be able
to have it, but it actually doesn't

## 51. Existing Clients, Bigger Profits： The Upsell Guide for Creative Agencies.

So, chasing new clients isn't the secret
to big revenue growth. It's maximizing
the value of the clients that you
already have. In the next few minutes,
I'm going to reveal the proven steps
creative agencies are following to
unlock millions in hidden income without
adding stress, begging for referrals, or
burning out your team. Every successful
agency I've consulted with has used
these same upselling strategies to
dramatically boost their revenue, often
without signing a single new client. It
resulted in better profits, stronger
client relationships, greater client
impact, and real business stability for
every client every time. And today, I'm
pulling back the curtain so that you get
the exact playbook. So, let's start with
what exactly is upselling. So, you
already have a current engagement with
the client, whether that's a retainer or
a project. And over the course of time,
the reality is is that with most
businesses, as one problem or one
challenge gets solved, another one
arises, another one ends up being
discovered, if you will. And so the
reality is is that as it happens within
the business, you should be there with
your clients as a partner, as an adviser
to allow for them to achieve the next
level of growth. And so upselling or
cross-selling, not directly
cross-selling, but upselling versus
cross-selling. Upselling is being able
to add on additional services, add on to
that additional scope, or sell the next
project if you're a project-based
agency. And so, the first step that is
vital here is actually truly
understanding your client's journey. So,
there's a framework that I have that I
use with every clients and it's just
called very simply go deeper. And
there's rules. And one of the rules is
that you are going to make sure that
you're asking meaningful questions in
every single time that you engage with a
client. Uncover new opportunities. Not
necessarily meaning every single call
you're going to be selling them. You
shouldn't, but you need to know their
needs, their goals, and understand the
timeline for them. And so they be
mentioning all sorts of things in every
single client conversation that you have
with them. They may mention a
competitor. They may mention stress.
They may mention a goal. They may
mention internal dynamics and
bureaucracy. They may be mentioning all
sorts of things in these conversations.
And what you should be doing is
inquiring and taking interest, a
curiosity that allows for you to better
understand the client, their journey,
their objectives, their goals. Every
single time you have one of these
conversations, all you're going to do is
write it down because you're going to
use this later. It allows for you to
then be able to identify those upselling
opportunities. So, you're going to start
looking out for signal. how many times
have they been talking about the same
thing. You're going to start talking
about milestones. Now they've achieved
goal one that they've had with you. Or
you're going to start to look at the
client behavior or how they're even
thinking about things as they're going
through different cycles within their
business. You know that they're about to
head into their busy season. You know
that they're about to head into this
kind of component. They just lost a
current team member internally. that
allows for you to be able to understand
how can you be a true partner for them
and understanding what those upsell
opportunities are. And yet, we still
have not made them an offer. And so, the
way that you're going to go about this
is it's a very simple meeting that
you're going to have with your clients
once a quarter to semiannually and even
for those clients that are
projectoriented. If you launched a new
website, you're going to check in with
them six months later to just go over
the impact that that website has had.
Little small things that you've noticed
that ultimately allow for them to be
able to make the impact. And so the
meeting framework is incredibly simple.
All you're going to do is you're going
to go over here are the goals that you
initially came to us for. Just validate
that those are still accurate, that
that's what they were looking to
accomplish. Yes or no? Yes. Great.
Here's the progress to those goals that
you've ultimately made. Here's what has
measurably measurably happened in that
timeline. So, it's been 3 months. Here's
the progress that we've made. It's been
6 months. Here's the progress that we've
made towards those goals. Now, let's
talk about your next goals. Our
understanding is that you're looking to
achieve X, Y, or Z. Now, is that
accurate? Yes. Okay, great. So, then we
start to look at here are the things
that we see within the work that we've
done that has been really impactful.
Here's the things that we see that we
can further improve upon or didn't go
exactly as planned. The reality is that
even the best plans do not go exactly as
you wanted them to. And here's where a
lot of agencies go wrong. They're not
building good relationship foundations
by ignoring the things that they didn't
do well. No partner has done every
single thing perfectly. Not a single
one. And if you are ignoring those
opportunities, you're ignoring those
things that you can continue to improve
on yourself. They know it. You know it.
you're just not being part of the
conversation. So, be part of the
conversation. Then, now that you have
the progress that they've made to their
goals, you've talked about the
opportunities that are currently there
within the existing work that you have
that can further drive them towards
their goals, the goals that they have
upcoming. Now, we're actually going to
talk about those opportunities. And that
timing truly is everything. Making sure
that we're bringing it up in that right
moment. We're not bringing it up
randomly. And so, I have a 7030 rule.
70% of the things that you bring up are
things that your agency can do and 30%
are the things that your agency cannot
have zero involvement in and they know
that you don't that you're just being a
good adviser, a good partner to them,
giving them advice on the things that
they should be doing and maybe even
someone within your partner network that
you can refer them to that would allow
for this goal or this thing to be able
to come to fruition. And so you're going
to have five to 10 things that you're
going to go through of here's things
that we were thinking about of your
biggest opportunity, our recommendations
to you to achieve these goals. And some
of them they're going to take interest
in, some of them they are not. Once they
have given you an understanding, then
you've been in front of that right
decision maker in this conversation. You
are then going to schedule another time
to talk about the potential
implementation of it. They're going to
give you a good indication of whether
that is of any interest. and those
things that are of interest, you're
going to ask them, hey, would it be
useful for us to talk about what that
might actually look like for you to
implement that and what kind of
considerations you're going to want to
be making to allow for that to be
possible? And the likelihood is that if
you have been a good relationship person
and relationship driver and have been an
adviser to them, they are going to want
to have that conversation. They're
already engaged in a relationship with
you. Here's the thing that I have seen

breathe a little bit easier. And today
I'm letting you in on how to make this
your new normal. So little bit of a
caveat here that I just want to mention
for those of you that are working with
enterprise type clients. This method
will still work with your existing
client stakeholder, but they have legal

approvals, all sorts that ultimately are
going to still create some aspect of
delay. So you're not going to get that
instant approval on it. So what ends up
happening for these approvals in the

feedback. You're asking for an approval
on getting things forward. And the
client's either given too many choices
or the request is actually quite vague
and they feel unsure of exactly what it

your team is stuck waiting because you
didn't provide all the information that

expert in what you do. And if you're

these things forward, you're creating
for a bottleneck in them and you're
creating for loads of anxiety. you're
suffering, they're suffering, nobody's
happy, and your project gets delayed,
and you end up making less profit. So,
what I want you to do is I want you to
curate your choices to be able to create
for swift approvals. So, the reality is
is that you want to actually go in with,
let's say that you're going to share
three different aspects of creative, but
what you want to do is you want them to
understand what is your one
recommendation. There should be one
aspect of recommendation of the
direction that you want to go that has
all of that thinking and criteria where
they have all the pros and cons, the
understanding, the criteria that allows
for it to go into it. and don't be going
in with open-ended kind of feedback
requests. So, here's the way that you
want to actually go into creating those
curated choices is you want to remind
them about their goals, what the
considerations were, where they are in
the project, what exactly they want to
be looking at, and how they should be
looking at this specific phase of the

considerations that you ultimately put
into it. So, like why did you choose

things that you took into it? What were
the considerations? What were the
discounts? What were all the directions
that you decided to not go? There was a
plethora of different options that you
could have gone and you decided to not
go in hundreds, maybe even thousands of
them. And there's reasons why, and you
need to give them that context. And
here's the Achilles heel kind of
problem. What ends up happening is this
open-ended feedback request. Let me know
what you think. Tell me what you what
you feel. Is there something else that
you think we should try? The reality is
is they don't know. They have no idea.
And frankly, if they did, they probably
wouldn't be hiring you in the first
place because you're here to be the
expert. You're here to guide them and
provide them with more clarity. And what
ends up resulting if you just ask them
this kind of question, you're getting
more rounds of revisions. They have less
clarity. They don't know the direction.
They feel uncomfortable because
ultimately what they care about is the
outcome for this specific project. But
you haven't given them the tools that
they need in order to be able to do so.
So let's say that the way that you want
to get feedback is in one specific
system. So, hey, you know, the objective
of this specific phase of the project is
to really get a clean understanding of
the direction that you feel from a

to give them what it was that you have

## 55. Free Up Your Time as a Creative Agency Owner (Without Losing Control)

So, here's the truth. Most agency owners
aren't running a business. They're
running a never-ending to-do list
disguised as a business. And the moment
that they stop working, everything just
stops with them. But the agencies that
end up being able to grow fast are the
ones that free up the founders's time
while keeping quality high. They just do
things differently. They build a
simple system, practical system, or SOPs
that their team can actually follow.
So, in this video, I'll show you
how to write SOPs that reduce chaos,
remove you from the day-to-day
questions, operations, and create
freedom without losing control. And this
is part one of our systemize your agency
series. Today, we'll build the
foundation. Next, we'll plug in
these SOPs into the delivery
infrastructure that makes everything run
smoothly. So, let's first start with
what is an SOP. SOPs are designed to be
able to create clarity, repeatability,
and ultimately freedom. They're not
these like really, really long
documents, these like corporate manuals,
if you will. I remember the first
version of an SOP at an agency was like
hundreds of pages long. I want you to
think more recipe, the steps and why it
ultimately matters, the expected outcome
that allows someone to be able to just
take it, run with it, do it.
Ultimately, the ideal place to be would
be someone being able to take exactly
from an SOP and be able to do something
for the first time and being able to
take it from beginning to end,
understanding exactly what to do, step
by step to take, but in a small
bite-sizeoriented version, the same way
that a recipe ultimately would. So the
SOPs that we want to create are for
things that are repeatable, that are
missionritical tasks. So here's how I
want you to think about it. If it's
something that is being done on
a daily basis, a weekly basis, a very
reoccurring oriented basis, those are
the types of things that you want to
prioritize your SOPs for, especially if
they're immediately touching you as the
agency owner. How can I get them off of
my plate and give them to someone else
that allows for them to be able to take
it fully? So where is the specific
thing? How do I do this? You know,
what's the next step? those types of
questions that are coming up for you
every single day. Those are the types of
things you want to be creating an SOP
around. And so I want you to think about
a workflow, if you will. Your agency
functions with loads of different
systems that are working together but
sequentially from beginning to the
signed so when the client first signs a
contract to the deliverable of the so if
you're project oriented or the
ongoing deliverables from a retainer
perspective but realizing that those
sequences of steps that you're taking
are ultimately the different SOPs that
are needed. And so thinking about them
in the bite-sized chunks that are
necessary to allow for you to be able to
build the agency, that workflow allows
for you to take what feels
insurmountable, being able to create
systems for the entire organization, but
actually just doing that small little
bite-sized junk, the Jenga block in the
Jenga tower, if you will. So the
more that you're able to actually
systemize your agency, the less chaos
because there's less questions, there's
more understanding of the thing that
needs to happen next within your agency.
And so what you're starting to go and
drive down into writing an SOP, how to
go about it to create simplicity
and clarity for the team. So you want to
make sure that you're doing so in a
couple of different formats, recognizing
that people can be visual learners, they
can be auditory learners, they can also
do learn by doing. The realization is
that you need to be able to cover the
gamut of each one of those pieces. But
to start with, I want you to think about
building things simply in bullet points,
not paragraph oriented format. keeping
the language as simple as you possibly
can and if possible adding
screenshots, links, tools, templates
that you're able to utilize, assuming
that you have them. The things
that are paramount to include inside
this SOP is what exactly is the purpose
for this? Why do we have this SOP or
this process in the first place? What
does it prevent? What does it do? How
does it create for greater quality? How
does it create for greater client
retention? Like what exactly does it do
within the organization? Then
make sure that you include all the
related documents, systems, softwares,
tools, templates that are related
specifically to that SOP. And then make
sure that it is clear on who owns this.
Who's the person that is ultimately
accountable to this getting done? And
then what is the applicability or when
is this done? The frequency of it or
within a system of a of a workflow, the
mapping of that workflow, when does this
get done and kind of what order? What is
the thing that happens before? What
gives an indication this needs to
happen? And then what are the
next steps after this SOP? Is there
another process that we follow? Is this
submitted to someone for review? What
exactly is the next step following the
completion of this process or is it just
done? Then finally, the expected output.
So what does complete look like? And how
is quality maintained? So if something
goes wrong, who gets involved? Who's the
person that needs to be consulted? How
do you escalate that problem to make
sure that quality is there? So how what
is the checks and balances? How do you
ensure that this actually gets done to
the degree that you want to? One of
quick little tidbit of a piece of advice

is you can record a Loom video or a
