an SOP. And then finally, the piece
that's really important is that you need
to be able to streamline the SOP updates
cuz one of the things that is incredibly
frustrating is making sure that they are
continually updated. So those questions
that come up, the things that ends up
being updated, a system has been
changed. If you're like, let's say a
media management agency, you're running
meta ads as an example, it ends up
creating for problems when the platform
changes. And so, how do you actually go
about either removing unnecessary steps
that's creating for
misalignment? How do you go about
automating pieces that are ultimately
necessary? But who's the person that
owns this SOP and making sure that it's
being reviewed when things need to be
updated? My favorite way about going
about this is if there's a question that
comes up is go back to the SOP. That
means that ultimately the SOP is
unclear. If a problem comes up then this
means that the SOP either needs
to be refined or it needs to be made
more easily available if it was
documented in that SOP so the person is
aware of it but ultimately it's a great
mechanism by which to ensure that the
SOPs are continually updated because if
there's a problem that means that
[clears throat] the system needs to
change in my viewpoint. Then finally
make sure that there is a clear
designated owner. So each SOP needs
someone that is accountable to it. So
who's the person that is accountable
that is make sureures that the actual
outcome takes place and then who's the
responsible party. The
responsible party is the person if
you're familiar with RIY the responsible
party is the person that's doing it. The
accountable party is the person that
ultimately is the one that is
accountable to it. Is the person that's
responsible to make sure that it
actually comes through to fruition that
the expected outcome actually happens.
And so you have the person doing the
task the responsible party and then you
have the person that ensures that it
actually takes place the accountable
person. There may be someone that has
consulted to ensure that this is moving
forward. There may be someone that needs
to be informed just because they let's
say it's an account manager needs to be
informed that this was completed or not
that allows for them to communicate that
to the client. So just ensuring that you
have clarity as to how exactly this is
being owned. And then the thing that
ends up being one of the key
problems for a lot of SOPs is that
they're not made easily accessible. And
so it's vital that this information is
easily accessible to the team.
My favorite way about going about this
is two things. one, having a central
folder that has a table of contents, if
you will, that is a Google doc or a word
document that has links to all
of those SOPs that's broken down by
department. And sometimes you have the
same SOP listed multiple times cuz it
touches multiple departments, but you
have a table of contents that
references out to the other SOPs. And
then you have folders by department that
breaks down each one of those SOPs and
an individual document. Again, think
recipe style to this SOP versus this
long large document that becomes very
time prohibitive to be able to go
through. It needs to be something that I
can easily digest in a couple of minutes
and then go do the thing that I need to
get do get going. But true expert level
in implementing this is having checklist
and SOPs directly within your project
management. So if something is assigned
to someone, they have a link to the SOP.
So if they forget, they have an
easy mechanism to go follow the SOP
first before needing to come to you. The
reality is is that people do not like to
look dumb or look like they forgot
something. And the reality is is
that because of that, they will either
end up doing one of two things. They
will avoid asking the question as long
as they possibly can until it becomes a
problem, or two, they will just not do
the asking at all and just do what they
think is best, which is then
avoiding the process that then ends up
creating a problem. So, we want to avoid
this by making them easily accessible so
that the person doesn't have to ask the
same question twice because sometimes
things don't fully cement. being able to
reference an SOP if they forgot how to
do something that they should remember
how to do. And it just allows for your
team to be able to make action, take
action, and get ultimately to the
expected outcome that you're looking for
in a meaningful way. So now you have the
foundation of a business that runs
without you. With simple SOPs, your team
will actually follow. But SOPs only work
when your team is aligned, productive,
and working inside a structure that
supports them. So before you move on to
part two of this series, make sure your
team isn't accidentally sabotaging your
systems. So, watch this next video where
I break down how high performing

I'll see you there.

## 56. Freelancer vs True Agency Owner Mindset ｜ Shift to Build a Thriving Creative Business

Here's the uncomfortable truth. You
might own an agency, but if you're still
thinking like a freelancer, your growth
is stuck on pause. I'm going to break
down the six mindset traps that keep
agency owners stuck wearing every hat,
hustling more, earning less, and then
wondering why things are not going the
right way. I've seen these hidden
beliefs sabotage even the greatest of
creative leaders, and also watched
agency owners flip the script, step into
real leadership, and finally unlock the
growth that they had been chasing for
years. And if you're ready to leave that
freelancer mode behind and run your
agency like a true business owner, this
is the reset that you need. The first
myth is, I'm just a freelancer and I'm
hired to do the things that other people
need. And the reality is is that you
should start looking at yourself,
believing, and then introducing yourself
as a partner with your clients. The
reality is is that when a business is
engaging with an agency, they're doing
so so that they can solve a business
challenge. And it's no different than
any other business challenge. Whether
that's a lawyer, whether that's a
scientist, whether that's an accountant,
it doesn't really matter what it
is that they're challenged with. They
are needing an expert within that given
field. And you are an expert in your
field in your own right as a creative
being able to solve a business
challenge, whether that's a marketing
challenge, a brand challenge, a website
challenge. You are a needed resource, a
needed partner for them to be able to
reach the next step of growth. And so
you need to one own the impact that
you're having, the need that they have
in order to be able to achieve their
next level of growth. The next myth is
having an agency sounds cool, more
impressive, but I'm not sure that's
actually what I want. What you want is
actually the thing that's most
important. And you need to be specific
in yourself and actually truly
understand what this means for you. What
is this going to create? Because the
reality is that business ownership,
hiring a team, doing all that work, it
does have its challenge. Is this a
legacy thing that you're looking for? or
is this to be able to create for more
income? Are you trying to create wealth?
Are you trying to have more impact? What
exactly is the reason why you want to do
this? Because the reality is is that
potentially being a freelancer to have a
lifestyle business that is providing you
with income is a very valid option if
that's what you want. And no judgment at
all for you. If that's the thing that
you're looking for, then that's the
thing that you should have. Don't let me
or anyone else tell you that you should
have and want something more than what
you want or different than what you
want. But being clear on what that is is
really important. I'm not saying that
that doesn't change and hasn't shifted
even for myself and the things that I've
wanted throughout life. It does. But
making sure that you have what that
north star is for you, that thing that
is driving you, that energy piece for
you is vital to be able to understand
where it is that you're headed. Don't
try to take what you think that you
should want and the belief that you
should want. It is actually what you
want. The next myth is the money that
I'm making should actually fuel my
lifestyle and that I should be able to
enjoy every single piece of it right
now. And so this is where a lot of
people ultimately end up going wrong
because business can have a little bit
of some ups and downs, right? You have
more profitable years, more profitable
months. And if you are consistently
growing your lifestyle alongside your
profit, it actually ends up creating for
a real hinge point for you. And it
actually removes a whole lot of
opportunity and a whole lot of potential
for you. One of the things that's really
important here is that as you continue
to grow, and I'm not saying that you
shouldn't have anything aspects of of
nicities and uh some aspect of
lavishness, you can have that, but I'm
saying that just keep it minimal. The
ideal for you would be as you continue
to grow the income that you're bringing
in, if you're able to maintain your
lifestyle, your basic needs are covered,
your mortgage is paid for, all of those
things are taken care of, then you want
to actually start to just maintain your
existing lifestyle. And every dollar
that you're earning, you're able to
build cash reserves. You're able to
invest those dollars either back into
your business or in another investment
vehicle, allowing for you to start to
create, for you to have some cushion, to
have the ability to have something to
fall back on. When you end up in a place
where you have to make choices, you have
to adjust, you have to pivot, those give
you opportunities in those moments. But
for those of you that have grown your
lifestyle alongside your profitability
growth, you end up in a place when that
happens. when you need to make a pivot,
when the economy takes a turn, you end
up with no options. You end up with
nothing to be able to back fall back on.
And it's vital that you make sure that
you have something to show for all that
hard work that you've had and that you
have some savings. You have some wealth
that you've continued to build. The next
myth [snorts] is if I offer every
service that people want, then I'm never
going to run out of clients. There's
always people that are going to want to
pay me. And here's the thing, it
actually creates for the exact opposite
dynamic. The more things that you end up
doing, the less things that you do well.
the more things that you end up
offering, the less that you're able to
differentiate. So, the less that you're
able to attract, the more that you end
up competing based on price. So, it ends
up actually being a spiral to the
bottom. And it's a true problem. It
really is. And I understand the reason
why. It's because in a lot of instances,
you have someone come in, you have a
good client, they end up trusting you,
and you end up being in a place where
they ask you for these additional
services. And so, you say, "Yeah, sure.
I I can do that." It's something that
seems tangential. It may even be closely
related, and you're like, "I'll just
figure it out." But the reality is is
that in order for you to be able to grow
the business, assuming that is what you
want to do, you need to be able to not
be involved in everything. And even in
those examples is that you haven't done
this. So doing something for the first
time, if you're anything like me,
presumably you didn't do the thing the
best time the first time round. You
didn't just I'm great at this. It's not
at least hasn't been how my life has
worked. And the reality is is that you
should focus on the things that you do
really well. You should focus on the
things that intrigue you, that make you
excited, that you're able to have
greater impact for the client because
that is real. The true value transfer
ends up happening, that exchange of
value for funds of what it is that we
are in the business of is vital in that
moment. So the more that you focus on
the thing that you do really well, the
less that you offer and focus more
actually ends up creating more
opportunity for you. The next myth that
is a big one and I understand this one a
lot because there's been lots of es and
flows myself but no one else can do the
things that I do in the way that I do
them and hiring is a risk that I can't
afford. So here's the thing there's
aspects of truth in that that you may
have decades worth of experience. You
may have a specific way that you go
about doing things and the reality is
that are people going to do it 100% of
the way that you did it? No, they're
not. But the reality is is that if
you're not able to bring on people to be
able to start to do some of the work
that you have, then you're not going to
grow. And that's okay if you want to
stay the freelancer. But if you do want
to have a growing business, an agency, a
true bonafide agency, you're going to
have to be able to start to delegate
things to other people. And my own
journey with this came to the
realization that there was just a big
oriented ego piece of it, and that there
was lots of work that other people could
do. and people that were better at it,
more interested in it, they were able to
do the things that I frankly
just didn't even want to do, that I was
disinterested in, wasn't excited about
doing in the first place. And we need to
start to vacate some of the areas within
the business. And that if you go into
hiring with a plan, you bring on someone
part-time or even a contractor to begin
with, you're able to remove things from
your plate that frankly are draining
your day-to-day that are not allowing
for you to do the thing that you do
really well and it's creating for a lot
of problem within your business. And the
last is if I say no to lowpaying work, I
might not have enough to live on. And so
there's a couple of things, but the
reality is is that what I find to be
most often here is that your lowest
paying clients are often the ones that
take up the most of your time and
attention. And it ends up creating for a
really true block for you to be able to
grow your business. And so the reality
of what I would want you to do here
if you do have lowpaying clients
or you're considering them bringing them
on, it was that opportunity moment that
I was talking about earlier. Is this a
thing that you really truly have to do
in this moment in order for you to be
able to have your own basic life
necessities met? Paying for food, paying
for housing, that sort of thing. And if
you don't do it, then you're going to
end up without it. Okay, fine. That's a
different conversation. We'll take that
and put that aside for a moment. But for
many of you, you're saying no to these
lowpaying clients so that you're able to
maintain your team and the salaries the
way that they are. You're able to
maintain your expenses as they are, both
personal lifestyle as well as business
expenses. And you're actually delaying
what is needed to be a today
choice that you need to be making. And
the reality is is that you should
operate your business like a business.
That taking lowpaying work doesn't allow
for you to live another day. Taking
lowpaying work is actually what is going
to be your demise. It actually creates
for a lot of the problem that I see
happening in a lot of agencies. It
actually is what is creating for those
margins and creating for you to question
yourself and what it is that you have as
a value in the beginning to begin with.
And it's a true problem. But the reality
is even if you have the right mindset
shifts. Most agency owners still hit
that invisible wall. They're stuck
repeating habits that keep them in the
same place. If you've ever wondered why
some agency seems to continuously
explode with growth, it's almost always

## 58. Get Clients FASTER & CHEAPER： Our #1 Lead Gen Strategy for Creative Agencies

So, there are thousands of ways agencies
can get clients, but there is one way
that is much faster and much cheaper
than the others. It is nearly always the
first lead channel we set up with our
agencies. And over the years, it has
generated millions of dollars of revenue
for our clients. So, today I'm going to
explain how it works so you can start
closing deals much faster. By the way,
my name's Robert Patton, and I've been
working with agencies for over 15 years,
helping them build sustainable and
growing businesses. If you run an
agency, I'm assuming you've signed at
least one client via referral. And
during the sales process, you would have
noticed it was faster and it was easier,
and ultimately a lot less price
sensitive. But, the issue was with
referrals is that you only know a
limited number of people, and so that
you can only have a limited number of
clients. There's only so far your
network goes. And so, ultimately it
isn't really scalable. But, over the
years we realized that there is a way to
scale this concept, and this is how we
do it. So, the first step is your ideal
clients work with other people. So, you
want to identify who those individuals
are. Someone that comes across your
ideal client often. Someone that
generally speaking is going to have
authority with them. Someone that is
already going to have a respect and
built-in relationship. What you're going
to then do is you're going to reach out
to them. You're going to get to know
them. What will speed up this entire
process is if you start to introduce
them to people yourself. Get them to
know others. Start sending them
referrals. And if you want more
information on exactly how to attract
the right type of client consistently
for your agency, you can download our
free book in the description, which goes
into detail on the exact system you need
to set up. The final thing is that you
do need to build a relationship with
them. And so, you might be saying, "This
just sounds like networking, and I've
tried to network before, Robert." And I
completely understand. Here's the thing.
It is really important that at the first
stage, when you're getting to know this
person, that you do the introductory
call in a specific way. Cuz there are
people out there that are entirely only
looking out to get clients themselves,
and are not looking for any reciprocity
in a relationship. So, the way that you
want to conduct the call is you're going
to enter in the call, and you're going
to start asking about them. You're not
actually going to say anything about
yourself. You want to wait and see
if the person asks anything about you.
And if they don't, there is zero chance
that they were going to help you. They
have zero interest in you, and they were
just there to sell you to get referrals
themselves. The ideal person that you're
looking for is like, "Hey, have you met
John? Have you met Vanessa? Have you met
Sarah?" That person is the connector,
and that is the ideal person that you
want to have in your network. They're
someone that is going to send you a lot
of business.
So,
for example, if I owned a video
production agency that was working on
creating personal brand videos, I might
reach out to a ghostwriting service that
works specifically with personal brands,
or a consulting firm that works with
CEOs, cuz that's often where personal
brands are being built. Making sure that
ultimately you are getting in front of
the right people that is getting in
front of and are in front of your ideal
client. However, getting clients and
growing your agency fast can actually be

all the work ultimately ends up falling

So, in order to avoid this problem,

million much easier, creating freedom

## 59. Handling Objections as a Creative Agency Owner (Without Sounding Pushy)

So, most creative agency owners fear
sales objections and not because they
can't handle them, because they are
taught these defensive, pressure-based
methods that just don't work. And so,
here's the truth. Objections aren't real
objections. They're actually emotional
frames your prospect is stuck in. And
when you know how to shift that frame
using simple communication psychology
methods, the conversation instantly
becomes calmer, more open, and 10 times
more productive. So today I want to show
you some communication techniques that
make objections easy to navigate so you
can stay calm, confident, and in control
of the conversation the entire time. So
let's start with what objections really
are. They are really just emotional
frames. So the reality of any
conversation is that the prospective
buyer needs to be able to establish a
couple of things. First, they need to
believe that they are capable of doing
the thing. The reality is is that
they're coming to you because they have
a business challenge that they need to
solve. and they likely have tried to do
this either with another agency partner,
they've tried to do this on their own,
they have struggled to do so. And the
reality is is that they are trying to
protect themselves and they are unsure.
They're overwhelmed. They're anxious
about which direction to go. And your
job is to allow for them to feel more
comfortable, to feel more sure of
themselves and in the direction that
they're ultimately making so that they
can make the best possible decision for
themselves. And so your job is to help
shift that frame and not to fight it,
not to push against them. Because the
reality is is that when you try to fight
anyone, they do this, right? They end up
immediately locking up and become
defensive. And if they are in a
defensive place, they are not hearing
you. They are not going to shift their
mindset at all. They're not going to
change their opinion at all. So they
need to be calm. They need to be in a
place that allows for them to feel more
confident. And you need to be in the
same place because the moment that you
change your tonality, the moment that
you become more anxious, it ends up
bringing this anxiety into the
conversation. So you need to be able to
not try to come to this conversation
trying to force them into any direction,
but ultimately attempting to allow for
them to gain the information that they
need in order to make the best possible
decision for themselves. And so it's
vital that you actually stay neutral.
The tone, the pace, your body language,
the way that you communicate creates for
the space. And so here's the concept,
the idea that I want you to take into
it, right? Let's say that you had just
had an argument with your partner and
you show up to a coffee shop and the
person, for better or worse, the person
behind the counter is incredibly
friendly. They are the most bubbly
personality and you just are having a
horribly bad day. And this person is
incredibly bubbly, incredibly excited. I
mean, the birds are chirping and the
butterflies are flying and you know,
everything's amazing to this person. and
they're meeting you with that energy.
And the same is true for your prospects.
You don't know the meeting that they
just came out of. You don't know the
thing that they're currently
experiencing. You don't know whether
they just had a fight with their
partner. And if you were in that coffee
shop and you just had a bad day and this
person is incredibly excited and
friendly, you could either potentially
get pushed a little bit in that
direction. But more often, what ends up
happening is it ends up really grinding
against you and it makes you feel quite
bad and even more aggravated. And so the
reality of it is that you actually need
to meet them where they are. I'm not
saying that you're going to take it to a
negative place if they've been in a bad
place, but at the very least stay
neutral or the tone in which you're
actually meeting them. You have to meet
them in the place that they are. Walk
into the space with them because the way
that you communicate with them is going
to allow for them to feel safe. It's
going to allow for them to feel the
confidence. It's going to allow for them
to be able to meet you and be present in
the conversation with you. And so try to
mirror their energy without mimicking
them. Because if you end up becoming too
obvious in the way that you're actually
mirroring their energy, it ends up
becoming even more kind of standoffish
because why is this person feeling like
a chameleon and not actually genuine? So
be you, but trying to just match their
energy level. So if your energy level
and you're that person in the coffee
shop that is incredibly energetic, just
tone it down just slightly. Don't become
a different person, just try to meet and
mirror the energy. And so let's start
talking about the deframe of the base of
psychology kind of technique that we can
place here. So, I don't want you to
rebuke anything or to fight anything
that they're ultimately saying. I want
you to try to to just start to reframe
things gently. And you need to use this
with permission things like this. So,
like, can I ask you something? And you
you always feel free to say no or you
may not want to answer this question.
So, give them kind of an exit to it. So,
you want to start to be able to allow
yourself to have a different type of
conversation with them, not one where
you are trying to explain why you were
the best thing, why they should be
buying from you, why they should be
doing any of these things. Because the
reality is is that people don't get
convinced of anything, right? They have
conversations. They try to make the best
possible decisions that they can. And if
they feel like you are trying to
convince them, they're going to put up a
wall and that wall is going to get
taller and it's going to get higher and
it's going to become harder for you to
be able to get over that wall or break
it down. And so the thing that you need
to be able to do is allow for them to
persuade themselves and not actually
become any person that's trying to
convince anything. So ask them for
permission to ask them a question or
take them down a different path. And so
you want to start to ask clarifying
questions that allows for you to
understand the thing below or behind the
objection that they've asked because
most often you have the known problem,
the unknown problem, and the hidden
problem. So as an example, the known
problem could be that they are not
generating the number of leads that they
need. The unknown problem is the fact
that their messaging is not actually
resonating. The hidden problem is that
they're also not in front of the right
audience in the first place that can
afford to buy their product. And so the
reality of what it is that you're
needing to do is that you need to be
able to understand each kind of
different layer. And so they have to
first admit to themselves that they have
this problem that needs to be solved and
you need to help them identify it. And
the more that you ask, the more that you
take interest, intrigue that allows for
you to be able to uncover the things
that you need to. So I want you to look
at what is the objection, but what is
the question behind it. So gently
explore what happens if nothing changes
as an example. Let's say that they bring
up a pricing objection, right? So here's
a common thing that a lot of people
bring up is you know hey the price is
too high it's like completely understand
can I ask this question I'm curious as
to what exactly you're comparing the
price to or in comparison to what or can
you tell me a little more simple things
that you're showing the bit of interest
and intrigue to allow for them to
extrapolate and the more that you know
the better that you can actually answer
the question. So ask how it is that
they're going to go about making
decisions ask about why it is that
they're looking to accomplish something
ask what they've tried to do to solve it
on their own. Have they worked with
another agency partner before? What
ended up happening when they worked
together? Have they hired someone
internally? What ended up taking place
when that took place? What happened when
they launched this campaign or that
campaign or take interest up to what's
actually going on and making sure that
you're understanding more behind the
actual question that they're asking
because they're asking it for a certain
reason. There's an anxiety, there's a
nervousness, there's a problem that's
there. And most often, they're not
trying to accuse you of anything. In

at this point. It's a simple, easy one
for you to be able to go to in times of
stress, in times of things that feel
difficult because it is something that
you have done so so long. It's something
that you can just look at the back of
your hand and you recognize it
inherently. And so over time, you have
become maybe a better manager where
you're more managing timelines, budgets,
but you still in moments of stress will
regress to the technician. And don't get
me wrong, there's moments that you do
need to wear that technician hat.
There's moments that you're going to
need to wear that manager hat and some
moments that you're going to need to
wear that CEO hat. But the reality is is
how much time are you wearing each one
of these hats? And as you continue to
grow, there's going to be a a phase of
you as a business owner. That means that
the technician hat has been officially
retired and you're no longer in the
work. That generally speaking is in the
higher seven figure echelon of revenue.
But the thing that I want you to be
thinking about here is when you are
executing work, when you are in the
day-to-day of the business, I want you
to be considering am I wearing
technician? Am I wearing manager? Am I
wearing CEO? Which hat are you wearing?
What things should you be doing? What
are the considerations that you are
making wearing this hat? And I want you
to be consistently thinking through that
as we are having conversations today and
in the future. It's which hat should I
be wearing in this moment? which hat do
I need to be wearing more and which one
am I overdefaulting into because this is
a thing that happens for every single
one of us. It happened for me included
as well. And so I'm not wanting to call
you out in any negative way because this
is just the reality. It is an easy
thing. It is a very human thing to go to
a place of comfort, something that you
know how to do well in a moment of
stress. And so I want you to be able to
shift your gravity upward. I want you to
start to let go of some of the
day-to-day pieces, some of that
technician function. start to use that
technician hat less and less as time
progresses so that you can actually
start to focus more on the longer term.
Focus more on the growth component, the
manager and then the CEO function of
your business allowing for you to be
able to continue to grow. And so here's
the thing that I see a lot of pieces
kind of get blocked here is that I hear
I've heard so many times that this idea
of delegation is it hasn't worked. I've
tried it didn't work. I can't I'm not
don't want to do it again. And the
reality is that there's different types
of delegation. And I want to talk about
that to begin with because the way that
you actually delegate is actually a key
component because the reality is is that
in order for something to be
appropriately delegated to someone, you
have to have them understand what it is
and where they're taking it. What are
the constraints? What are the
requirements? What is it that you're
actually looking to? And I see here that
what happens most times is that we
either did not provide enough
information for them to know exactly how
much authority they had or two that we
then ended up involving ourselves too
much in the process and we're not
actually allowing for the team to take
the next step and more ownership more
authority within it. So level one
delegation I want you to do exactly what
I say in this way step by step do
exactly what I say largely a training
and SOP etc. Right now, let's say that
we now want to move to the next level of
delegation. So, we're going to give them
a thing that they are going to need to
research and I want you to come back to
me with the research that you had. So,
check in with me and then we're going to
go to the next step. The next is going
to be I want you to do the research. I
want you to figure out which is the best
solution and then I want you to come to
me with your one recommendation.
Essentially, let me just make that final
approval to move forward. Now, the next
one is I want you to do the research. I
want you to find the right solution. I
want you to choose the one and then I
want you to just inform me. Make the
choice. I want you to move forward, but
just let me know what direction you're
deciding to go so that I'm informed. And
the final is you are completely removed.
Here's an initiative that I want us to
do. Here's a direction that I want us to
go. So, I want you to find the right
solution. I want you to find the right
plan. I want you to put it together. I
want you to make the decision. And I
trust you. Just do it. And so, here's
the thing is that what I often find is
that most managers, owners are stuck at
level one. And so they are just giving
the task and so they actually haven't
delegated really anything. They're not
clicking the buttons anymore, but it
hasn't actually elevated any of the
thinking. So they're the still the one
that's doing the thinking. And so what
ends up happening in this stage that is
the biggest challenge for most owners
and most managers is that the team then
comes to you with the problem that they
have and then you answer the question.
You don't actually allow for them to
come to the actual conclusion
themselves. So you're not fostering the
ability to be able to think through
these pieces. And it is a huge challenge
for most people. And so in order for you
to be able to start to gain more freedom
is that you're going to have to start to
transfer some decision-m power,
decision-m authority, and not just task
ownership. This is going to require
training. This is going to require
trust. And this is going to require
clear guard rails for them. What are the
constraints? And what do you feel
comfortable with? And clearly, you're
not going to give someone carb blanch
from the first time that you give them a
thing to do, you need to feel
comfortable that they're making the
right types of decisions. And so, in
order to get them into this place,
there's a very key kind of piece that I
have found that is very necessary. And
so I was actually having a conversation,
it's been several years now, with a
friend of mine and he ran into a
situation with his then EA, now ops
manager that she was making a choice and
had made a choice that seemed logical
but didn't consider the risk and he was
quite upset at the decision that she
ended up making because it ended up
creating a whole lot more risk and
unnecessary risk for the business
because she hadn't considered the
potential downside of what happens if
this happens, if this happens, if this
happens. And I told him that it was his
fault because the reality is is that as
a business owner we recognize that we
hold all the risk. So we have actually
trained our minds. We have trained our
process. We have trained us to recognize
the risk inherent in every choice that
we make. But we don't actually have our
team start to think about the risk
scenarios. What is the worst case
scenario? Can that be undone? Can it be
fixed? What happens if that happens?
What are we going to do in that scenario
if it takes place? What is the benefit
of moving forward? What is the risk of
moving forward? So, what is the client
risk? What is the financial risk? What
is the operational risk? What is the
culture risk? What is the team risk?
What is the MI risk? And understanding
what that actually looks like and
actually evaluating those pieces. And
so, are we comfortable with that risk or
are we not? If we are, cool, we can move
forward. If we're not, how do we
mitigate the risk so that we are
comfortable with it? And so what happens
and the reality is is that as a business
owner we see this, we know this, we have
it, we completely understand it because
we own it. And so having a clear
understanding of how do we have the team
understand and calculate and be able to
even understand and identify that risk
in the first place because the reality
is is you are practiced at this. You
have developed this skill and if you
want your team to be able to understand
this, you have to help them cultivate
it. You have to help them develop it.
You have to help them be able to
understand and also understand your own
risk tolerance because guess what? Your
risk tolerance is different than mine.
Your risk tolerance is different than
theirs and they're making decisions on
your behalf on behalf of your business.
And so it's important for them to
understand what type of risk. So if this
risk falls beyond this threshold, I want
you to escalate it to me. I want you to
bring me involved. If it becomes this
point, I want you to collaborate on how
we can mitigate it. If it's in this risk
threshold and however it is that you
feel comfortable, I want you just to
decide and move forward because I'm okay
with that risk. And what does that
actually look like? What does that
actually mean? And when do we actually
have a viewpoint where we're eye to eye
with the risk that that team member has
assessed? Because once we have an
understanding that we are in alignment
with that risk assessment that they have
done the right thinking there in the
first place, we are going to feel a
whole lot more comfortable with their
decision-m criteria that they are going
to be making in order to actually start
to delegate a full outcome. So that
final level of delegation that I was
talking about earlier where they are
going to be given here's the vision,
here's the thing I want to accomplish. I
want you to find the right solution. I
want you to put together a plan. I want
you to make the right choice and then I
want you just to implement. I don't even
want to know just do it. And so we are
not going to feel comfortable with that
until we know that our team is thinking
about things at the level of risk that
we are comfortable with. And if we don't
believe that, guess what? We are going
to want to be involved because then what
happens? Our risk is too high because we
are never going to feel comfortable with
it. And so in order for us to be able to
be comfortable, we have to know that
they are seeing our risk tolerance
clearly, that they are seeing the risk
clearly so that we can ultimately feel
comfortable. And once that happens, once
we see that happen regularly enough, we
will feel comfortable because the most
well-developed component of us as human
beings in general, not just as business
owners, is assessing risk. What is the
risk to us? But as a business owner, we
have a very different type of risk. And
so that skill has been developed in
time. And help your team develop it.
Pour into your team, help them
understand it. And being able to
actually have that be clear. The next
piece that I wanted to talk about is
this solutionoriented mindset. And I
kind of alluded to it a little bit
earlier, but the thing that I find was
really an interesting piece for me is
that like I really, as I have mentioned
over and over and over again, that I had
this kind of transition that I had to
realize and still kind of happens now
from time to time, but this
understanding and idea that I enjoyed
feeling important and I enjoyed feeling
needed. I still do now, but too a little
bit too much, we'll say. So when someone
came to me with a problem, I enjoyed
solving it and I would just give them
the solution and it made me feel good.
But the reality is is that it did a
couple of things. One, it made them
reliant upon me to solve the problems.
Two, it didn't actually have them being
solutionoriented in their own mindset.
And so that we ended up kind of stuck in
this place that I was the decision maker
for all things. And eventually I started
to get really frustrated. Like why am I
the only person thinking? I'm like,
well, I kind of did this to myself. And
if you're in that place, you very well
might have done it to yourself as well.
And so here's the thing I want us to
talk about. There's this concept that
Dan Sullivan created, the 131. And I
really enjoy it because of its
simplicity. And I think that there's
things that you can do to expand upon
it. And I have expanded upon it in my
own practice of it. But the idea is
incredibly simple. It's what is the
actual core problem? And and I want to
hit this one very importantly. So you
have a problem in front of you, but what
is the actual core problem? And what I
find happens so incredibly frequently is
that we're solving for the symptom.
We're not actually solving for the
problem. It's like, hey, um, I've got
blockage in my arteries and it's I'm
going to go then, um, I'm going to take
blood thinners. I'm going to then clean
out my I'm going to rotor my my veins.
It's like, well, why is that there in
the first place? Your cholesterol is
high. You've been eating wrong. All
those sorts of things that need to
happen. So, that's the actual core of
the problem, right? And so the challenge
is that first one is actually really
important and it does take some time in
mentorship to get them to this first
piece. But one is what is the actual
core problem. So in general I see here's
the actual known problem, here's the
unknown problem and then here's the
hidden problem. And that hidden problem
is actually difficult in a lot of
instances to uncover. But I find
personally that finding the actual
problem is 99% of the solution really is
that you have to understand what the
problem is in the first place. And once
you have a clear understanding of what
the actual problem is, then we start to
automatically start to see what
potential solutions are. So once you've
actually identified that problem, you
can start to identify the solution. So
what is that one core problem? Then what
are three potential solutions and what
is your one recommendation? Because the
the idea here is that as the owner, as
the manager, your job is to say yes or
no. That is your job. Your job isn't to
assess all the in between. Your job is
to say yes or no. And once you're
actually fully implementing this
framework with your team, it actually
allows for them to start to be more
autonomous strategic thinking. The way
that I implemented this that I actually
took this into practice with my team
when I first was kind of learning this
concept is that I created a Countly link
and I had them actually do it. I did a
bitly kind of trackable so that I
understood how many people would go to
it in the first place and how frequently
it actually happened. And what I found
was that eight times out of 10 when they
actually started to follow this and
actually start to do this over time,
eight times out of 10 they wouldn't even
end up scheduling a time with me. So in
order for them to schedule time with me,
for me to help them evaluate a problem
or to help them solve to solve a
problem, they would have to go through
solving and answering 1 131. And if they
didn't know the final one, then fine,
they could still come. It wasn't a
required field. But just the thinking
alone, putting this blockade actually
allowed for them to start to think
through. And they actually didn't really
need me. And it became abundantly clear
as they started to go through the known,
the unknown, the hidden problem. And as
they started to to really flex that
muscle, work out that muscle, it became
abundantly clear to them, it became a
whole lot easier. And so the thing that
I want you to be thinking about here and
the final kind of piece that I want you
to distill is the signs that you are the
bottleneck and that you need to be
thinking about some of these solutions
that I'm talking about today is that
you're the only one who knows what's
going on in all departments. You're the
one that actually has the beat of what's
happening in your agency today.
Deadlines slip, things go past due
unless you are the one pushing them.
team members default to you for
decisions that you know that they could
make themselves that they've done this
12 times but they're going to come to
you just to double check and you feel
like you cannot unplug physically,
mentally, emotionally because without
you things would feel like they are
stalling. If that's the case, you are a
bottleneck. Whether you're going to
solve all of those points today or
tomorrow, probably not. But you can
start making traction towards them. And
so part of it is your own reaction, your
own action is causing some of these
pieces. But the way that you engage with
your team is core to solving this
problem. Whether it's delegation,
whether it's risk assessment, or whether
it's actually the solutionoriented
mindset, but these frameworks will help
you develop a path, a structure to allow
for you to be able to trust and start
letting go. creating structure like
SOPs, project management systems,
decision frameworks that allow for them
to move forward, trust that you can
build with your team that allows for you
to feel comfortable that they're going
down the right direction and eventually
being able to start to let go. So, if
you find yourself stuck in the weeds,
consistently answering questions and
unable to focus on the growth of your
agency, frankly, you're not alone. It's
not just a time management issue. It
isn't. It's a system and leadership
challenge. Start by identifying the role
that you should be playing or the role
that you are playing too much. Build
that decision-making framework with your
team, with your leadership team, and
trust your team to rise to the occasion.
One of the key things that I realized at
one point was that my team cannot stand
in a place that I'm currently standing.
And while I'm not going to vacate the
room, I just need to take a step to the
right or to the left to allow for them
to stand in that spot. And so that's how
you're going to start to be able to get
some of your freedom back step by step,
minute by minute, hour by hour, week by
week, and unlock some real true
sustainable growth in your business.

## 67. How Agencies Accidentally Destroy Their Own Margins

So creative agencies stay stuck because
they are consistently sacrificing
something. So they will push for speed
and the quality drops. They will chase
perfect quality and the timelines
ultimately end up exploding or they try
to do everything and the team just ends
up burning out. But healthy, profitable
creative agencies don't rely on hustle
or heroic effort. They rely on a simple
framework that keeps quality high,
projects moving, and the team energized
consistently. So in this video, I'm
going to show you the structure that
allows creative agencies to stay wildly
profitable and healthy. And once you
understand how to balance these forces,
your operations become smoother, your
margins end up going up, and your team
becomes far easier for you to be able to
manage. Let's jump into it. So, let's
talk about the creative agency's sort of
triangle, if you will. You've got
quality, speed, and team wellbeing. And
the way that we think about each one of
these, and I'm going to jump into it.
So, quality is the the standard of work
that we are ultimately delivering. Speed
being how fast can we ultimately deliver
it. And then team's well-being is the
energy, the capacity, and the morale
across the team. And so, here's the
thing that you want to think about,
right? Quality is paramount. Being able
to deliver on time is important because
every single week that we go over
timeline, the typical percentage of
margin erosion is 6% per week. But if we
don't have a happy team, then we don't
have great service delivery people,
right? That the actual person that we
are the thing that we are selling our
team's talent, time, and attention, we
don't have a good work product anymore.
So most agencies over prioritize one and
unknowingly are damaging the other. So
if we're prioritizing quality, then
speed takes too long. And if we are
prioritizing speed, then quality suffers
and the team well-being suffers. And if
we look at both quality and speed, of
course, the team is going to suffer. So
how do we actually allow for us to
balance each one of them is vital. So
let's talk about what happens when one
side of this triangle breaks. So if
quality breaks down, you end up with
unhappy clients or you end up with scope
creek because you're needing to try to
make up for the things and it ends up
just causing a lot of rework when you
end up having quality issues. If we end
up with speed issues, we end up with a
clogged pipeline of work that we
currently have, late projects, lost
referrals because we didn't deliver on
time, and with lower profit margins. So,
the reality is is that every single week
that you're going overdue on a project,
the typical amount of margin erosion is
6%. So, every single week you're losing
6% of profitability. And when you think
about the most agencies, you're anywhere
between, you know, 12 being average,
that 2 weeks can mean that you go from
making money to not making any. And if
you are more profitable than that, it's
still again just within 6 weeks of
something going past due, which is a
normal amount that I do see that
happening, you end up being
unprofitable. So this is a very
important component. And then the
well-being of the team. So if the team
is not being paid attention to, you end
up with inconsistent delivery. You end
up with turnover on your team, which is
a lot of headache. You end up with
burnout across your team, and you end up
with bad service providers, which ends
up with not a great client experience,
and what ends up in client turnover as
well. So understand that this triangle
needs to have an equilibrium, a balance
between the three, allowing for you to
be able to be in a better place. So
let's talk about the reasons that a lot
of agencies become unprofitable. So they
are overservicing their clients because
they have unclear expectations at the
beginning of the project of what exactly
where do we begin and end, what are we
doing, what's included, what's excluded.
And that poor project scoping ends up
and not having a meaningful delivery
rhythm which ends up creating quality
problems, speed problems, and morale
problems. And the founder ends up kind
of just centered around this operational
component. They are either too involved
or not involved enough of what's
actually going on. And when each one of
these pieces break, it is going to have
a profitability problem. And so quality
ends up with not having great client
experiences, which ends up not getting
referrals, which ends up with a
profitability problem. We end up with
speed issues which then results in
projects not being delivered on time
which means that we then can't bill
which ends up with a profitability
problem. We end up with a team member
turning over. We have to spend more time
on training so that reduces our
resources availability which ends up
having a profitability problem. And so
realizing how each one of these has an
immediate impact to your net profit line
is vital. So let's talk about the
healthy agency kind of framework and how
does this actually change and turn
around. So clear scoping allows for us
to protect quality and time because if
we have a clear understanding of where
we begin and end exactly what we are
delivering we are able to deliver upon
the things that we have outlined in the
scope better and to the quality standard
that we have ultimately ideally on time
as well. If we are able to have
predictable delivery so how do we
actually go about the systems the
step-by-step process the rhythm that
allows for us to deliver on time. So
who's meeting when, what exactly are we
doing, what are the checkpoints that are
required, how is this information being
communicated between team members
internally as well as with the client.
Then role clarity. So if you're not
familiar with REI, go check it out. It's
an incredibly useful tool, but having
who's responsible, who's accountable,
who's consulted, and who's informed. So
each person on the team is one of those
different pieces of different components
of a project. and the people that take
ownership, full accountability over the
deliverable of that specific component
of the project or of that deliverable or
whatever internal process you're talking
about, it allows for that team member to
own it and actually take it through to
fruition. And if they know that they are
accountable to so most people want to
meet the thing that they are responsible
and accountable to. So allow yourself to
have that clarity on your team so that
they can take that accountability. And
then resource planning so that you know
exactly who's working out what and when
and it avoids burnout because burnout
for your team members if they're working
in an inordinate amount of time
consistently then it is going to cause a
problem and they are going to leave
which creates a plethora of other issues
as we've discussed. So understanding
where your team's attention is going at
any given moment is really useful. It
allows for you to understand too ahead
of time if you're bringing on more work.
Are we at an overflow place? Do we have
capacity on the team? It allows for you
to make more meaningful decisions. This
structure is what creates a sustainable
creative agency. Allowing for you to be
able to have each one of these three
things in balance is paramount and
incredibly useful. So let's look at now
how do we go about establishing a
delivery rhythm that allows for things
to be more meaningfully put in place. So
couple of little suggestions. So you
start the week understanding where
things are going. So this is true for my
team as well and we are massive time
blockers of here's what it is that we're
going to be doing from this time to this
time on Monday this time to this time on
Tuesday and being very clear of what
things are going to fit into what given
day so that we are able to communicate
internally of hey I have got more work
on Monday can I get this done on Tuesday
so that we are able to meaningfully
communicate with each other then a
midweek check-in on what things are
going how things are moving along
creative reviews that sort of thing and
then on Fridays being sure that you're
taking a look at a postmortem on what
happened this week any things that are
going to an issue and then also looking
at the following week of what's coming
down the pike. The further out you can
actually get the better things are going
to go and then having meaningful ways to
unblock people. So the first version of
it for us was having daily meetings. I'm
not a fan of those anymore cuz I didn't
find them to be particularly productive.
But if you're not in a place where you
have a meaningful rhythm and
predictability, then it creates for a
bit of an issue. So you can allow for
yourself to be able to take from these
standups and allow for each person to
communicate meaningfully and unblock
them as time progresses. You can use
project management as a means to do so.
You can use voice notes in your instant
message channels. However you want to go
about that internally for communication,
but making sure that the team has a
means by which to get unblocked. Not
saying that they need to just not plan
anything ahead of time. They should be
planning things ahead of time. I got to
a point where I do not accept live
conversations, live questions for the
things that they have in that given day
that they have to be thinking ahead of
time. But realizing that rhythm equals
predictability, predictability equals
profitability. And knowing that those
pieces have a direct equation to each
other is paramount. Now let's talk about
some early warnings and signs that the
agency is becoming unhealthy and
becoming an issue and that you need to
watch for these things. Emotional
exhaustion on the team. If the team is
consistently not coming to work with,
you know, compassion, with passion, with
excitement, there's issues that are
going on and potentially it may even
just be one person that is causing and
will rot the rest of the bunch. slow
approvals of things that going on
internally. So if something is supposed
to happen on Thursday but it doesn't end
up happening to a week or two later that
is showing that there's a massive
bottleneck most often surrounded around
the founder when the agency is in the
constant emergency mode. If you are
constantly feeling like everything is on
fire and that there's no time and
attention for you to get the work done
that you have your list gets longer

## 72. How Creative Agency Owners Accidentally Build Their Own Burnout

But here's my truth and my belief
system. Burnout isn't caused by the size
of your ambition or even the workload
that you have. It's caused by how you
are spending your personal energy,
effort, and focus. And in many ways,
we're building our own burnout with the
choices that we are making every single
day.
So for most agency owners, burnout can
feel like the death of a dream or the
end of the road. And it's not something
that we celebrate or we see as a sign of
effort. It's something that we actually
fear, something that we try to avoid,
ignore, or even outrun. But here's my
truth and my belief system. Burnout
isn't caused by the size of your
ambition or even the workload that you
have. It's caused by how you are
spending your personal energy, effort,
and focus. And in many ways, we're
building our own burnout with the
choices that we are making every single
day. So, in this episode, I wanted to
talk to you about what the real problem
is in burnout and how it actually is
happening and that it's actually an
energy drain. And when you're spending
your time in the wrong places, even work
that you might love, it can become a
true weight. But when you realign your
focus on things that energize you, that
fill your cup, the business can become
lighter. It can be more profitable. It
can become more sustainable. And so can
your energy within the business and
ultimately a real meaningful way for you
to be able to avoid burnout. So let's
talk about how we can get back to that
the initial dream, the passion, the
excitement in your agency, the thing
that and the reason why you ultimately
started the business in the first place.
So, here's my belief system and I've had
in a bit of an evolving viewpoint in
this and having reached at one at points
in my own career where I felt like I was
edging towards burnout. And I am, as
you've probably heard me say, a
recovering workaholic. And the fact is
is that I can overinvest myself into the
business. And initially this idea of
burnout to me and here's how I
understood it is that burnout was just
too much focus on the business and not
having enough quote unquote work-like
balance which I have my own qualms with
that idea in the first place is that I
mean to me I think that there's never
going to be a full balance in anything

balanced with personal life if I'm on

that it's more about the mix and the

into your business and making sure that
you are properly paying attention to and

So examples of these things could be,

conversation. Without this, you have no
potential sale whatsoever. So, even if
you have the best product and the best
service and sliced bread, you're never
going to get them across the finish line
unless they actually believe that what
they're looking to accomplish is even
possible. Let's say that you have a
skydiving business and the person that
you're talking to and trying to get them
to go up in a plane with you believes
that they're destined to die today.
There's zero chance that you'll ever get

today. And that belief system is the

it's possible for them to accomplish the
goal. A successful sales system should

get them motivated about the next stage.
You need to have them first start to
believe that what they are wanting is
even possible. And doing it in a way
that allows for you to make sure that
who you're talking to even has
the remote possibility of working with
you. Do they have the money? Really,
right? If your service costs $100,000,
talking to a local barber shop probably
not going to be the right fit and they
have to have the revenue to do so. And
remember that the only thing that you're
looking to do here is you're not selling
yourself yet. You're just selling the
next call.
Now your prospect is motivated for the
call and seeing the possibility. What do
we do next? Define the need. Define the
gap and create safety. Don't sell. Once
you have defined the gap, you're able to
allow for the person to articulate to

they already know. They're in the room

Otherwise, they would have never reached

to want to solve this problem and go

Often creative agencies think growth
requires more people, more designers,
more strategists, more account managers.
But here's the truth. More people
doesn't actually mean more output. But
it often means more complexity. So many
top agencies operate very differently.
They grow by increasing talent density,
hiring fewer but higher performing
people, removing drag, and building
systems that let their best people do
their best work. So in this video, I'll
show you how elite agencies build high
performing teams without hiring more
people, and how you can replicate the
exact same structure inside your agency.

about the kind of problem bigger teams
equal better teams. Not necessarily

there's several distinctions and other

throughout this video that may or may
not apply to your specific model. So,
we'll explain the distinction between
them, but here's what people often
create. So, more communication lines is
what happens when you have a bigger
team. They have more meetings. There's
more handoff. Often, more confusion,
more management, and frankly, just more
operational drive. In order for you to
have a successful larger team, it
necessitates greater operational
infrastructure. More systems, more SOPs,
more communication frameworks, more
clarity of who communicates when and

some of the communication frameworks
that are happening. But small lead teams

Less communication makes things easier.
So less process is needed in order to
make things function well. So it allows

to build that baseline infrastructure
that's necessary for those larger teams.

can start using it today. So my
automation journey actually started in a

Then, not only were we able to automate
all of these pieces that made it

a reminder to the contractor 2 days

on from a specialization perspective
there's like this big fear component to
it where I didn't have it because I felt
so like aligned with it and I'm curious
whether you ended up having that fear in
narrowing so aggressively or did it
actually just feel like I'm in the right
spot and let's push forward?
>> I think there's always that fear. Even
to this day, I would say when
opportunities come and we see a shiny
object, we're entrepreneurs. We're
always like, "Oh, but I could just I
could take them on and help them and
figure it out and we could make so much
money and da da da." You know, there's
always that opportunity that comes and
you have to say no. But on the flip
side, when you just align yourself, and
this this is kind of how I felt. I was
so aligned and I saw such a blue ocean
for my specialization that I knew saying
no to people was just pushing me in the
right direction. And until you can get
to that point, I think it's always going
to feel easier to to say yes to
everything until you can get to this
yeah, blue ocean feeling of like, "The
world's my oyster over here. I can't say
yes to this other opportunity cuz it's
just going to bring me further away from
my ocean."
>> Yeah, that makes perfect sense. With
that kind of in mind, do you in the
agencies that you work with and the
reality of the world in that they are
living in, do you see that many of them

has it actually been that, you know, all
of us talking about the riches in the

>> Majority are not focused enough. And
they'll all say that. Every agency will
say, "Here's my niche. Here's Here's
exactly who I want to work with and
soon I hope to only work with those type
of clients, you know?" And
And it's like a later thing. It's like,
"But I have these like 10 clients that
I've worked with since day one and like
I love working with them and but all of
our marketing, you know, we're really
working towards working over here." And
I'd say majority of agency owners don't

it's tricky, you know? Agencies kind of
evolve and they all have that goal of

>> I suppose I was a lot crazier in the
fact that I just went in fired some like
85% of my revenue overnight and my

>> See, you're bold. You're a lot more more
bold than most agencies we work with.
Yeah.
>> Yeah, I'm a jump into the deep end head
first kind of person in general and
don't know if it works out.
>> Mhm.
>> Or it could have not. So, fortunately it
did.
>>
>> Um I'm curious on the like the premium
specialized productized component
because the the reality when I think
that a lot of people looking at account
management, right? They they see it as
like potentially can see it as like a
support role, you know? And Dalton Co.
provides this elevated premium work from
an account management perspective. And
so, how did you go around changing the
perception and having that conversation
that this is a premium product that is
going to provide this outcome?
>> Yeah, in our industry account managers,
most of the time people just look at it
as a butt in a seat. They're like, I
just need to hire an account manager, so
then I never need to talk to a client
ever again. And that's so far from the
truth
as you know. You know, if you don't hire
a an amazing account manager, you're
going to be talking to them more and
it's just going to cost you.
>> Or you have no clients.
>> Yeah, ex-
yeah, exactly. And early on there were
so many people that said to me, that's
not going to work. And I only want my
media buyers or my creatives talking to
my clients because I don't want to
create this he said, she said telephone
game. And to me, I never looked at it
that way because I was a marketer first.
I could speak about the marketing. I was
like, get these media buyers in their
zone of genius which is not talking to
clients. Like, do not let them on the
call and do not let the creatives on the
call either because they're going to get
offended and it's just not a good scene.
So, I knew right from the beginning that
this was not just like an administrative
role because I was able to go in and
actually create real revenue. I was able
to retain clients a lot longer. I was
able to upsell clients a lot quicker. I
was able to just create efficiencies
that created more money for the agency.
So, I knew that if I could do it, it was
doable. So, when I went out to create
this productized service, I knew I
needed to find the best of the best
people out there to be the account
managers. They could not be junior
people. They needed to be marketers.
They had to be top-notch excellent
account managers, or else this would not
work. And so, early on, I knew that we
were creating its own category, versus
just being an account manager. That's
why we call ourselves client account
managers, because it's a whole different
category. It's not just an account
manager. That could be anybody. We are
client account managers.
>> Well, I mean, the account manager
position, I'm
honestly a a little surprised that
people think about account management as
being something that is just a butt in a
seat, because it is so core to the
future success of the agency. And the
account manager, in reality, is the one
that holds the relationships with the
clients, right? And if you don't have
trust there, you don't have clients. You
don't have new projects. You don't have
growth within the accounts. Like, it's
just so incredibly vital. With that in
mind, you're describing a specific type
of person. And your model, I know for
you, you were able to get out of the
day-to-day, and and eventually be able
to sell Dot & Co., and which had to
decouple the business from you in order
to allow for that to happen, which
requires a reasonable amount of scale.
Um, the thing I'm curious about is that
hiring versus that personality component
of it. So, like, how did that, like,
melting pot of these considerations end
up kind of coming together to create
this system that was able to grow?
>> It's It's harder than I thought it would
be, to be honest.
I always knew I wanted to sell the
business. Right from the beginning, I
knew at some point in my career, I
wanted that on my I don't want to say
resume, but
>>
>> The things that you accomplished.
>> Yeah, my accomplishments list that I
wanted to sell my business. I wanted to
create something that was worth
something that somebody wanted to buy.
And right from the beginning, as I
learned about how to build a sellable
business, the biggest thing everyone
says is make sure you are removed from
the day-to-day seat. So that when
someone comes in to buy it, you're not a
key man risk. And so I knew that to be
true for my business. And so I knew I
needed to create systems and processes
that were scalable without me. And when
you're trying to replicate yourself, I
know that sounds kind of selfish, but
like I wanted what I was able to do for
agencies, I wanted to to basically
duplicate what I was doing. So I knew I
needed to build a team who was very
similar to me. And in a lot of
businesses, a successful business,
people are not the same as you. They're
very, very different. But in my model,
everyone had to be very similar. So that
was kind of tricky as we were growing a
business that was sellable, like Dot &
Co. to be sellable, but also how do we
grow a team of people who are very
similar? So we kind of broke it out, you
know, there was the account manager that
we were hiring for, but then the
operations team was very unique. But I
wanted to promote from within. So it was
Yeah, it was it was this juggling act of
hiring these different roles and growing
and scaling and figuring it out as we
went. What ended up working really well
for us was the account manager model of
hiring was very productized. So we were
looking for a very specific type of
person, personality, experience,
location, time zones. The whole package
had to be very cutthroat. And then, when
it came to promoting people into
operations roles, that was looking for
very specific skill sets as well. So
within the pot of account managers, we
needed leaders who could then lead the
account managers. So I'm looking for
very different skill sets and experience
and um putting people into positions
that made sense. You know, my sales
leader is very different than my
director of operations and my, you know,
managers or the admin team. So, yeah, it
was very tricky to figure out who was
the right person for the each seat as we
were going.
>> I think that's really interesting cuz
effectively what you were trying to do
in one specific component which I
imagine is a lot of your team or the

are doing the actual work, and then you
have the edge cases of different roles
from an operations perspective. But that
you were looking to specific persona and
then kind of clone them over and over

and I think quite an astute observation
of what it is that you did, but also
doing one and hiring for someone that
kind of mimic or mirrored you as an
individual of like what your experience
was because it worked and why not just

replicate it. I'm curious how that
translated into the process component of
like how you went through documentation
in the business and that kind of a
two-pronged question of how did you go
about the assimilation of so you've got
the personality type, they have the
experience, but everyone comes with like
different pieces of how they went about
the systems by which they operate and
the things that they ultimately ended up
doing. So that it was systematic of the
experience across every single client,
your client's clients. And how quickly
did you document and then context of
listeners, do people often wait too long
to do it? How did that kind of play out
in your timeline?
>> Yeah, that was a big thing for us early
days because we were fully remote from
day one. So, I knew in order to hire
people and train them, I could not be
sitting next to them every day. So, I
had to build the training online as
quick as possible. So, pretty much from
probably my third hire, we started to
build out our what we call CAM school,
client account manager school, for that
reason. It was really just to train our
own team. I wanted to take what's
working, what's making us unique and
special and build that into a
essentially onboarding for our team
members. And that's of course evolved
over the years, but that's kind of where
it started was really, really early
days. And I think if you're an agency
owner listening, I think you should
start this sooner than you than you
think. Because you need to basically
know that this whoever you hire today is
going to leave at some point. Have that
mindset, you know, it's like how can I
document how I train this person so that
when they leave, I can get someone else
in trained up as quickly as possible. So
for us, that was kind of right from the
get-go, built our school so that we
could hire really quickly. We identified
that the bottleneck we're going to have
is hiring. You know, we didn't have a
problem with getting leads or clients or
a waitlist. We had to hire amazing
people quickly. So I spent the majority
of my time focused on hiring the right
people, and then they had the training
ready to go, so I didn't have to spend
time training them.
>> Frankly, it sounds like you went about
the system to do so very deliberately.
So I have another two-pronged question.
You mentioned that from a very early on
in the creation of Dot & Co, you had the

pathway to an exit? And then two, at

things that we choose to do are almost
as important, or maybe even some in some

things that we choose to deliberately
not do. And so what throughout that

like the key moments you're like you
know when you saw this like pathway,
like I could go this direction, I could
go this direction, that you specifically

path?
>> I think looking back, it was really
early. And I think exit to me at the

the day-to-day, because I knew I'm an
entrepreneur, I get bored really
quickly, and I knew there'd be a point
where I wanted to do something else. So
I was kind of building this Actually,
you know what? Thinking back, I read the
book 4-Hour Workweek, which I don't know
if that's a good idea or not.
>> [laughter and gasps]
>> But, what what he talks a lot about is
building a business that you can take a
take months off from. Like, close the
door and leave, and it still works
without you. That was kind of the the
goal for me. And so, right from the
beginning, I I looked at the business
and said, "Okay, I can do this myself. I
can have two to three clients. I can
work remote. I can live my best life and
make decent money, and I could this
could be it. Or, I could build it into a
team, a scale a
sellable asset, and a productized
service that runs without me." And so,
right from inception, I'd say I was
building it to exit, whether that meant
a full exit or just day-to-day.
And some of the things that I looked at
was, yes, I read things like the 4-Hour
Workweek, or I listened to tons of
podcasts on how to sell your business.
And the biggest nuggets that they all
said were, "This business needs to run
without you, and you're going to be the
biggest bottleneck." So, right from the
beginning, I was kind of building this
with that in mind. I, you know, one of
my first hires was someone to manage the
team. Really early on, I was thinking
about removing myself from the actual
fulfillment. I was thinking about, "How
do I remove myself from marketing?" You
know, the business is not the Taylor
show. It's Dot & Co. It's not my face,

leadership team in place so that I'm not

"I don't want to work all the time. I
don't want to be a 9-to-5 girl." I knew
I wanted to have kids in the near
future. I didn't want to be tied to my

I would say early on, I was really
looking at this as a system versus the
Taylor show.
>> A follow-up question to that, I'm

that you did early on was hire someone

So for me, I knew just based on my

Like that was something that I said, I'm

You can be my first hire. I'm scared,

I felt like I had no business being a

Because I felt like I just couldn't do

I think I would be like, "You know, I

>> Interesting. That's really interesting.
Um,
there's another kind of two-pronged

I'm curious on whether there was any

he was passionate about was like, "Well,

part of the business was my identity and
as much as I tried to get out of it and

day-to-day client-facing seat, it was

it was just a business in my portfolio.
And
uh yeah, so it was just always my goal
to sell. And anytime I thought about

was like, "That'll be so fun." Like that
was just the vision since day one. So, I
knew I had I just had to follow through
on that and he respected that. So, fast
forward to having these conversations
about the acquisition, you know, I had a

like, "Sure, let's talk about selling

that's what it was. You know, I thought
being removed from every aspect was
going to That's the only way I could

>> Cuz it was a strategic acquisition.
>> It was a strategic acquisition and I

we had X amount of contracts for this

>> What I was more meaning was if it were

>> Mhm.
>> Than it having been a strategic, yeah.

the value of what we bring.
>> I'm also curious on how much the
