## 93. How to Build the Perfect Creative Website

here's the things that I see on loads of
agency websites and the common mistake
that I find we are the best agency at X
that's something that you believe in
that you have not established any
authority to be able to say or you have
some creative quote some creative
thought bubble that you have posted that
eh cool does nothing for me you have
this absolutely long horrendous list of
things that you offer or just this hodg
podge of loads of different creative
imagery that absolutely does not have me
want to stay on your site whatsoever
after working with over a 100 agencies
we have found on four key elements that
are needed in every single agency
website in order for you to be able to
make more sales let's start where your
ideal client starts on your homepage the
issue that I see for the vast majority
of creative agencies you guys are a
little bit too creative you have loads
of fun graphical elements it's
phenomenally designed but it fails at
one really core thing we have no idea
what you do if your ideal client someone
that does not work in the creative
agency's Place cannot understand what
you're doing they are not going to build
any trust with you so I want to walk you
through a couple of core elements that
you need to have on your site that is
really really pivotal in order for you
to be able to build any trust with them
and have them stay on your side at all
so the first piece is your hero should
convey one thing they are in the right
place and you can help them the next
piece is how are you going to capture
their contact information there needs to
be something that you're offering them
to download a piece of information
subscribe to a newsletter or something
that allows for you to capture their
contact information the next piece is
you need to build credit ability whether
that's award clients that you have
worked with to testimonials that you
have been able to get over the years
there's some component of credibility of
trust that needs to be built and then
you need to explain largely kind of
really high level 30,000 ft view of what
it is ultimately that you do how is it
exactly that you work with your clients
and what problem is it that you were
solving with your ideal client the next
component of it is your service or your
process page as I mentioned already
earlier in this video one of the
predominant problems that I see here is

## 95. How to Pick a Profitable Niche as a Creative Agency in 4 Steps (and Scale to $1M+)

If you want to make more money as a
creative, you should pick a niche. But
how do you know which niche to pick? And
how do you know it will be profitable?
Well, over the past 15 years, I've
worked out a four-step system that helps
you make this decision based on facts
instead of relying on your gut feeling.
So, let's find out what these four steps
are and how you can use them to grow
your business. But before we get into
that, let me quickly explain the
benefits of picking a niche. So, one of
the primary components that a niche
provides for is a better, more targeted
marketing campaign. You're able to know
exactly where your ideal client is. What
are they consuming? Where are they at?
What kind of decisions do they make?
What exactly do they need? And
ultimately, what are they thinking? What
are their challenges? What are you there
to solve? So, you're able to have more
refined, specified language messaging
for them that makes for your conversion
so much better. It makes marketing
incredibly easier and it makes every
single one of your marketing dollars go
so much further because the more general
of an audience that you have, the harder
it is to market. And you know this, the
other item here is that it actually
allows for incredibly easier process. So
for the vast majority of agency owners,
you end up in a pro in a state that you
are the predominant delivery person
because you're the only one that has the
breath of knowledge and the years of
experience that allows for you to be
able to apply it and even then makes it
hard. So replacing yourself either is
incredibly expensive, incredibly
difficult or just impossible. And so
you're not able to actually create a
system or process because it's all up
here. It's only there and it's so
nuanced. you have 25 different clients
and 24 different exceptions which you
cannot create a system for that. When
you have a niche, you have the same
challenges, the same knowledge, the same
process. You're able to create a method
that allows for repeatable results for
your clients. This creates simplicity in
the way that your agency functions. It
makes it so much easier because you're
able to hire for that expertise. You're
able to train for that expertise. You're
able to implement the exact processes
and systems that allow for scale. The
more complex of a business that you
have, the more likely it is to fail. The
more simple that you have makes it more
scalable. Many of you are looking for
how do I scale this business? How do I
remove myself? Well, here we are at the
answer. So, now we understand why you
want to pick a niche, but how do we
actually pick one that is going to be
profitable and scalable? Well, there's
four basic steps that you're going to
want to take. The first of which you
really have already done. And all I want
you to do is grab a white sheet of paper
and a pen. And I want you to write down
the industries that you've worked with.
Go through your client list and just
write down what industry they were in.
Then I want you to layer on on top of
that industries that you're passionate
about, that you have a lot of knowledge
about from your personal life or a prior
career, one that you have lots of
experience in. From there, what I want
you to do is process of elimination. And
I want you to use three filter criteria
to actually eliminate through this list.
And so I want you to ask these three
questions. And if they do not fit one of
the three, you are going to market and
get rid of it. The first is, do you like
the people in that industry? Are you
inspired by that industry and the people
in it? And your expertise, can it help
them? Does it help solve their
challenges? If the industry does not
meet all three, it is eliminated. If you
end up with more than two or three
industries at this point, you are not
being honest with yourself and you need
to do a little bit more self-evaluation
and be honest with whether you really
truly like them, are inspired by them,
and can help them. I'd be surprised if
anyone is able to meet those things with
more than three industries. The final
piece, as I mentioned at the beginning,
we want to confirm with data. So,
there's three pieces of criteria that we
want to evaluate here. Can they afford
you? Is there enough of them? Is the
industry growing? So, let's start at the
first one. Can they afford you? Let's
look at the average revenue of the
businesses within that industry. You
need to take a look at your services.
So, if your services are at $250,000 a
year with the average size of revenue
for that business, does it make sense
for them to invest with you? Yes or no?
The next piece is, is there enough of
them? So before you actually do the
calculation here, I want you to look at
what is your growth aspiration. Do you
want to be at 1 million, 10 million, 100
million? What is the size of the
business that you're looking to grow?
And then take your average revenue per
client per year. So let's say that my
agency operates at $10,000 a month and
my goal is to get to a million. All I
need to do is sell nine clients and I
have a million dollar book of business,
$90,000 a month. And so then I would
take that nine clients and divide it by
0.01 or 1% of the market. That gives me
the size of the market that I need to be
working with. If you end up with a in a
place where the industry that you are
targeting is more than 10x that number,
you have gone way too wide. And in that
example, you have 90 versus the 9. You
again take the 90 divided by 0.01 which
gives you exactly the number of
businesses that you need to target
within that industry. And the final
piece is is that industry growing
otherwise known as in accounting speak
compound annual growth rate. And so you
need to find out what that keer is for
that specific industry. So quick Google
search, type in industry C A G R and
it's going to give you a couple of
articles around what the compound annual
growth rate for that industry or what
the annual amount of growth that that
industry has. If the industry is not

it's actually shrinking because the e
the economy global economy is growing
faster than that. And if it's not
actually growing faster than the average
amount of growth, it is actually
shrinking. So you need to make sure that
the industry that you're serving is
going to continue to grow. So let's
avoid the likes of like newspaper
publications. So now you know how to

## 96. How to Raise Your Creative Agency Prices Without Losing Clients

Most agencies need to raise their
prices, but they don't because they are
embarrassed or they feel like they're
not worth that price, which
unfortunately means less profit and more
stress for the agency owner. So, to help
with this agency pricing pandemic, I am
sharing a system that you can all use in
your agency. And this system will help
you raise your rates based on data
instead of gut feel so you can feel
really comfortable and really confident
when you do it, which funny enough is
going to help you sell more as well. I
want to preface this with that you're
not going to 3X your agency overnight.
It's going to take work. It's going to
take learnings. It takes trial and
error. And the first step you need to
take is actually a step back because you
need to understand what you are
currently doing. By the way, if you
don't know who I am, my name is Robert
Patton and I've worked with agencies for
over 15 years, helping them build
sustainable and profitable agencies. It
is ultimately impossible to know how
things are moving along unless you're
tracking your progress. So in an example
and it's written in the book Atomic
Habits by James Clear. What he depicts
in the book is imagine yourself in a
room that is below freezing and there's
ice sitting on the table and the
objective that you have is to run around
the room as quickly as you possibly can
and utilize friction to warm up the room
so that you can melt the ice in the
middle. So you're running around
vigorously doing it for hours and
ultimately you don't actually know
because there's no temperature gauge in
the room to see exactly how much
progress you've made. and you've taken
it from 29 degrees to 32 degrees, you're
nearly at the point that you're going to
start to be able to melt the ice, but
you haven't actually held on to being
able to move forward and you don't know
the progress that you've made. So, if
you're looking to grow revenue and
profit this year, you need to be able to
track a few things and not ultimately
wait until December and to just realize
at that point that you've missed your
goal and oh well, what is measured is
ultimately improved. So you need to be
measuring your activity, understanding
what you're doing, know exactly what you
have to do today so that tomorrow can be
better. And if you're not measuring it,
you're leaving it to chance. So if
you're anything like me, and like the
vast majority of agency owners that I've
spoken with, we all have a feeling of a
limiting belief of imposttor syndrome.
And this is especially true when it
comes to fees. We don't feel comfortable
until we are actually able to sell it at
that price point. But once we're able to
sell it at that price point, we're able
to move forward. And I've had an
incredible amount of conversations with
loads of different agencies about this
specific topic. And in one example, I
want to talk you through the specific
salesperson within an agency. And when I
was depicting exactly the price point
that we needed to be selling at, she
absolutely said, "There's no way that
we're going to be able to do that. I'm
already getting pricing objections as it
is." And to begin with, she did struggle
a little bit predominantly because she
was not comfortable saying the price
point. And so when she set the price
point, she kind of set it in a reserved
fashion, unsure about it actually being
able to work. Eventually, after a couple
of sales calls, she sold her first one
and she felt affirmed in the direction
that we were headed and that it was
actually indeed possible and that people
believed that we were worth that price.
So what I want us to be able to do is
find out what the market is willing to
pay and exactly how we are positioned,
recognizing that there's a direct
correlation around bringing risk down
and price point going up. The higher
amount of confidence that a prospective
client has allows for the price point to
increase as well. And if you want to
make sure that your own agency is as
profitable as possible, I'd recommend
downloading our free agency analyzer in
the description below. The next thing I
want you to think about is my 30 to 70%
rule. So, what ultimately goes is that
cold leads, generally speaking, are
going to be between 20 to 30%. Once
you're above a 30% conversion rate in a
cold lead, your price point is too low.
That ultimately you're having too much
success. Now, with you having referrals,
you're in the 70 to 80%. If you're above
that line as well, you have the same
problem. Your price point is too low and
you are the cheap offer. And so, here's
what I want you to recognize, what it is
that you're converting right now. And
look at both your cold leads and your
referral leads or warm leads and
understand that each one of them need to
be measured differently. If you are
currently converting at over 70 to 80%
in your referrals, you need to raise
your rates. And if you are converting
more than 30% in cold, you need to raise
your rates again. And you'll
consistently do this over time and be in
a much profitable place doing less work
for more money. So that said, raising
your prices is fine, but we're business
owners and what really matters to us is
profit. So check out this video next to
make sure that your agency is as
profitable as it possibly can

## 98. How to Take Back Control of Difficult Creative Agency Clients

So creative agencies don't struggle with
intentionally difficult clients. They
struggle because the agency has lost
control of the relationship. So things
like too many stakeholders are chiming
in, feedback becoming messy, unclear,
even contradictory, approvals dragging
on for weeks, the client changing
directions mid-p project, and suddenly
your team is reacting to the client
rather than leading them. But here's the
truth. Clients only become difficult
because the agency stopped leading them.
So, in this video, I'll show you how to
take back control even with the most
complex or high maintenance clients
using simple boundaries, approval
systems, and leadership principles that
make your projects run smoother and your
team far less stressed. Let's go ahead
and get into it. So, why do clients
become difficult? So, it's not a
personality thing. It's actually more
about structure. So, client becomes
difficult when they don't have clarity.
They don't have leadership. They don't
have boundaries. They don't have a
process. They don't have a single source
of truth. and they don't have a clarity
as to who the decision maker is. So
without structure, they fill the gaps.
So here's the thing is that when someone
feels out of control, it ends up
creating for a micromanagy oriented
scenario. So you have lots of different
personality types. Obviously some people
are more prone to micromanagement than
others. But ultimately it's an anxiety
response. It's a feeling out of being
out of control. So they try to control
so that it gets back into a place where
they feel comfortable, where they feel
safe. And the reality is is that your
clients have no business micromanaging
something that they ultimately end up
knowing nothing about. And so their
reality of what this ends up doing is
this creates chaos because they are
trying to control something that they
don't actually know how to control. So
if you're able to mitigate the anxiety,
mitigate them being scared or
frustrated. The feeling of being out of
control, making them feel more safe, the
less that they are going to be that
micromanagy oriented client, becoming
less high maintenance, becoming less of
a problem. I'm not saying that there's
levels obviously across the board, but
it is an anxiety response. So, as you

don't like this or my spouse likes
purple. like that sort of feedback where
you have the approval drift where it
just like kind of goes on forever often

or you have priority whiplash. So the

frankly every single one of these are

framework to do so. How exactly is that

## 99. How to Turn Data Into Decisions That Scale Your Creative Agency

So, some agencies say that they're data
driven. You may track revenue, leads,
close rate, CAC, and even client
retention. But here's the truth. Looking
at data isn't the same thing as using
it. So, if you don't start with a clear
business question, your dashboards just
will become noise. You end up reviewing
numbers instead of making decisions from
them. So, in this video, I'm going to
show you why most agencies data never
turn into insights and how you can
ultimately fix that. So, let's talk
about the illusion of being data driven.
So, you may have weekly metric reviews.
You may even have dashboard
screenshots. You may have Slack reports.
You may have end of month recaps. But
the thing is is I want to meaningfully
ask you this and like I want you to look
back to it from that information. What
decision was derived from it? What did
you actually do from that data? What
meaningfully operational system did you
change that allowed for you to take
impact from it? Information without
intention actually is just noise. It's a
distraction and you're not doing
anything with it. And it's incredibly
vital that that data actually becomes
something meaningful. So the true
problem when you're not actually asking
a question. So the within the data
there's a story behind each data point,
right? And it's incredibly important. So
that approach is kind of just the
question more broadly of what happened
this month. The approach that I want you
to be taking is very specific. Why did
close rate drop 8% last month? What
service line is actually having the
biggest margin compression? Why did we
have profit drop? And which
service line was the one that led mostly
to it? Is our acquisition volume
constrained by budget or our existing
conversion and what do I need to do to
change it? So that insight for you to be
able to derive from the data requires a
meaningful question that you're asking
of it. That question creates direction
which then will ultimately lead to a
decision. So data without decision is
ultimately just fully a distraction. And
so if the metric doesn't answer what
should I be doing differently? Frankly,
it was completely not useful and
pointless. So my traffic is up. Okay,
revenue is down. Well, why? So, numbers
are inputs. Decisions and
impacts are the outputs or how I want
you to be thinking about the data as a
utility within your agency. And it's
meaningfully important. The reality is
is your data should tell a story. Your
data should guide your decisions of yes
or no criteria in the directions that
you're going. What types of things
you're solving for your existing place.
And the reality of this, every business,
agencies specifically, is that finding
out the actual problem is most of the
solution when you're able to very
clearly define exactly what the problem
is. So if in the example of the margin
compression question that I was asking
earlier, now that we know exactly what
service model it is, it's either our
potential solutions are we can adjust
the inclusions, the pricing or we can
adjust the system by which we deliver
the hours. And so it's very
clear as to what solution is and it's
just this or this. and now we just have
to enact it and just do the thing. But
if we know our margins are down
and there's so many different potential
reasons why, it's very difficult for you
to make a choice. So how you go about
isolating the problem from all the
potential variables creates for a lot
clearer direction into what it is that
you're doing. So let's talk about the
decision-making framework. So step one,
define the business constraint. Revenue
stuck, margin is shrinking, pipeline is
inconsistent, what have you. What
exactly is the business problem that we
are having? And then ask a very clear
diagnostic question. Not just what
happened, but what is limiting this
outcome? Exactly. What is the data
that's allowing for this to happen? What
activity input? What is the activity
that's taking place that is creating for
this? And so be as specific as you
possibly can. Largely, it can be kind of
a hypothesis, if you will, of like,
well, is it this? Is it that? Is
it this? Now, pull the data that is
relevant to that question. And I'm not
just trying to get you to validate your
preconceived ideas. I want you to
disprove or prove the question and then
identify the lever. Is it pricing? Is it
capacity? Is it our close rate? Is it
acquisition volume? Like what exactly is
the lever that we are going to need to
change in order for this to be improved
allowing for us to actually meaningfully
enact this? So again, as I was talking
about earlier, now that we have the
defined problem, we have very clear
potential criteria for solution exactly
what we potentially can do and
then decide. But if you don't have
insight, you have no decision and then
you have no forward action and no
meaningful direction. And so you just
stay stuck. You stay in that exact same
place. You may review metrics
instead of diagnosing the constraints.
You may, you know, track like kind of
lagging indicators only, right, of like
it was the reality from things from 6
months ago that I'm no longer able to
actually have any impact to. You need to
collect data and then have a
decision-making cadence that if the
numbers in front of you, it should
meaningfully guide the decisions that
you're making. And another thing
is mistake within reporting as well,
which is incredibly important. But
having dashboards for dashboard sakes
don't fix thinking, don't fix decision-m
problems that actually isn't a solution.
You have to have a clear decision-making
framework, the hypothesis, the theory,
the idea of what it is that you're
needing to do so that you can get into

directional decision forward. Now that

it's actually pretty good. I can go in

>> Yeah, so so so one fun part to start
with this is is in very sort of
layperson terms just explain how an LLM

lot of confusion surrounding that. So
when you go on like ChatGPT, right? When
you type something in, that's that's a
conversation. That's a threat. So if you
start when you type in the first
message, that's the first piece of
context that it builds up. Context is
like this set of information that stays
in the conversation that the LLM is
continually consulting as it gives you
responses. And what we're doing is we
have a question answer question answer
and the LLM just tries to continue
answering what's happening in there.
They might have some specialized prompts
injected beforehand telling it what you
should do and what you cannot do that
that protect it, but that's context. And
when you hit new chat, that just starts
off like a new thing. So, one of the
things that we started talking about a
few years ago when this all came to be
to be was how do we manage this, right?
So, let's say I start a conversation and
I ask something not in quite the right

than what I wanted, right? I could spend

no, I didn't mean this, I meant that."
So, maybe please adjust the

redundant context in there that may be

effective just to hit new and just
rephrase it the right way and ask it
again. So, that that's one thing to sort
of keep in mind of how that context
works. You're not dealing with a human.
You don't need to explain yourself. You
just need to ask a very precise question
that gets you from A to B or where you
want to go in your workflow. So, that's
number one. Number two is we're

is looking over the conversation and
it's instructed to look for anything
that seems important that I should keep
in mind for later, right? And and that's
I'm simplifying this. There's much more

the LLM can sort of learn a little bit

And then the next time you start a chat

## 101. How we Made $2M in 90 Days With Just 1 Email.

So last year, one of our agencies
created $2 million in opportunities in
90 days from just one email. So
obviously all of our other agencies
started to use it as well, and the
results have been pretty awesome across
the board. So now, slight disclaimer
here, they didn't just send one email
and then put up their feet and then wait
for money to roll in. They actually had
to sell the client afterwards and being
able to build a relationship. So in this
video, I'm going to break down the
strategy behind the email and who they
sent it to and why it works so well.
Plus, I'll explain the sales sequence
you need to follow up afterwards so you
can actually close that client. But
before we get into all of that, let me
first give you a bit of context to the
agency itself. So, this agency is a
branding agency and what happened for
them was that they realized that they
had a revenue gap and that they needed
to close it in order to reach their goal
and we didn't have a whole lot of time
to be able to close that gap. So, we had
to do something quickly and what stemmed
this entire strategy and being able to
do this outreach. Ultimately, the thing
that we recognized is that they actually
did have this revenue essentially right
in front of them on their porch already.
So, who were we targeting? We were
targeting people that we already had a
relationship with, people that we had
either lost an opportunity to or that we
had done business with before. So, there
was some built-in rapport and some
built-in relationship. And we ended up
picking email because it was the way for
us to go about it and do it a bit more
at best and being able to do it at
scale. So, we sent out an email, crafted
a template to every single one of them,
personalized everyone that we sent, and
over the course of a couple of hours, in
a day's worth of work, we were able to
generate the amount of calls to generate
the $2 million of opportunities. So,
what did this email say? First section
of the email was actually very personal,
either about the project that we had
recently engaged with or the project
that they had in mind that they
ultimately decided to not move forward
with. and then ultimately sharing a
couple of components that we had that
are ultimately of value around something
going on in the industry, some insights
that we thought we might be able to
provide and then offering a time to just
reconnect with them. And so the email
was very simple, something along the
lines of, "Hey, John, it's been already
3 months since we worked on XYZ project
together. Wow, time flies." I was
wanting to reach out to see exactly how
that ended up impacting your business.
if you had some key metrics around it
actually providing for ROI for you. You
know, one of the things that we've been
working on with other clients is meeting
after the project was actually launched
to allow for us to give them additional
insight of what they could be doing to
make slight tweaks to the work that they
are doing to have even more impact.
Thought I would reach out to you offer
this as well because it's been having a
lot of impact to clients. Let me know if
you've got 30 minutes next week to
reconnect. It was that simple. Very
easy. Written in the most friendly
fashion, but also assuming rapport. And
ultimately, people like free advice.
People want to have impact. And
especially for those that already have a
relationship with you, they are very
likely to respond to this email. Aside
from the content of the email, it's what
comes afterwards that really actually
makes the money. So, the call was the
real kind of money maker here. What we
did was is that we started the
conversation off with where they were
when we first met them. Any metrics that
we've been able to gain since the
project actually launched or since the
timeline that we ultimately had. We had
a brainstorm session before every single
one of these calls of the things that
this client could be doing either with
us or with others to allow for them to
accomplish the goals that we had
initially spoken about in the sales
conversations. Note that this is
predicated on you having a good sales
system and making sure that you are
asking the questions that are going to
allow for you to leverage this type of
call format. Now, in the call, we asked
them to reaffirm where they're looking
to go. What are the business challenges?
Where are they looking ahead? What are
the objectives of what they're looking
to accomplish? And so, what we did is we
shared strategic ideas of things that
they could do both on the work we had
already done and work that we could
potentially be doing with them and
following a key component, the 7030
rule. that 70% of the work that we were
suggesting to them are things that we
could do. 30% of work were ones that we
could not do or had nothing to do with
and they were aware that it had nothing
to do with the agency services allowing
for them to feel like we were here
actually providing them with advice and
this wasn't just a big pitchfest so that
as we had these conversations in the
future they're open to this conversation
because we are having meaningful advice.
And I want to make a massive caveat
here. Do not sell on this call. If they
have interest in any of the ideas that
you have, you schedule a separate time
to talk about what it would look like to
work on a project with them like this.
And make sure ultimately that you're
building rapport and taking a position
of advisor. Incredibly important to be
able to do this so that they are willing
to have conversations with you like this
in the future. And if you want more
information on exactly how to attract

for your agency, you can download our

and agencies that were able to close,
you know, $7 $10,000 worth of revenue.
And ultimately there was varied results

had been in business and the amount of
relationships they ultimately had. The
younger the agency, the less the impact.
The older the agency, the more the
impact. And over time, you're able to be

being able to have a more impactful

so a few years ago I helped this

place very exciting time in his life he

Crux of the problem was that he was
everything to his agency he was the

any consistent profitability but
ultimately because of the amount of
things that this agency did none of the
team members had the amount of
experience that he did allowing for them
to actually execute on the work there
was far too much Nuance far too much

areas that made it nearly impossible for
him to be able to get out of the work
what this ended up actually creating was
a 30% gross profitability and after you
account for rent and general
administrative expenses he largely was
taking home very very little to support
his family so by the way if David's

you have been sold a Lie by so many

that's ultimately driving profit and

with and ultimately how was he connected
to them so to begin with he was working
with everyone that came knocking on his
door and ultimately what we wanted to do

had the most experience with ultimately
ended up being the medical industry
because he had over 20 years of
experience working in that specific
industry had the knowledge the expertise
and the specialization that allows for
him to actually have the connection so
going from the place where he was

someone that he knew very well and
exactly how to help them step number two
was essentially the what or his offer
what is he doing and how ultimately does
it work what we recognize in actually
going through his specific background he
had a specific set of expertise in

his core expertise was where he knew

it worked so we could build a system on
how to deliver it step three was

from Project to recurring Revenue so
what he was actually doing to begin with
is that he was selling his websites for

you don't need to hustle more. You need

important. You've chosen this life of

here's the like synopsis of where I

I'm going to look it up so I don't

between what you love, what the world
needs, what you can be paid for, and
what you're good at. The crossover
between what you love and what the world

what the world needs and what you can be

because I pushed through that that and
and I leaned on that. You are going to
have those es and flows and I think so
how do you control that because it's
easy when you have the eb to give up and
one of the things that I learned a long
time ago was I take as much emotion out
of it as I can. I don't put labels on
anything. If I'm putting something in
place and I'm implementing it, I'm
projecting I'm going to have this
result, but it could also go this way.
And a lot of times it's like, oh, well,
if it goes this way, oh that's bad.
That's horrible. I don't know what I'm
going to do. It didn't work out. I lost
a bunch of money. And when you get into
that mindset, it's very difficult to be
creative to move yourself out of that.
So, I started taking labels out of it.
It doesn't matter. It's fact. It just
is. I remember one time it was 3 days
before my payroll was due, which was
$18,000, and I had $1,000 in my
operating account. And I'm like, I could
have been like spiraling, you know, and
it's like, nope, it just is. I can
spiral all I want, but it's not going to
change that $1,000 balance. But it freed
me up to start looking for ways to
collect that money. Look at our payment
plans. Look at our defaulted payments.
how many appointments are on the book
that we can get higher retainer amounts.
All of that allowed me to be creative to
solve it. So, whenever I look at
something, I don't put a label on it.
So, it helps me to stay out of that
mindset of, oh, this is two steps
backwards. No, what did I learn from
this?
>> I think that's interesting. I don't know
that I'm quite as good at creating that
emotional separation as you are and like
not adding a label. But the way that
I've gone about it is to just allow
myself to have that moment of like beat
myself up and then like all right, I'm
going to give myself 30 minutes to do it
and then I'm going to move on and like
I'm just going to get pick myself up and
I'm just going to have to move forward
with what it is that I need to do. The
reality is is that it doesn't change the
facts of the facts. The facts are just
they are what they are. And so we now
have to look at how do we go about
changing things within the business. And
like the one of the things that I
thought was one the day that if it could
go wrong, it did go wrong for you and
making it to court, but being able to
help someone. There's also a piece to it
that I was curious about in your own
journey as well and how it is that you
help clients in this moment is that
there's also this like component to
sacrificing yourself fully. And as I was
talking about in the early stages of
business, it does take a lot of
sacrifice of yourself. And as you start
to continue to grow and you're in a
place where you're in the 70, 80, 100
hour weeks, what do you do to get out of
that place? And like that that feeling
as an entrepreneur that so many of us
have this lie I believe that we tell
ourselves that you have to sacrifice
yourself in order to achieve success.
I'm curious of of your take on that.
>> Yeah, that's a that's a really great
question. It's called the doctrine of
sacrifice and it's easy to buy into
that. The doctrine of sacrifice says
I've got to be here 24/7 365. I've got
to work 80hour weeks. This business
can't it can't work without me. When you
buy into the doctrine of sacrifice and
you're telling yourself that because of
what you've heard other people tell you
that are necessary, you develop these
limiting beliefs that you have to do
this. And pretty soon all you've done,
you've got a business that's not
profitable. You're working 80our weeks.
you just bought yourself a job and
you're usually making less than what you
were when you were working for somebody
else. So, I think the first thing that I
had to do was wrap my mind around things
can be different. In fact, to this day
now I've been in business of some form
or another now for almost 40 years. My
number one rule is be different. If I'm
hearing all these people, all these
lawyers especially saying, "Oh, you got
to work 80our weeks." I'm like, why? Why
challenge it? I had, again, I had a
5-year-old, 2-year-old, one-year-old
from the very beginning. I told my wife,
I said, we're not working past 4:30. Not
happening. I'm not going to miss their
school events. I'm not going to miss
their sports. I'm not going to miss
dinners with them every day. Now, our
business was open till 700 p.m., but I
wasn't there. So, how do we do that?
Well, I had to leverage the talent of
other people to be there during those
business hours that actually do the
stuff better than I do and engineer
their compensation plan so that I'm
making a four to 5x return on investment
on what I'm paying them. They're there,
I'm not. It's a win-win. And and so
that's what I did was I set out to find
people who are much better at doing
those things than me. And the first
thing that I did was fire myself from
those roles, get myself out of
production, let other people do it, and
now I can work on the business instead
of working in it. Now, does that mean
it's not a 24/7 job? No. As you're a
business owner, it's always going to be
on your mind 24/7. It's just time.
>> Yeah. It's just when you when you have
the right people, you have the factory
and the system set up and the procedures
so that things happen. Um, I look at
like McDonald's as a great franch uh
systematic way of doing things. When you
have that all set up, what it does for
the owner is buy back your time. And I
can choose to be there or I can choose
not to be. Now, I'm not an owner where I
abdicate responsibility either. So, most
of the time, unless I had other things
going on, I chose to be there. But I'm
not working in the business. I'm working
on what's next. Oh, well, let's open an
office in Detroit. What about Kansas
City? Let's look at all the right and
it's it's staying up here at the 50,000
ft view that I can't do if I'm in court
five six times a week or I'm doing sales
or I'm doing petitions or whatever it
is. I I cannot focus on the business if
I'm working in it. I'd say the number
one thing to break out of the doctrine
of sacrifice is change your mindset.
There was this like interesting moment
and my husband had got me a book and I

sacrificing my life moment and I looked
at it like yeah right do nothing.
>> I'm working 80our weeks. We'll talk
about Do Nothing later. The thing that I
found really interesting in the book
that I wasn't aware of until I read it
was that there was actually a PR
campaign that was created in like I want
to say it was back in like the 40s
around this idea that when you sacrifice
yourself in work and to success that it
actually is like next in and attached to

in society. And one of the things that
like I realized over time is that I am
great at filling my list of things to do
because of this societal belief system
that being busy means that I'm doing

you like get together with friends, we
end up like almost bragging about our
busyiness. And it's exactly the wrong
kind of idea. And if you set up these

around yourself where you're able to
then not be in that place. And I really
love that from the very beginning, you
set up a constraint was 4:30 and done.
And it just forces a different decision.
It forces a different result.
>> It does. And I've had I've had people in
in a Facebook group for lawyers that
were on there just bragging over each
other. Oh, I worked 70 hours last week.
I worked 80. I worked 90. And I'm like,
what are you guys bragging about?
>> Right? Not that I brag about not having
to work in my business, but what it did
allow me to go to your point about
staying busy. I agree. I think if I
point to scripture for me is being
productive, is being busy is doing those

by putting the systems and the people in

>> but it's also still serving your why.
I'm I'm curious on what your advice

I find there's a lot of similarities
there. So agency owners is very similar.
And the very first thing I do is have

doing this? Why did you choose to become
an agency owner? Why did you choose that
over going to work for somebody else at
at at this agency? Why did you choose
the type of agency that you own? What is

do this? And the more that you can tune

story with your with your staff and
across your brand, it's powerful. It
allows you to really key in on I'm not
just here. In fact, I had this question
come up early on, not early on, but we
were making about $4 million a year at

cases and I remember somebody asking me,
"How do you live with yourself making
that much money off of people that are

doing to those people? And I'm m I'm

But then when I started to think about

fresh start to save their houses, their
cars, keep their jobs because they're
not late at for work again to put food
on the table and give their kids a
better life and just have something that
they couldn't get on their own. And when
I started to look at the value that we
bring, it was like the money that we
charge was a bargain compared to that.

not doing this to somebody. I'm doing it

## 116. Parkinson's Law for Creatives： Work Smarter, Reduce Stress, Gain Time

Working in a creative environment can be
really stressful. You work on different
projects at different times and nothing
is really planned in advance. Or maybe
you've planned everything, but a client
calls you and suddenly everything just
goes to So you end up working late
or on the weekend to get everything
done, which means you spend less time
with your friends and family, which just
blows. But if this sounds familiar, not
to worry. There's a very simple way to
be able to fix this. I'm going to walk
you through a simple productivity hack
for creatives that will help you take
back control and win a full eight hours
a week. But in order to explain this
hack, I need to start and explain a law.
All right, so this is a law principle
rather than a law that's going to get
you in trouble. But the law is called
Parkinson's law. The concept is that
humans will utilize all available
resources that are made available to us.
And we know that that's true time and
time and time again from bank accounts
to earthly resources etc. But this
applies primarily to time in this
instance. So I want to give you two
different scenarios. The exact same end
result but two different people with two
different motivations and different
amounts of time available. So first we
have grandma. She needs to send out a
birthday card to her granddaughter.
Grandma sits has her coffee thinks about
what kind of birthday card she wants to
send out. what she wants to write in
that birthday card. And then she goes to
CVS to go find that birthday card. While
she's there, she realizes, "Oh no, I
forgot my purse at home, drives back
home, sits down, has a cup of tea, and
then goes back to CVS, buys that
birthday card." Then she goes back home,
thinks about exactly again what she
wants to write, writes it in the card,
and then drives to the post office to
get it mailed out. It took her over 4
hours to get this entirely done from
beginning to end. high-powered CEO. She
has a whole lot less time available, but
needs to send a birthday card to her
niece. She's got 30 seconds in between
two meetings to get this birthday card
out. So, she goes to her EA and says,
"Hey, I need you to go down to CVS, pick
out a birthday card that's largely
around this area, and here's what I want
you to write inside of it, and then I
want you to mail it out to my niece."
So, she got the exact same thing done,
but in 30 seconds. That took grandma 4
hours. So, let's take a look at how this
plays out in your real life. So, you
have your day planned. You have a
project that you were about to start.
You get going on the project and then an
email comes up. Bing. You get pulled
into that email. You're starting to
respond. In the midst of you getting
that email, bing over here, you now have
a Slack message. And then finally, okay,
I'm now back to the project again. I can
get back into it. And then someone turns
around and asks you a question and
interrupts you. In the midst of you
doing that, your phone goes bing and you
get a text message and so now you're
pulled over there and then finally back
to the project again and then a phone
call. Okay. And then repeat. And this
happens day after day, month, hour after
hour, month after month, year after year
ongoing for the vast majority of
creatives. There was a study that was
done that dissected the actual
productivity of white collar workers and
found that 6 hours of an 8-hour day was
wasted in these exact types of
distractions and you need to avoid them.
So now that we have what the issue is,
how do we actually solve for it? So the
first thing that is incredibly important
is understand what your ideal day looks
like. So understanding when you're going
to spend time for meetings, when you're
going to reply to Slack messages, when
you're going to reply to those text
messages, when you have phone calls,
what time you're going to work on
projects, and when are you going to get
those administrative things done from
even reviewing creative work to having
conversations with your team,
understanding exactly what 30-inut
blocks are dedicated for what, giving
you a mechanism to understand where
things will fit in. Then we have to
evaluate that long to-do list that in
some instances has items on it that have
been collecting dust for a long time
that we haven't been able to get to
because of all of the things that has
been creating distraction. So I want you
to take that to-do list and I want you
to take a white sheet of paper. Write
them all down. Once you have them
written down, we need to filter through
them. The first thing is, am I the only
person in the world to get this done?
Should I actually be doing this? Is this
worth my time? Yes or no? I want you to
truly evaluate this and make sure that
you should actually be the one doing
this. The next thing is, is this a
priority? Is this going to move the
needle for me or not? It is important
that we focused on the priority items
that we need to get done both for your
clients as well as for your agency.
Making sure that you're able to make
progress. There's nothing more defeating
than having a to-do list that continues
to grow that somehow ends up longer at
the end of the day than how it was when
you started. It makes things incredibly
stressful and really incredibly
defeating. So from that point forward,
this is now Friday or Thursday. Whenever
you end your week, you're going to go
through this entire list and you're
going to plan out the entire week ahead
of time, listing out exactly when you're
going to be responding to emails and
communication, when you're going to work
on those projects that you had on the
to-do list, when you're going to be
meeting with both clients and your
internal team. Being very clear of when
you are going to do what I want to point
out here is that you want to be careful
to understand that this is not going to
work perfectly well the first time.
First day it's going to go ah a little
bit chaotic still. You still have the
habit of continuing to be distracted.
Close those programs down. Day two
better. Day five better. Day 50 better
still, but still not perfect. you'll
continue to get better over time,
allowing for you within a short period
of time being able to actually save an
incredible amount of time, be less
stressed out, have loads more focus, and
make a whole lot more progress. And
that's the life I want for you. So, know
that the average is able to save 8 hours
a day for the vast majority of
individuals. But in some examples, it's

## 129. Stop Decision Fatigue： Creative Agency Leadership Hacks

Decision fatigue is real, especially as
an agency owner. So, you've got client
choices to internal ops, hiring,
pricing, volume of decisions that you
have every week, frankly, is just
overwhelming. And when everything feels
like it's on you, it's easy to either
slow down completely or make choices
that you're going to ultimately end up
regretting later, just kind of needing
to make a snap choice. So, in this
episode, I want to break down practical
ways to reduce decision fatigue, and
ultimately be able to make better
choices, better decisions. And so, we're
going to cover frameworks of decision
ownership, how to evaluate risk more
clearly, and how to build a
solutionoriented culture so that not all
things are going to come to you. And
then ultimately you can have a clear job
of yes or no but not needing to think
through all the nuances of every single
decision that needs to make. And so here
let's start with the sort of cost of
decision fatigue. The reality of it is
that it really truly just drains your
energy and it creates delays in your own
workflow and your team. So the reality
is is that the larger of a team that you
start to grow, you end up becoming a
bottleneck in potential and indecision
and being able to have an issue of being
able to actually make the number of
decisions. And it actually creates for
so much nuance, so many different
considerations that it is just
overburdensome to your own nervous
system. And if you're in a place where
you don't have all the information to
even make the choice in the first place,
it actually creates for a bad choice.
There's a reality of there's so many
pieces around the initial stages of
business that actually create for what
reality of it is is that I feel like
it's a bit of an inevitability at a
certain stage of business that you have
to feel in order to change and actually
make a discernable difference in how it
is that you go about making choices but
at the first stage of business and the
reality is as an entrepreneur as a
business owner that you know that the
risk ultimately falls on you and with
that risk it ends up creating a dynamic
where you want to be involved in the
decision because you end up feeling like
if a bad decision is made, the wrong
choice is made that the reality of it is
that it falls on you. And while that is
true that the ultimate thing ends up the
the buck stops with you in the end, that
if you are not actually allowing
yourself to grow, it ends up creating
greater risk in the long term. And so
what is it that you have as a risk that
you're willing to live with? And so you
don't actually have to have everything
going through you. And the reality is is
that mistakes are going to happen and
that each one of those mistakes that
ultimately do happen is an opportunity
for you to be able to learn from it. And
as time progresses in your own
entrepreneurship, in your own business,
in your own agency, is that over time
you are going to not have the capacity
to make these choices and you're going
to have to give them off to other
people. And as that starts to happen is
a bit of a change not only in mindset
but in practicality of it. And it
becomes a necessity at some stage. It is
a difficult stage for every entrepreneur
to get to, frankly. But it is a it is a
skill that you're going to have to
learn. And the shorter time that it
takes you to learn it, the better off
you're going to be, the it took me a
long time to learn it. And it happened
in the most obscure way where I learned
it. And there was a lot of my own ego,
my own belief in selfworth that was tied
into the busyiness into the decision-m
into people relying upon me for those
decisions that ultimately created for it
for myself and for my business. But the
reality is is that it doesn't have to be
that way and you likely don't even
really want it to be that way and it
becomes such a problem and it actually
un letting go of pieces of it is a big
catalyst for your growth. And so if
you're in a place right now where you're
feeling like you are starting to get to
aspects of decision fatigue where
everything is falling on you and you are
the block in the funnel, hopefully you

reality is is that when you're in this
place and this kind of what I was
describing as the inevitability is that
it ends up creating this piece that
you're training your team because of
that. You're training your team to come
to you to make the decision. And so you
end up diluting or even deincentivizing
their own freeth thinking to solve the
problems on their own. And so they end
up coming to you for every decision that
needs to be made. And so you're creating
for this block in the funnel yourself.
And you're making it happen. And in
order for you to be able to escape from

show up with your team. And you're going
to have to retrain them to start to be
freeth thinkers themselves with limits
that allow for them to come with
solutions. Allow for them to start to
not rely on you for every decision that

really simple framework that was
initially brought forward by Dan
Sullivan called the 131. And so as he
describes it, it is called one identify
the true core of the problem. And so
there's often and there's nuance to this
piece and I don't know if this is Dan
Sullivan specifically but you've got the
known problem, the problem that you have
in front of you, the unknown problem and
then the hidden problem. And what you
need to do is really understand what is
the core of this actual problem that you
have in front of us. And then I want the
team member to come with three potential
solutions to solve this issue and then
their one recommendation. And so what's
important here is for them to start to
think through what are the
possibilities, what are the things that
could potentially solve for this problem
and then start to come with that
recommendation. And very frequently to
begin with, they are going to come with
the wrong solutions and the wrong
recommendations. And you need to not
solve the problem. You need to have them
go back and consider them again. And did
you consider this? Did you consider
this? Did you consider this? And so this
process empowers the team to come
prepared to you. Not just to simply
escalate the problem to you, but to come
prepared with potential solutions. It
helps create clarity and communication.
It starts to build that decision-m skill
across your team cuz it is a skill. And
it shifts the responsibility away from
you decide what needs to happen, which
you're still doing in that circumstance,
is here's my thought. What do you think?
Do you approve? And allows for you to be
able to have a bit of a different
dynamic. And it's the first stage to
removing yourself from this block in the
funnel where you are in this constant
place of needing to be the solution to
every problem. The piece that becomes
really important here that is a bit of a
difficult thing to learn. And as I was
talking about at the beginning of the

owners, we see and we know and we
recognize that ultimately we hold the

to be able to understand which pieces
are we willing to delegate the risk to
and understanding kind of different
taring of the types of risk factors that
are being assumed within the team. And
the reality of that is that as time
progresses we get better and better at

that it's still something that you

well. It's like what exactly is the

power. And so being able to start to

fatigue from where you ultimately feel.
And then finally the piece that I think
is important for you to start to instill

question that is being asked, what
should we do in this scenario, it's what
what have we considered? What have we
already tried? What have we thought
about? What were the solutions we
explored? What is the risk profile
already here? And the reality is is that
any evolution in a career results in

things, going and venturing out into new
potential within the agency and starting

as that we as we start to fill each one

they're so often around this idea of
process that you're giving them step

## 134. Storytelling That Closes High-Ticket Clients (For Creative Agencies)

Storytelling isn't about being
charismatic. It's not about telling
longer case studies. For creative
agencies in 2026, storytelling is a
strategic tool. It's how you transfer
belief before you ever ask for
commitment. If a prospect fully believes
the problem is real, the solution is
possible and that you are the right
partner. Price resistance will almost
disappear. But most agencies don't use
storytelling correctly. In this video,
I'll show you the exact storytelling
structure high performing creative
agencies use to close deals without
pressure. Let's go ahead and break it
down. So, what most agencies rely upon
are portfolio slides, deliverable lists,
process explanations. But the reality is
is that information doesn't actually
create movement. And people don't just
buy because they understand. The reality
is is that if they wanted to understand,
they would actually do your job. What
they end up doing is they really buy
because they believe. And if belief
forms emotionally before they've
justified it logically, then it allows
for them to actually fully buy in. And
if that emotional buyin doesn't happen
first, the logical explanation ends up
feeling like pressure. So the reality is
is that you need to ensure that they
have that emotional buyin. They need to
believe that they can accomplish it,
that it is actually possible, and that
you are that right partner. And so you
do that by actually connecting with them
human to human, being able to actually
allow for them to articulate their
problem, have some clarity as to exactly
how it is that they can go about solving
it, and ultimately selecting you as the
partner, and then we can rationalize the
decision that we've already made. The
real function of storytelling in sales
is that storytelling really is not
entertainment. It's about how you
actually transfer a belief system to
them. And they have to actually believe.
And so when it's structured correctly,
what storytelling will ultimately do is
it creates that emotional identification
how I see myself in this story. It's
able to actually normalize a decision
because the reality is is that we
largely as human beings are skeptics and
reasonably so. And how we can go about
normalizing the objection of, oh, I feel
this, but someone else
ultimately felt that as well. It makes
things a lot simpler and easier. And it
also allows for us to demonstrate
transformation.
So none of us really truly believe in
the easy button that we do know that it
is going to take some transition. It's
going to take some work. And the more
that you try to sell this like snake oil
oriented piece of it, it ends up
creating for someone to have
more disbelief rather than belief. And
again, this is entirely predicated on
belief that they have in the problem and
the solution and you and then
having an understanding about what that
outcome ultimately looks like. someone
had this, they went through this, they
accomplished this allows for
someone to feel like it actually becomes
possible. The analogy I like to use here
is
nobody is going to be convinced to get
on an airplane if no one had ever flown
one before, right? The first airplane
that was ever flown, people did not want
to get on it. And the reality is is that
you only feel comfortable getting on an

single day and largely we know that
they're relatively safe. It's
very uncommon for there to be
destruction and it allows for us to feel
more comfortable. So understanding what
the outcome actually is and that other
people have accomplished it. Prospects
don't just buy the result, they
buy the feeling that they're looking to
achieve. The reality is is the
result is a means by which for them to
identify themselves. So this is how this
feels now and this is how this should
feel instead. And that's the emotional
shift that you want your
prospect to feel. I feel this. I want to
feel this. And you are going to be that
connective tissue between the two. And
so as you actually follow this
framework, this emotional kind of story
arc, I want you to walk through each one
of these different pieces. So
step one, you're going to talk about the
negative emotion, the before feeling. I
want you to describe how the
client felt before they solved the
problem. So you know, they were feeling
like a failure. They weren't sure that
they were capable. They had tried so
many times and they thought maybe it was
them. And then two, you want to talk
about the concrete problem. So exactly
what it was that was going on within
their business. So the factual situation
of what they were facing. So, you're
going to describe the problem now, the
actual true black and white description
of it. Three, you're going to talk about
objections. So, the reality is is that
every single person, every single

worked with asked you some kind of
question that
made them worried that this potential
direction may not be the right one. And
I want you to hit them head-on, and it

this direction. You're going to say
things like they weren't sure that
working with an agency would work
because an agency is not going to know
them as well as they know themselves or
they thought that they could solve this
internally that they had the people

happen or they were concerned about the

would this provide for the ROI that they
were looking for considering they were
still in their launch phase. So what
this does is this normalizes the
prospect's current hesitation that if
someone else felt this before and oh I
feel this right now then this makes this
experience normal. So this changes the
way that they interact with the feeling
that they currently have and they're
going to have it. Normalization of it is

Exactly. What happened? What was it?
Revenue increase. Did we increase

## 137. The #1 Mistake Creative Agencies Make That Loses Clients Every Time

So, in today's world, great work isn't
enough to keep clients coming back. Most
agencies focus on deliverables, but they
forget that in reality, clients care
just as much and maybe even more about
this particular thing. After years of
losing seemingly happy clients and
learning a bit the hard way, I started
asking them for feedback, and the
pattern was a bit clear. It was rarely
about the work itself. Instead, it was
lack of clarity, poor follow-up, and
simply not feeling listened to that sent
them somewhere else. Today, I'll show
you the most common reasons clients walk
away so you can fix your system and keep
the clients that you worked so hard to
win within your agency and continuing to
grow. The way that I'm going to go
through this is that I want to break
down for you this versus that contrast
of the way that we ultimately see things
and how it is that we should be
adjusting and making things better to
allow for things to function
appropriately. So, the way that most
agencies think is that a polished
project is ultimately the priority. But
the reality is is that clients care
about fast, clear, and frequent updates
much more. So there's been so many
examples where when working with
agencies, the clients end up being

you're looking for.

working in a creative environment can be

law the concept is that humans will
utilize all available resources that are
made available to us from bank accounts
to Earthly resources Etc but this
applies primarily to time in this
instance so I want to give you two
different scenarios the exact same end
result but two different people with two

is am I the only person in the world to

## 141. The #1 Profit Killer for Creative Agencies (Most Owners Miss This)

You may not be losing profit because
your pricing is wrong. You're losing it
because of the invisible work that never
gets tracked, never gets scoped, and
never gets talked about. It's the tiny
tasks, the quick fixes, the slack
replies, the reexplaining the same
thing, the chasing clients, the
correcting avoidable mistakes, the
things that don't feel big in the
moment, but end up adding on hours every
single day and every single week. These
hidden tasks quietly destroy your
margins, end up overwhelming your team,
and make projects feel so much harder
than they ultimately should be. So, in
this video, I'm going to show you the
hidden tasks that are killing your
agency's profit. The simple system that
allows you to eliminate them so your
projects become healthier, smoother, and
far more profitable. Let's jump in. So,
let's first start with the type of tasks
and what they actually are. So, they're
the small unplanned things. They aren't
in scope. They aren't on the timeline.
They aren't in project management. They
don't show up at any invoices anywhere.
They aren't tracked. And they eat up
time kind of silently. They feel tiny
but when you multiply them across the
team they end up killing margins and
actually can be a significant amount of
time. One quick little example agency
years ago they did not have a email
service that allowed for them to be able
to see what other calendars were
available and did not share their
calendars agencywide. So they spent a
lot of time emailing each other back and
forth of when they could ultimately
meet. And one quick little fix of a lot
of that specific thing ended up saving
them almost a full-time person across a
35 person agency. Just being able to do
that one little thing that made things
incredibly simple and saving an
incredible amount of time. It was almost
a full-time body. So there's lots of
little things that are going on within
your agency. So let's talk about why
hidden tasks can destroy profit. So they
end up breaking focus is a key piece.
Knowing that ultimately from a
productivity perspective, if I am being
interrupted to do this small little
thing, it takes me a while to be able to
dive back into the thing that I was
focusing on, it ends up adding on, you
know, a cognitive load. So when I end up
feeling like I have a lot of things that
are happening throughout the day, it
ends up creating for a diminishing
ability to be able to focus my time and
attention on other things and makes
especially for creatives, it ends up

## 145. The 4 Types of Team Members Inside Every Creative Agency

Sameness is one of the fastest ways to
lower team performance. When you apply
the same expectations to everyone, you
don't create balance, you create
stagnation. So, some people get over
managed, some people get under managed,
and the overall standard quality drops.
Creative agencies don't struggle because
their teams lack talent. They struggle
because leadership fails to
differentiate. Capability and commitment
are not evenly distributed, but
leadership attention often is. So, in
this video, I'll show you how
high-performing creative teams are
actually built and why managing everyone
the same is actually what keeps your
agency average. So, it's actually a
failure of leadership to treat everyone
the same, and fairness and sameness is
actually not the same thing. So, the
reality is is that when you treat
everyone the same, high performers end
up feeling constrained, low performers
end up feeling protected, development
stagnates,
and frustration builds quietly across
the entire team. And so, here's what it
ends up doing to the agency. A number of
small people carry the creative output.
Dead weight hides behind this kind of
team culture, and the founder becomes
the performance equalizer. So,
high-performance leadership really
requires you to actually treat people
differently in order to be able to
support them of where they are. So,
differentiation here in the way that you
show up as a leader is actually vital to
allowing for you to do so. In it,
fairness is still important, sameness is
the problem. And so, here's how I want
you to be thinking about it. And the
dimensions that I want you to be
considering is capability and
commitment. And how do you actually
think about this matrix of people? So,
capability is skill, judgment, creative
quality, execution, and then commitment
is the ownership that they actually have
over it, the motivation that they have,
the engagement, and energy that they
show up to every single day. So, I want
you to be able to like pivot these
really on two axes, so that you're able
to understand four distinct profiles.
Each of them requires a different
version of leadership response and a
different version of leadership from
you, the owner, or from their manager.
So, quadrant one, low capability, low
commitment. So, here's what happens.
They're missing deadlines, they require
repeated direction, they show minimal
initiative, and they frankly just create
emotional drag across the team. We need
people committed and being able to
actually perform. They consume a
disproportionate amount of attention
from the team. They actually drain team
morale and they quietly normalize low
standards across the entire team. So,
here's what you need to be doing
differently and here's the correct
approach of how you should be managing
this specific quadrant. Immediate
clarity to the expectations of how you
expect them to show up, a short
performance window of when you expect
for them to meet those expectations, and
specific measurable improvement. So, not
black, it needs to be very black and
white and I performed it, I did the
thing or I didn't. And not this like
area of gray. They need to be fully
measurable and here's the thing that you
need to do. And then decisive action if
no change. So, that may be a PIP and
that may be just a termination. And you
need to allow for the reality of the
people that you elevate and the people
that you terminate are actually creating
your culture. Maturity in this moment
means protecting the team standard and
protecting the rest of the team.
Tolerating underperformance lowers the
ceiling for everyone across the entire
agency. All right, quadrant two is high
commitment, low capability. So, this
person shows up eager, they're positive,
they're coachable, and yet they're just
not strong enough technically to
actually do the work. And so, the error
that I see happen, and I've done this
myself too, is that we assume that
attitude actually compensates for lack
of skill indefinitely. It It It doesn't.
We actually need high performers and
people that are committed to. And so,
why this ultimately matters is they want
to contribute and they want to become,
they can actually have become strong A
players. But, unmanaged, they are going
to actually create a lot of frustration
and problems. And so, they require a lot
of personal development and a lot of
time and attention to do so. And if you
don't have the time to actually pour
into them, it's not going to work out
for them or for you. And so, here's the
correct approach assuming that you are
able to invest appropriately. Structured
skill-building plan of here's exactly
what you're going to be focusing on and
here's exactly how we're going to
structure your development. That clear
capability milestones of here's when we
expect you to be able to do this
specific thing. Defined timelines for
growth in order for you to allow for
that to actually happen and then regular
coaching or feedback for this particular
individual. So, like I said, this
actually does take an incredible amount
of investment in this specific
individual to allow for them to perform
to the degree that you want to. And
commitment cannot be trained. Capability
often can be. And so, that's an
incredible distinction is that
commitment is a very difficult thing for
you to be able to bring in, but
capability you can help bridge the gap.
And this is where you can invest, but
with meaningful structure. Then we have
high capability, low commitment. So,
this is the person that is technically
strong, cynical, disengaged. They do the
absolute minimum and frankly they're a
potential flight risk. So, the reality
of the world is that you end up ignoring
them because they just they they
deliver. They are not problematic.
They're the least squeaky wheel. These
people influence culture heavily. Their
disengagement will spread across the
entire organization and their exit
actually creates for major instability
because they are the performers, they're
the ones that you relied upon, they're
the ones that has been the easy person
that's always been there. So, they have
slowly created for greater disengagement
across the entirety of the team. So, the
correct way that you want to manage this
particular person is that you want to
have a direct conversation about their
ownership over things. And you want to
look at how do you actually increase the
amount of responsibility that they have
and allow for them to accept that
ownership, that commitment to
it. And you want to challenge them and
not just provide them with comfort. That
trying to provide them with comfort
actually doesn't do anything for them.
And you do want to also create greater
visibility and recognition. So, when
they do great things, you want to give
them greater visibility and greater
recognition because that increases the
amount of commitment. High capability
without commitment is frankly wasted
leverage and you want to be able to
allow for things to change. And what
exactly is the thing that is creating
for that disengagement and lack of
commitment? Is that something that is
recoverable? Is that something that you
can actually shift or change? And your
job is to reengage them or ultimately
let them go if you cannot do so. And
then quadrant four is the high
capability and high commitment, your
true A-players. They exceed
expectations, they they proactive, they
raise the standard, and they influence
others around them positively. Why this
really is important is that they drive
up to 80% of the real progress that's
happening across the organization, and
they shape culture silently without
recognition. And frankly, those A
players are the ones that are ultimately
poached and recruited against
constantly. Those are the type of people
that people are looking for, and they're
trying to find those individuals. How
you want to approach this specific
individual is you want to increase the
amount of autonomy that this person has.
You want to give them authority over
decision-making power. You want to allow
for this person to actually influence
more of the organization. You want that
allows for them to elevate their
responsibility, elevate their
commitment. And you want to be able to
strengthen and involve them more in
their strategic decisions. If you
involve them more in strategic
decisions, you're going to strengthen
their commitment and strengthen the
resolve in that future world. The more
that they actually see themselves in the
direction that the business is headed,
the more impactful they are going to
feel, and the more commitment you are
going to cement. You do want to
recognize them consistently, because
those are the people that you want to
elevate. This is the type of person that
we want to emulate. And so, we're
showing the rest of the team that here's
the archetype of the person that we're
looking for. Here's the archetype of the
person that you want to look like, and
here's the person that we should be
recognizing. That allows for us to start
to develop meaningful leadership
capacity, because these are the
individuals that are going to free up
leadership and potentially even become
leaders of the organization in
the future. Here's the thing, high
performers will leave when stagnation

## 147. The Best Way To Start A Sales Pitch for Creative Agencies

Most agencies lose the pitch before they
ever start. Not because their offer is
bad, not because the pricing is wrong,
and not because the prospect isn't
qualified. They lose it, frankly, in the
first 5 minutes of the call. Because
instead of taking control, instead of
establishing authority, instead of
creating agreement, they start pitching
and making themselves appear or feeling
desperate. And once you position
yourself as a peer instead of as an
authority, you've already lost all power
in the conversation. In this video, I'm
going to show you how to start a sales
conversation in a way that makes your
prospects naturally agree with you
before you ever start talking about
direction or your services. So, let's
talk about why prospects are lost early.
So, what agencies are often doing is
they start to explain what it is that
they do. They believe that they are
needing to explain effectively why
they're the best thing since sliced
bread. And they are trying to build
rapport, but not in a meaningful way, or
they will let the prospects control the
tone of the conversation. They'll ask
questions, they will try to fully mimic
the other person, and they will sound
eager to make the sale rather than
actually feeling structured. But here's
the thing that's incredibly important.
If you feel like a peer, you're going to
get peer pricing. You cannot and you
shouldn't be. The reality is is that
people tend to buy from someone that
already has something, knowledge,
expertise, experience, something that
they need to acquire. And a premium
requires authority. You shouldn't be a
peer in that scenario. So, step one of
this is that you need to take control of
the frame. So, you're the one that
should be establishing the rules of the
conversation. And I don't want you to do
it aggressively, you should still do it
clearly. So, here's an example of it.
So, "Hey John, Vanessa, whatever, we've
got 45 minutes on the schedule today.
Does this still work for you?" And then,
the follow-up, presumably they say yes.
So, here's how I generally like to
structure this conversation, blank.
Is that okay with you? Then, all right,
so here's the next step. So, assuming
that this happens, this would be our
next step in the conversation. Does that
sound good? And getting buy-in. So,
here's a couple of things. One, you're
creating them getting used to saying yes
to you. Little trick, kind of hack
oriented thing. The thing that's most
important though, it actually creates
that you're the one in control. You're
the one creating the structure, which
creates authority. When someone else is
in control, people tend to feel safer.
The reality is even for control freaks
like myself, when we're buying a
service, what we want to know is someone
else is actually manage this piece
without our involvement. And I'm hiring

people only pay a premium to someone
that has greater insight, greater
clarity, and who ultimately also feels

like one. So, even if you're successful,

And then you're going to feel like

You are the person that has the
experience here. You're the one that

I had gone on a trip in Greece with a

So like back in the day it was like it

>> Right.
>> And so my emails would kind of load but

this ego need of mine to feel needed.
And in order for me to be able to get to
what I wanted to have in the business so
that I wasn't at the stressful place, I
needed to actually not be in the space

no one can actually stand in the same
place that I'm standing in. So, I have
to get out of the spot
>> and and let them actually take it over.
