coming to me with a problem or a
complaint, number one, you have to
clearly define what the problem is. Not
just be like, I don't know what to do.
There's issues. Like, that doesn't work.
It's like the rorowaz isn't what we need
it to be on this one campaign. Okay,
clearly define the problem. Step two,
bring three potential solutions to the
table. Be like, I think we should mix in
some new creative. I think we should
adjust the landing page. Um, I think we
should test some new audiences. Like,
okay, those are great, viable solutions.
And then at the end, what is what would
you do if this decision fell 100% on
your shoulders? So 131. And we've
integrated that into our agency. And
it's amazing. My team will come to me
like, hey, we have this problem. Here's
the three things. Here's what I think we
should do. We don't have to get on a

is keeping things simple and getting
back to what you're good at. A lot of

to be maniacal about your systems and

been agencies. So it's the market where

number one challenge that we come across
is an overdependence on referrals and

business except if they are referred or
if they somebody from their network
hears about them and reaches out. And
that's a shortcoming. I mean it it works

my clients were international either
from the UK, the US or sometimes more

had to learn how to do outreach to

about 90% of our business was coming
from outbound. And that's when I

owners that wait a minute in the agency
market this is actually a uh a big

of when I first started to think about
doing this as its own full-time thing
and that's the most common problem.
Aside from that, the second most common
problem that I see with agencies is
probably around hiring and building a

maintaining the quality of the work

difficult. Finding good people is

and he told me that look I've gone

seven months and then I have to fire

are the the top two challenges that I
see.
>> Those are very common challenges. I mean
I think that from a just like a
reflective perspective on the reason why
the referrals piece is I mean at least
for me in my encounter with the vast
majority of agencies that I come across
is that they tend to be a bit more
introverted. So the idea of putting
themselves out there is a bit scary, but
it also has this like I I don't think
that it can be understated the the
impact that being waiting for a referral
to come across and to come knocking on
your door creates for pressure within
the organization because then it makes
you feel like you have to sell every

door. And then it ends up making that
you're then putting your pricing down in
order to make sure that you are able to
close the deals which then ends up
having impact to margin which then ends
impact to who you can hire and how much
you can afford to hire and then the
talent that you're able to have which
then creates loads of other problems and
then this idea of scaling ends up
becoming more of a problem because you
just end up scaling chaos. Speaking of
that kind of direction from a pricing
perspective, I mean the reality of where
we find ourselves right now is that
there's recessionary pressure that's
going on, buyer skepticism that's
happening because of it and that's also
creating for you know there's this
reality of increased competition out
there because there's so many people
that are agencies now like it's a person
with a laptop sitting at a Starbucks and
so how are you seeing that impacting

>> It's definitely a big impact. I would
say that the market has grown more
skeptical over the years. The low
barriers to entry are obviously part of
the problem and standards are also part
of the problem. And I've seen this in

much and then the agency actually

They're like, "Wow, you know, I didn't
expect this." Which is funny because,
you know, that shouldn't be the
standard. Um, it's definitely more of a
problem. But I will say this, even
though skepticism is higher than it than
it used to be in the past, even though
there's more scammy agencies or agencies
that deliver lower quality service,
there are still ways to stand out and to
connect with the right people where you
can actually make a difference through
your business. So um this is something
that I think every founder has to
reflect at a personal level. I believe
personally in pricing based on the value
that you deliver and that's something
that I try to impart to the agencies
that we work with. If there's a way to
measure the impact that you're having on
a business, for us it's much easier,
right? Because we deliver meetings, the
meetings have a certain value, it's a
bit easier to do the math. But even if
you sell something like design, right,
like you, let's say that you do logo
design, even if you have a service like
that, there is still some way to assess
the impact or the meaning of what you
do. And my favorite example which I
always give when I speak with the owners
of designer branding agencies is let's
say that you're speaking with a trucking
company and the trucking company tells
you we want to redo our logo. Most
design companies are or design agencies
will simply tell them here's the price
you know let's get started. But that's
the wrong approach. What you want to do
is you want to actually identify what
value redoing the logo has. One way to
approach that, for example, is to ask
them, well, what do you actually plan to
do with your logo? Because, for example,
they they may say, we want to put it on
our trucks. Well, how many trucks do you
have? We have a,000 trucks around the
world. Okay. How much does it cost to
get the logo on one truck? Oh, it costs,
you know, $100, right? So suddenly 100 *
1,000 is now a much bigger value right
that um in terms of the impact right so
if they get it wrong the cost is huge so
they want to get it right it's things
like that that I think most agency
owners still don't know how to do and
it's affecting them because they can't
communicate the value of their work to a
buyer in a way that's meaningful for
that buyer and if the value is not
understood then they cannot be paid or
compensated as they would want to be.
The other issue that I see is that many
owners lose track of this and they're
like oh my design is just worth this
much and they don't really think from
the perspective of the buyer well you
know what's the actual value here for
them. So, I think learning a good sales
process is important because you're
going to be able to do these things and
you're going to learn to estimate your
own value, not just for yourself, but
also for the other party,
>> especially as you're now dealing with
people that are that are not coming in

they have even more skepticism than
those that came in via referral. But I
but I but I agree, but I'm going to add

the reality is is that logo that needs

we have greater impact with our

conversation around this productization

people feel like hey you know I don't

skepticism a little bit more. So buyers,
the reality of buyers today is that they
are more skeptical than ever, but
there's both the economic component,
there's the agency component that that
there's that lower barrier of entry. And
so they may have had a bad experience in
the past that makes them reasonably
skeptical around the impact that the
agency work is going to have for that
that organization or for them because of
past experience. And so what approaches
are you seeing or strategies or cutting
through that skepticism to have a more
meaningful conversation? The best
strategy is to have an entry-level
offer. An entry-level offer that
addresses that particular objection that
they have at that point. Like for
example, the objection could be this
doesn't work. We've tried before. Or it
could be something else. It could be
we're afraid that you're not actually
going to deliver. So the idea in all
this is that most people and most
agencies just think these are our
standard offers. this is what we're
going to sell and they're stuck in that
mindset. What I try people to do and
what we do at our own agency is we have
entry- level offers. So in our case, if
somebody comes in and they're like, I
have no idea if outbound is going to
work for me. What we're going to tell
them is like, okay, we're not sure if
it's going to work either. How about we
do a physibility study? It's going to
cost this much. We're going to know for
sure if it works. We're going to tell
you how big your potential target market
is. We're going to tell you how many we
can reach. And based on other data that
we have from companies targeting a
similar ICP, we will give you an
estimate for how much you're going to
end up paying per meeting. And that's an
offer specifically designed for somebody
who is skeptical. And that's why it
works because it's designed for them.
The mistake that I see a lot of agencies
doing is that they just have offers for
their referral clients, not
surprisingly, who already trust them.
So, it's no surprise that you struggle
to sell those offers to a cold market
because they were never built for a cold
market. This is also why lead generation
for agencies, at least outbound lead
generation, usually has a bad name. Most
outbound companies don't have a focus on
agencies, unlike us, and they haven't
learned the specific problems that
agencies have. With an agency, it's not
like you have a SAS product, here's what
it does, and you just go out selling it.
You're actually selling the expertise
that the people have. But that expertise
can be condensed in multiple different
offers that are going to appeal to
multiple different people. And you have
to design the right one that's actually
going to work for a um cold audience.
And I call these entry-level offers
because they make it easy to get started
with you. Sometimes they're fully backed
if they're not satisfied because it's
not a big value. They're fully backed by
a guarantee and it makes it easy for the
target audience to say yes. And once the
relationship is started, and I'm sure
you've seen this, and most other agency
owners have seen this, once the
relationship is started, it's much

## 153. The Exact System We Use To Integrate AI In Our Creative Agency

When I think more philosophically about
an approach to AI, it's really about
amplifying the value of what we do as an
agency to begin with. The first
principles of agencies really have not
changed. And and my view is that clients
are not hiring us for what we do.
They're hiring us for how we think and
what we know. It's part of the reason
I'm so bullish on small specialized
agencies because what you know about the
industry that you specialize in is a
huge part of the value that you bring.
Hey everyone, today we are joined by JD
Pro. He is the COO of Ricardi Group, a
New York-based B2B strategic marketing
agency focused on helping complex
organizations translate insight into
meaningful business impact. RG brings
together strategy, creative, and
operational discipline to build stronger
brands, drive demand, and deliver
measurable outcomes. Jay leads the

finance, HR, legal, sales, operations,

building a business that can grow with
intention and consistency. Known for his

wrong way to be thinking about it and
exactly as you're describing Pennywise
and pound foolish. But the the thing
that I think is important here, right,
is for an agency to have the
conversation with the client about what
actually matters and making sure that
they're thinking about what actually
matters. What are the things that we're
measuring against? What are the where's
the totem of what is actually important
here? And so what would your advice be
in having that conversation? Well, have
a real conversation about value and
outcomes. Like that's where the
conversation should start. What is the
north star of what we're trying to
accomplish here and everything that you
do needs to be reverse engineered from
that goal and that outcome? That is the
conversation you should be having. I
think too often again like in the penny
wise pound foolish mode like it gets
straight to cost and economics. Well,
can we do it for a little bit cheaper?
And you're not actually focusing on well
how do we drive the best result
possible? I know you're a fan of of
Blair Ens. He talks a lot about this
too, like in working with his clients
and folks that he's advised. Oftentimes
when you actually have the value
conversation, clients are willing to
spend more money when they realize I can
actually drive more value by blowing up
my budget a little bit. And so that's
the thing that you really need to be
talking about. It looks so different
depending on what exactly that objective
is, but that is everything needs to be
uh reverse engineered from there. I will
also say like marketers this is the time
to be deeply involved in the process and
do not be complacent in the procurement
process. I've seen this way too often uh
with RFPs that we've participated in
where where procurement has a lot of

taking uh a passenger seat and they're

that they're looking for. And most

servers for their firm is the guy who's
now evaluating their marketing agency.
and the marketers kind of like went away

Like, it's important that they're
involved. Like, that to me is like the
biggest sin you can commit right now.
>> If you're trying to get to the best

## 155. The Hidden Cost of Saying Yes to Every Client as a Creative Agency

I forgot which client we had that went
down this rabbit hole and they have been
doing like adjacent non-billable work
for like two-plus years and the work was
like over like $50,000 a year or
something that they should have been
charging for that they weren't and to
have that conversation with their client
was just like a "Oh, but you guys kept
saying yes and like it's not our fault
that you guys kept placating to a
Absolutely
wasn't. Or I just placating to us.
>>
>> Everyone, so agencies often have a
client selection problem and it usually
doesn't really show up at the the
immediate part of the relationship. It
shows up later. It shows up in delivery
issues, in team frustration, and scope
creep. And you know, really just clients
that never felt quite right. And what
we've seen over and over again is that
the cost of saying yes to the wrong
client is far greater than the
short-term revenue that they end up
bringing in. So, today Gear and I are

agencies go wrong here and what exactly
is it costing you and how to start

start a little bit, but

## 158. The Real Operations Behind Fast-Growing Creative Agencies

Most creative agencies don't fall apart
because of their creative work. They
fall apart because their delivery system
can't handle the growth. The agencies
that grow year after year, they don't
get lucky. They build infrastructure, a
delivery system that keeps quality high,
timelines more predictable throughout
the project, the team aligned even
throughout the busy season. In this
video, I'll show you exactly how high
growth agencies structure their delivery
pipeline so that they can grow without
chaos, without the burnout, and without
you, the founder, needing to be involved
in every single decision. This video
also happens to be part two of our
systemize your agency series. And if
you've already watched the SOP video,
this will layer perfectly on top. Let's
go ahead and jump in. So, let's first

things go from initial signed S.

that we communicate, the communication

client expectations, it's all of those
little pieces, those small processes,
systems that actually work in tandem
with each other that create for not only

maintain quality, and make sure that
we're having the impact to the client.

individual small little process systems

## 159. The Real Reason Clients Never Refer Your Creative Agency

Most agency owners are spending an
enormous amount of energy on winning
clients, the pitch, the proposal, the
onboarding and getting everything dialed
and then the project ends and it just
well stops. The last invoice goes out,
maybe a quick email, and that's it. No
structure, no ceremony, no real closing.
And what most agency owners don't
realize is that the way that you end a
relationship is often the thing that
they remember most. It's the last
impression. And in the creative
industry, last impressions turn into
referrals, testimonials, and repeat
business, or it just doesn't. It dies.
So, in this video, I'm going to show you
exactly how to offboard clients in a way
that keeps the door open long after the
project has closed. So, why is this
actually happening? Most often, we're
treating the close of a project as an
awkward formality. It's not a strategic
opportunity. So, they are rushing
through this off to the next project and
it ends up feeling a lot like it was
just a transaction. So there's no
structure which means to that it creates
loose ends you know that they're not
actually transferring things over no
file they're just final invoices moving
passwords over that sort of thing and
that's it and those loose ends create
frustration that overrides all the good
work and that last impression is well
the lasting impression. So what is
meaningfully at stake here is that
referrals don't just come from satisfied
clients. They come from the ones that
really truly remember you and
testimonials have to be asked for in the
right moment when they're actually
really happy. And repeat business is
entirely dependent on how your
relationship ends and how they end up
feeling. And one off-boarding bad client
can actually undo months of great work
and lasting amount of impact. And a
great offboarding process can generate
years of new business referrals and all
sorts of things. So the step one is
being able to actually have a meaningful
offboarding call, sunset call, whatever
you want to call it. You actually want
to make it official. The acknowledgement
that this project has come to an end.
You want to confirm the things that are
being stopped. exactly what's going on,
exactly how to transfer things over,
what they're going to need. Maybe
there's Loom videos for them to be able
to actually make edits to a website. You
want to celebrate the work, the
milestones, the achievements, and the
impact that it has had to their business
so that they can actually truly
acknowledge it and understand it. Make
sure that the client has everything that
they need moving forward with this work.
And so, this isn't a sales call. It can
actually be a moment of reminder of
what's going on. And there's meaningful
utility to this as well. Because one of
the things that I really like to do that
you can inject into this call is the
reality is is that as business progress,
there's always more that needs to be
done. There's always more business
challenges to solve for. And the reality
is assuming that you have more to offer
this client that is going to help them
actually further their objectives. You
should be talking about those types of
things. And so I've got a 7030 rule
here. 70% of the things that you're
going to talk about of strategic ideas
of what they can ultimately be doing are
things that your agency does and 30% of
them are things that you don't. So you
actually don't turn this into a sales
call. You're actually making it a
strategic conversation about how they
can actually achieve their goals faster,
better, stronger. The next step is you
should often be asking for a
testimonial. You should be doing it
during this call, assuming that they are
actually happy and not doing it in an
email weeks later that they can
ultimately ignore. You should be
specific on what you actually need. Is
this going to be written? Are you going
to be interviewing them? Is there a
platform that you're going to send over
them to actually ask for it? And tell
them exactly how you're going to be
using it. Transparency is really
important and it actually allows for
them actually to feel comfortable and
actually doing this and make it as
easily as possible. You're going to
share a link. You're going to actually
schedule the call. You're going to give
them the prompt. And the reality is is
that this becomes really easy at this
moment when you actually allow for them
to feel very great about the work. And
the reality is is that when someone
feels great about the work that was
done, they often are a person that will
send over referrals because you help
them transition into this phase, this
next phase of their business, you help
them achieve the outcomes that they're
looking for. And the reality is that a
lot of like people hang out with each
other and those people end up going to
conferences. They are, you know,
interacting with other colleagues and
they ultimately can be making
introductions to people. Making sure
that you also have an offboarding kit
for people. It's incredibly useful here.
It is the professional document that
actually wraps everything up in one
place, allowing for you to be able to
transfer everything over. So, this could
be the actual files. It could be a
portal of all the details. It's going to
list out the strategic documents. It's
going to have the final art that is
being delivered. It's going to be, you
know, the utility or training of
actually how to move everything over.
So, all the documents, the files, the
assets are handed over clearly. Make
sure that they have all the login
credentials that they have exactly what
they need. Making sure that everything
is clearly there. And so it is a quiet
reminder of how you actually offer
things for them in the future. And if
you have it in a client portal, which I
do highly recommend, whether it's a
simple internet that allows for them to
be able to download files in the future,
is that it is you become a constant
reminder while they're accessing those
oriented pieces. Make sure that they
have a clean and easy way to come back
to you. Making sure that they have the
contact information to be able to reach
out to the person that they should,
ideally, your account manager, so that
they can reach out to you when they need
help. What's really useful here is what
are the types of things that they
actually should be reaching out to you
for is like when you run into this
situation when you're in this scenario
when you're looking to accomplish X when
you've already achieved this milestone
it would be a good opportunity for us to
have a conversation and I'm going to
reach out to you at that moment as well
to also just rekindle a little bit of a
conversation making sure that you
ultimately go to that final step making
sure that they meaningfully understand
and you should really stay in touch. So
the offboarding should not be the end of
the relationship. It is a transition for
them. And so you should be checking in
occasionally, not to sell, but just to
connect, making sure that things are
progressing well, that those assets are
aging well, that they have the things
that they need. Do they have any
direction that they are going to need
support in? Do they have any questions
about how they can be using the the
assets of what you ultimately did
produce for them. Remember again,
clients come to you for the outcome, not
just for the deliverable. And so you
want to make sure that you're sharing
resources, things that came up, you
know, learning lessons and other
projects that are similar to them. That
reality, it actually builds a lot of
trust in you with that client, allowing
for them to actually reach out to you
more, and it also keeps you top of mind.
So the reality is is that agencies that
actually stay top of mind are the ones
that end up being called first for new
work when it comes up. People
transition, people move around. This is
also an incredible piece too is that if
your contact at the organization ends up
moving from one organization to another,
that is an incredible opportunity for
you to also be able to build a
relationship with another organization
that they have ultimately moved from.
And so this is a great opportunity. And
what does this actually meaningfully do
for you? It turns a finished project
into a future referral source. It
creates testimonial videos that you're
able to utilize in your marketing and
sales. It opens the door and keeps the
door open for you without having to
actually consistently needing to pitch
things. And it positions you as a
meaningful professional in the space,
one that actually understands fully what
they're doing. And perception is
everything. And it makes saying see you
later rather than goodbye feel like the
beginning of the next stage of the
relationship and ultimately not the end.
The way that you end a relationship
determines whether they come back, refer
others, or simply forget that you
existed. A great off-boarding process
protects all the work that you put into
winning and delivering that work for
this client in the first place and the
outcomes that you were able to achieve.
And if you want those clients coming
back and referring others, you need a
pipeline system that makes the most of
every relationship you build. So, I want
you to watch this next video on a simple
growth strategy that grows your agency
so fast that it feels like cheating. and
learn how to turn your best former
clients into your most powerful referral
source.

---

## 160. The Real Reason Creative Agencies Stay Small (and How to Break Through) ｜ Ben Gaddis

And so what we did with that billboard
was we turned it into the biggest
homeless sign in the world and we put a
homeless person on top of this billboard
for 24 hours and people would text in
and if they texted his name Danny to
this short code and donated $10 only,200
people had to do that. He got a new
house. He we raised millions of dollars.
It was on CNN. It was on every it was on
everywhere. It was everywhere. Um, and
and it was because we we just had like
this tiny tiny thing that we could work
within and it was probably the most
creative thing that we that we ever did.
>> And so I think that framing is it's a
cool challenge. It's something that as a
creative I'd be excited about.
Hey everyone, today we are joined by Ben
Catis, the co-founder of Superstep
Capital and former CEO of T3 and the
co-author of the book Embracing
Irrationality. Ben scaled his first
agency from a small creative shop to a
$50 million firm that later merged with
a $350 million organization before a
transaction with Blackstone. Along the
way, he discovered a powerful truth.
Heroics don't scale. Systems do. And the
businesses that thrive are the ones that
think irrationally enough to break from
break away from convention, move fast,
and value progress over perfection. Now,
as an investor and operator, Ben helps
founder le agencies and tech service
companies grow from 15 to 50 million by
turning brilliance into repeatable
systems and by challenging the
assumption that doing it the normal way
is the best pathway to success. Welcome
to the show, Ben.
>> Thank you, Robert. Great to see you and
thanks for having me. of course and
reading the book actually there was a
lot of inspiration that I took away from
it and the thing that you talk about is
really challenging leaders to think
differently than you know this kind of
idea of being irrational and I I'm
curious about what that fully means in
the business context for listeners so
that they have an understanding of the
premise behind the book. Yeah, the
premise is we spent a lot of most of my
career as what we called ourselves an
innovation agency. And what that meant
is that big companies paid us millions
of dollars to come in and help them
think differently. And we'd get to the
very end, they'd pay us all this money
and we'd build a strategy and we'd show
them, you've got to do something that
it's not just thinking differently, you
have to act differently, which might
mean you have to build a new business or
a new revenue stream that could be the
antithetical to your core business
model. and you've got to go do that. And
they would say, "Great, we want to do
that. We want to do that." And then at
the very end when they had to go to
their board, everyone say, "That's
crazy. Like, we're not going to create
something from a hotel chain like Airbnb
or if I'm a transportation company like
Uber." And at the end, most of those
initiatives didn't get over the line
because they couldn't think about their
business devoid of a constant. And we
think of a constant as a core pillar of
your business that if it changed, it
would make you have to rethink
everything. and constants are the enemy
of innovation. And so in the book, what
we tried to do is take all of those
learnings, all of those late nights
where we tried to get people to think
differently and show how you can do that
without having to leave a big
organization and get the entire
organization to embrace the change. Uh,
and you got to be rational to do that. I
mean, I think it's quite a topical
conversation considering the industry is
in this like big shift and there's a lot
of people that are starting to want to
look at how do we think this irrational
way or like how do we be truly inventive
and kind of get rid of the old agency
model these constants as you as you're
referring to them. And the the item that
I think is really interesting is you
know you've got the existing business
model that you're currently operating
that is paying the bills is paying
salaries is taking care of your
operations but then you need to make
this migration shift and change. So
you've got kind of two competing things.
One that you you do talk about a bit in
the book. One, I think you talk about
both of them, but one of them being how
do you maintain the existing business
while making this migration and then
two, how do you actually take people
along the way with you? Get them to
embrace this new form of thought.
>> Yeah. So the the process that we
outlined, it's really simple. One is you
have to identify the constants. And so
before you can do any of that, if you
don't understand what is driving the
core business today, like where is all
your revenue coming from? Is it
traditional agency services? and break
that down. Is it copywriting? Is it
strategy? Is it creative production? But
then from there, what would happen if
that changed? What if the model was no
longer time and materials? What if it
was all performance-based? What if it
was all valuebased pricing? What if it
was SAS or something else? And then from
there, instead of just blowing up the
model and saying, "All right, well,
we're going to stop doing everything
that we've been doing for the last 15
years and immediately shift everything
over." We call these these onederee
decisions. And one degree is really
powerful. Like if I was flying from
Austin to New York and I was off by one
degree, I wouldn't notice it at the very
beginning, but by the time I got there,
I'd probably be in Maine. I wouldn't end
in New York. And so those shifts today
are extremely important. And so we think
about how could you look at every single
decision that you make with a onederee
lens. meaning, is this going to push us
in the same direction as our new goal or
is it going to prevent us from getting
to that goal? And so those shifts have
to be, they have to happen every day,
but they can be really small, minute
shifts. Could we shift 5% of this
project away from time and materials to
performance? Could we uh take something
that we did on the last project,
productize it, and make it repeatable,
and it only makes up for 3% of that
revenue? Well, that's a great way to
start. And before you know it, you're
sort of closer to that vision than than
when you uh first thought about it.
>> Yeah. There's a client that was going
through a bit of this like ideiation
shift in their business model and they
they did a couple of things that I
thought was interesting. one there was
this progressive thought of a specific
team member and you do hit this in the
book as well but the idea that an
organization will one reject the idea of
these innovators the person that's
thinking irrationally that wants to
adapt the model and then the way that
they ended up instituting this that I
ended up kind of brokering between this
particular individual and the CEO was
why don't we just try it working
simultaneously the exact same project in
your old model the new model working
simultaneously yes you're going to
reduce margin on this specific project
but let's test it to see what it looks
like and just go live with it. And if it
doesn't go well, then we just don't
present it to the client. If it does,
then we can. What are your thoughts on
that?
>> I love it. I think that's an irrational
way of thinking, right? We're going to
take margin degradation on that one
project. But what you're going to get is
not only proof of whether that model
worked or didn't, but I bet that person
because you spent time on it and they
brought you in is one of their best

"No, we're not going to do that." Guess
what happens? That irrational person is
going to do what all irrational people
do? they're going to leave and they're
going to go start their own company and
it's going to be from day one built with
that new model and the likelihood of

a lot higher than them doing it in the

choice am I going to embrace this and
give them a shot which but you know
what's the worst that could happen we
don't present it to the client we lose a

motivated and and heard
or we piss them off, they leave, they

every founder that we have in our

>> I I mean there's like just such a

like perfection rather than going into
version one very quickly. They want to

>> Yeah, I mean they say what perfection is

do you say that? And I think I I think

understand where people come from. And
what we we actually did a lot of

happens is people actually physically
react to that that change in a way
that's almost like a fight orflight
mentality. And so I don't want to sound
like, you know, completely hippie- dippy
here, but you got to create a safe
space. You got to you got to you got to

it doesn't mean that you don't have

is let's think about first let's just

that sounds big. It sounds like it's

full-time uh geographic thinking about

talk about change, we're actually
changing 3% of the delivery model.
That's not that scary. If you just say

different conversations where people are
talking past one another. So if you
start with that, then you can say, "All

over the next five projects 2 to 3%
incremental change. By the time you get
on the other side of that, it's very

is significantly higher. And the
employee satisfaction around that is
usually pretty high because they don't

>> Maybe I'm a little bit hippie dippy. I'm
I am in California. So, the idea of
safety, I think, is incredibly important
uh for several reasons. I don't know if
you're familiar with Project Aristotle
from from Google. Are you familiar with
it?
>> I I remember the name.
>> It's from years ago. I mean, I want to
say it's like 15 years old. But this
idea that there's the a person in a room
that is like the naturalborn leader,
that the one that speaks up first, the
one that's conducting the meeting, the

And I think that there's two things that

that irrational thinker, the person that

innovative way of doing something and
then we end up quashing it by either

leader in the room, that they end up

making it more of like a brainstorming

me and my personality type, I am that

that creative person, that you need to

hearing.
>> I think that's the role of a leader and

maybe you facilitate it, maybe you

Okay, well, we probably should. Let's

Well, that will never happen, but if it

say what if uh what if people just

that would never happen like we it was

company that was a Michelin star

They were they had to spin up delivery
in like 3 days and now it becomes it's
it's actually for them a core competency
of hospitality outside of the

>> In the prior conversation in our in our

>> Yeah. So I think a premier agency number
one has an understanding of the role
they play in a market but it's driven by
a point of view and they know that if

whatever the budget was that they said
they need they know exactly what to do

is extremely important. But a premier
agency it shows up in the market. It
attracts the absolute best people. Those
people do the best work which means they
attract the best clients which becomes
sort of this virtuous cycle. And so we
think about building a premier agency
starting with a point of view and a
premier brand. And I think that is what
is missing in most of these services

but we don't have a point of view on
when all of those things come together
what the outcome looks like and how it's
different than everyone else. And so
that that's where we start and I think
that's probably what's lacking in in

>> And and what do you think that is? I
mean, I've got my opinions of why that
comes to pass in so many agencies, but
I'm curious as to what your opinion is
as to why that happens in the first

>> Well, one, I mean, you have to have

so it's hard to do, but I think it comes

everything to everyone. And when you
start to have a point of view, it seems

And I feel like you and I have probably
had these conversations so many times
with agency owners and it seems it seems

from it. But when you're in it and
you've got mouths to feed and you've got

it's going to be $500,000 of revenue,
it's really easy for me to say yes. and
and we can stretch for that, but every
time you do that, it takes away from
that core value. It makes it harder to

impossible to have a point of view that
stands out and is differentiated. And
so, I'm not saying you got to pass that
up, but I'm saying you have to start

in mind. And when you do, you can have

that like real hardcore point of view.
>> There was this uh social media post by a

or something that was talking about, you
know, posting your political opinions
online and whatever your political
opinions are. I I don't know that that's
the point of the conversation, but the
the thing that he mentioned is that if
you do this specific thing, if you were
to mention it, you're like pushing away
half of your potential audience and that
that's a bad thing to be doing. And I
sat with it for quite a long time of
well, I don't know that I agree. Like I
see his point, but I don't agree. This

both magnetic as well as detracting from
people. And being that premier agency is
that point of view. So many people are
afraid of being polarizing in that point
of view and that perspective. And my
opinion of it is that I am who I am and
there's people that are going to like
me, people that aren't, and I'm not

>> Yeah. If you think about just think
about it in in the context of thought
leadership, right? So, you know,
manifestation of that point of view by

being isn't that you're bringing
everyone together. It's that you're

you're really just not saying anything.
And there's not with all the AI slop and
everything out there, there's not room
for boring milk toast run-of-the-mill
content. And so that's that's thinking
about just content. Well, think about
when you talk to a potential employee.
Think about when you talk to a client.
You have to remember that they're
hearing the same pitch. They're hearing
there's 50,000 agencies out there.
Literally, there are 50. So to have
something unique is is extremely hard.
But when you go at it from a generalist
standpoint, you have to imagine that
client's heard that same idea 10 times
and when you say it with conviction and
believe that it is the first time they
hear it, it is the biggest turnoff for a

and go, I can't believe this person

now. We probably talk to 100 150
different companies a year as we think
about making investments and it it is

say something like it is revolutionary
when in fact I've heard it 50 60 70

>> I I find it I I agree. One of the things
that I find very interesting when it
comes to this idea of differentiation
and focus and point of view is that so
often talking with agencies where
they're talking about how is it that
they're going to position themselves in
the market. They first start by looking
at all of their competitors which I I
mean personally it's like the same idea
if I were to go back to, you know, my
high school years and trying to define
who I am going to be and who I am as a

Like that doesn't help me define who I
am. that helps me define the people that
I'm around and it doesn't allow for me
to plant my flag as a as a human being,
as an individual. And the same thing is

## 161. The Sales Mistake Almost Every Creative Agency Makes

So, you've already done the hard work.
You won the client, delivered the
project, got great results, and then you
moved on to the next one. But, here's
what most creative agency owners never
do. They end up never documenting it.
They never turn that result into a
story. And that story is the most
powerful sales tool that you have. Not
just your portfolio, not your pitch
deck, not your pricing page, a real case
study told the right way is something
none of those things can ever do. It
makes a future client look at someone
else's result and think, "That could be
me." In this video, I'm going to show
you exactly how you can build it. So,
here's the reality of the world. Is most
agencies end up confusing
testimonials with case studies, and
they're not the same thing at all.
Testimonials are endorsements. Case
studies are proof of what took place.
Testimonials are vague, and case studies
are incredibly specific. Most agency
owners assume clients don't want to
participate, and they don't have a
system around actually going about to
collect these items consistently. And
so, that's the reality of the world, and
I want to shift that paradigm for you.
And so, here's some things that I want
you to be thinking about. Is that the
true reality and distinction between
testimonials and case studies is that
testimonials can say that this agency's
great, and case studies show you why,
how, what, when, what took place.
Testimonials can be actually quite hard
to collect, where case studies tell a
story. There's the before, there's the
after, there's the intervention, there's
intrigue, there's learning lessons that
allows for someone to actually
understand the situation, to actually
buy it. I mean, bluntly, online, when
you're looking at something and you just
see that someone's had this
transformational story happen, and that
they haven't told you any of the in
between that actually shows how long and
how much hard and painstaking work it
actually took to achieve, do you believe
it? I don't. Your clients don't either.
So, you can always pull a testimonial
from a case study because there's a
dozen of them, many dozens of them
potentially. But, you can't pull a case
study from a testimonial because you
don't actually have the emotional layer
that's necessary to build it in the
first place. So, let's talk about why
case studies in the first place, why
they actually work. And so, they build
trust faster than any other marketing
asset really. They overcome objections
before they even happen because the
person actually understands the true
narrative of what took place. Let's talk
about the human existence for a moment.
When you think about things about how we
actually understand and create
connection with other people, it's we
actually understand where they've been,
we're able to actually empathize with
another human being. But for us, the
reality of the world is that we only
start to make action forward when we
feel like it's safe to do so. We are
incredibly adapt able to identify risk,
identify danger, and avoid it. That is
actually the thing that human beings are
the best at as a species, as an
animal. And the reality is that we are
no longer facing life-threatening
situations on a daily basis unless we've
put ourselves in them, generally at
least in the Western world, but we do
have financial risk, we do have sales
risk, we do have vendor risk, we have to
all sorts of other types of risk that
ultimately ends up having the exact same
trigger. In order for us to actually
feel comfortable moving forward, we
first have to believe that we are
capable of doing something. And that's
what case studies meaningfully are able
to do is they are able to actually have
someone understand, here's the situation
that someone was in, here's the thing
that they wanted to accomplish, I want
both of those things, but the story in
between allows for someone to truly
empathize with the situation and see the
pathway forward for themselves. It
allows for you to be able to position
yourself as the expert without you ever
having to say it because bluntly, having
to say it sucks. It's not fun. They work
24/7 with you in the background because
people are able to just watch them
whenever they want, especially if you
have them posted in a meaningful way.
And so it allows for you to help
overcome this imposter syndrome as well
because now you have loads of
affirmations that other people have
shared about you and the work that
you've been able to accomplish with
them. And so it allows for you to feel
more secure in the direction that you're
headed. So step one, I want you to
actually put a list of clients together.
I want you to prioritize the clients who
got great results. The ones who loved
working with you, the ones that have
measurable results of what the work
you've done has had impact and output to
the clients. Don't go after the
difficult ones, you want to start with
the obvious wins cuz it's going to make
things a whole lot easier. What I find
really useful is reaching out at the
natural end of the engagement, not
months later or years later, but when
things ultimately make sense with a
massive caveat. You need to have known
and they need to have been able to
measure the outcome of the work that you
put into place. So, the reality of the
world is if you are a branding agency,
then immediately after the project is
completed, it's not going to actually
have measurable outcomes. You're going
to have to wait some time. Start with
one. It's easy for you to be able to
reach out to just one and get just one
in the first place. You'll start to feel
better, easier about this once you've
gone through just the first one. Step
two, you're going to go through and
collect the story. My preferred way to
do it is in a live interview or a call
with them. It allows for you to have a
bit more of a dynamic conversation,
being able to actually get a bit deeper,
allow for you to be able to build even
more relationship with this person as
well. Because it allows for you to both
reminisce through the actual project and
the relationship that you have built.
You can ultimately do this with like a
tool like video ask that allows for you
to be able to do it asynchronously if
you find that your clients are just
incredibly busy and very difficult to
get time on. And you can have both as an
option depending on where the clients
go. There's three main things that you
must capture throughout this. First
place is where did they begin? Where
were they? That includes two components.
Measurable where they were background
and then how they felt about it.
Remember that we connect to how people
feel and we empathize with that. Then we
need to understand exactly what the
challenge was, what their concerns were
with it. It allows for us to capture
that that objective of what they're
looking to accomplish. And then finally,
the outcome. What measurably took place
and then how did they feel about it at
the end? So, those are the three things
that you must capture going through it.
It's in very important that you also
make sure that you have some component
of what you specifically did to help
assuming that you had this impact, you
actually did produce this work. They're
going to have mentioned you throughout
the process, but it's incredibly
important that you have specific
results. So, how is the business
different now? Did it increase revenue?
Has it increased LTV? Do they have
greater brand recall? Are their
advertising dollars working better? Is
it that people are now sticking better?
The conversion rate on their site's
better? What exactly happened
meaningfully that took that was
different before them? I do highly
recommend that you brief them before the
recording, making sure that they're able
to make eye contact in the right place,
that they use ideally the agency name
and not you as an example, making sure
that you're able to actually get through
things, but also giving them the numbers
potentially if they're not a numbers
person, the numbers that they need to be
able to share. Ideally, you want to keep
the whole conversation. The interview
necessarily can go a bit longer because
you're going to end up cutting this
down, but you want to try to target a
total of 5 minutes for this total
interview. Then you're going to edit it,
you're going to structure it. So, not
going to mansplain to all of you video
editing. Try to keep it under 5 minutes.
Try to cut out anything redundant. Make
sure that it's not slow and that it

recommend that you transcribe the video
so that you can use it as a written

is that archetype of a case study. The

that's why someone is buying from you.
They're buying an outcome, not because

and will make them feel more

you get permission to publish the work,
to publish this live on your site. You

changes when you start using case

## 164. The Smart Way to Invest in Your Creative Agency (and Actually See ROI)

So, if you want to grow your agency,
you're going to have to invest into it.
But how you ultimately invest or what
money you spend or where you spend it is
ultimately going to determine whether
you grow intentionally or you just are
burning your cash. Too many agency
owners either spend based on gut feel or
delay investment for too long because
they aren't sure where the return or if
the return is going to come or where
it's going to come from. So in today's
episode I want to break down how to
build a growth ready operational budget,
how to compare investments and use
simple risktovalue oriented lens and how
you can make some spending decisions
that are aligned with your revenue team
goals your own lifestyle goals. And it's
not just a number that you're looking
at, but recognizing exactly how it is
that you go about that ROI of investment
that you're currently making. So the
first piece that I want to start with is
the base operational or flat budget. So
the the way that I typically like to
build things out is that I like to have
multiple different scenarios. The first
is flat of if nothing changes
year-over-year, I stay exactly the same.
Then an operational that is going to
take trend into account. So what is the
existing trend of the business? Am I
going up? Am I going down? Am I growing
by 25%, been growing by 50%. Like what
exactly is the the trajectory that I'm
currently going? And if I am growing, in
some examples, I have clients that are
growing 50% or 100% year-over-year. And
I don't like to plan that from an
operational spend to begin with. And
then you have goals and stretch goals
that you would then have from there. So
you have more scenario-entoriented
budgets. So, I don't want you just to
look at last year's numbers and last
year's goals, but I want you to look at
the reality of the trend of where you're
currently going and then how you were
spending and then also make sure that
you're structuring the way that you're
looking at things in different buckets.
So, the buckets that you want to look at
the amount of expense that you have is
to begin with billable cost of delivery.
So, here's the cost that it actually
takes as a percentage of total revenue.
Here's the cost of the money that it
takes for me to actually deliver the
service. Then here's the hard cost that
you have as it relates to the delivery
of your service. So maybe your design
softwares, it could be the stock
imagery, it could be if you're if you're
an a events agency, it could be the cost
of production of the event, that sort of
thing. So here's the actual cost
eventually getting to your gross margin,
your gross profit. So like here's the
total cost of what it takes for me to
deliver because that scales with revenue
as revenue continues to grow and it
should be considered as a percentage of
revenue. So making sure you have your
salaries, all those sorts of things. And
then you want to break down your
operational expenses into a couple of
different buckets. First is the cost of
office, so utilities, your rents, those
sorts of things. Your GNA expense, so
your insuranceances, your office
supplies, those types of things. Your
administrative human resources and
payroll includes benefits, that sort of
stuff. And then sales and marketing. So
now you have everything kind of broken
into buckets. Some of which scale as you
grow, some of which allows for you to be
able to have some economies of scale.
And so the reality of is as you start to
get above the million, 1.2 2 million,
1.5 million in revenue, you start to
have greater economies of scale and your
your margins are able to start to get
better. So, as you start to have these
like different ranges in how it is that
your business is operating, as you get
over those thresholds, you start to be
able to have the 25 20%
in your total operational cost that
allows for you to be able to have higher
net margins. And so, the budget, and let
me just clarify something that I should
have said at the beginning that I
didn't, is the budgets aren't just meant
to track where you are. They're actually
meant to guide decisions. So hey, I'm
currently trending in this area of
revenue right now. Am I trending to
operational? Am I trending to goal? Am I
trending to stretch? Like where exactly
am I throughout this year from a
concentration of revenue perspective? So
you may have seasonality your business,
that sort of stuff. That allows for you
to be able to understand where you are
right now and kind of where you are
headed. So as an example, the first 6
months of the year that you have
throughout the year allows for you to be
able to start to guide where you are in
the second half of the year. So, let's
say that the beginning half of the year
you're a lot slower and then the second
half of the year you tend to have your
the real heavy kind of months of the
year from a revenue perspective. And if
you have to if you start your year in a
pretty high revenue oriented place, it
allows for you to start to make some
reasonable assumptions of where your
revenue is ultimately headed for the
year. And it's really useful for you to
think about it this way because it
allows for you to start to send see the
trend. Not saying that I want you to
blow money out of the water and that
sort of thing. you want to start to make
still the appropriate calculus of where
you're wanting to go, but making sure
that you have an understanding of what
that looks like so that you can start to
see what seasonality and kind of where
am I headed over the course of the next
6 months. The other thing that I want to
talk about is the risktoval calculus.
Like how do exactly do you think about
where do I want to invest? So, as you
start to grow the business, there's
going to be different pieces of you,
different pieces of your team that are
going to be necessary. So as you start
to extract yourself from different
components of the business, as you start
to extract yourself and want to grow
revenue in this specific segment, you're
wanting to have greater client nutrition
or client retention, lower your cry
attrition, and start to manage these
different pieces and start to look at if
I invest in this specific capability
within the organization. What does that
actually mean from a time savings
perspective, a capabilities perspective,
a revenue perspective? So what is the
actual potential upside if this goes
well? what is the potential upside? What
could I potentially gain? And and
everything ultimately there's
assumptions that you're going to be
making, but everything as you're going
into these types of investments, there
are base assumptions that you could
making on what does it actually look
like. So, I'm going to hire a director
of client service or director of
accounts. That means that I should be
able to assume that my client attrition
that is currently at 70, my client
retention is at 75. So, I'm losing 25%
of clients if I make a 10% improvement.
Not only have I covered the salary, but
I've also removed myself from needing to
be involved in accounts. I'm no longer
doing that. So, it allows me to reinvest
this time into sales, which then should
mean I should be able to generate
another million dollars worth of revenue
because of the time that I'm able to
invest in demand genen bisdev within the
organization. So, like, all right, not
only does that mean I'm able to add a
million dollars worth of client
retention revenue, but I'm also able to
add an additional million dollars of
bisdev because of this hire. So, I'm
spending 100,000, let's call it 175,
more likely, 200,000 for someone that's
going to be leading your accounts. And
then at that point, being able to remove
yourself from those components and allow
for you to be able to have the upside of
that. And then the other thing you want
to have is an understanding is what is
the downside risk as well. So, if I
bring on this person, what does it
actually have from a loss of capital
opportunity cost for you as well? What
does it actually look like from a net
margin perspective? How long is it going
to take to get them up to speed? What
are the reality of how long it's going
to take to have impact? all of those
sorts of pieces and essentially just
starting to understand of if it doesn't
go well, what does that look like and am
I okay with that? And what does it
actually look like from a timing
perspective? So, how long do I does it
take for me to know if it's working or
not? So, when am I going to see the
initial indications of the pathway for
that to be successful or is it starting
to actually become a little bit of a
challenge that I need to maybe change
the direction that I'm headed? And so I
want to be very clear here that it's
important. I I take this as a very
linear in my thinking, especially when
it comes to numbers and and finances, is
that you need to make a clear decision
at the time that you're starting to make
that investment. You don't want to have
to make a consistent different decision
over time and like, hey, you know, I've
hired this person. Do I keep them on? Do
I let them go? Like what actually
happens? Depending on how you're looking
at making this investment, I'm going to
make an investment for I'm committing to
it for 3 months. Once I've committed for
it for that 3 months, I need to see this
initial indicator. So here's what it
means. Here's what it would mean to let
this person go. Here's what it means
that I'm going to continue the test for
another 3 months. Here's what it means
for me to okay, this is working. It's
great. I'm going to keep this role. I'm
going to continue to run into the next
phase of the business. I'm looking to
solve this one particular challenge. Now
I can move on to the next. But make sure
that you're thinking about it in what
you're doing. Because the reality is
that if you start to invest time, money,
effort into things that you can
overcommit to something that ultimately
isn't working. And I want to make sure
that you're thinking about it in the
appropriate amount of time. Now, I want
you to then start to think about the ROI
assumptions to start to be able to
prioritize. So, you may have four, five,
six different potential investments in
your organization or in your agency that
you may want to be considering in this
moment, but how do you determine how do
you determine which one is the right one
for right now? So, I would start to put
them in a couple of different buckets,
right? So, it may be I'm going to spend
$2,000 a month on this tool so that I
can save 20 hours of time. It may be
that I'm going to spend $5,000 worth of
additional ad spend here so that I can
generate more leads. And so what does it
actually look like from what is the
investment of time? What is the
likelihood of success? What are all
these components that allow for us to
have an understanding of the investment?
The reality is that no investment is
risk-f free. There's always some aspect
of of of risk in involved in it. But how
do you start to prioritize the
likelihood of the outcome that you're
looking at? What are your immediate
goals you need to be able to accomplish
in order for you to be able to hit your
longerterm goals? and make sure that
you're leveraging past data to be able
to inform the decisions that you're
making. Right? So, let's say that I'm
considering the ad spend in the example
I gave, I know that with the current ad
spend that I have that with each $1,000
of ad spend, I know I'm able to bring in
an additional lead. Every two of five,
I'm able to convert into a client, which
means 100 grand worth of revenue. Do I
want to continue spending? I would say
in that calculus, yes, you definitely
do. And so, how do I make that
determination of what it actually makes
sense as it continues to grow and scale?
like how do I look at these potential
investments, these budgets, these
directional decisions that I'm making
that allow for me to be able to have
these components and realizing that
success is in a lot of instances is
predicated on the focus that you have to
be able to get this work done and that
you don't want to do 12 things kind of
meh. You want to make sure that you're
doing them well and that you're able to
focus the appropriate amount of
attention that it is required in order
for you to have the success within that
specific component of the business. So,
make sure that yes, this is going to
take this amount of dollars of
investment, but it's also going to take
this many hours of investment. So here's
the total cost that it has, opportunity
cost, actual cost of salaries, including
your own. And making sure you have an
understanding of what that actually
looks like. And then build in the

even if it's your own feedback loop to
yourself. Here's the way that I'm going
to look at the measurement. Here's the

it. That allows for you to understand
how is it that you measure the success
of that and when do you have that

mentioned earlier that you have the goal
budgets and I want you to start of kind
of the outcome that you're looking for

Here's the seasonality of my business.
So I can plot the revenue throughout the
course of the year. And then I'm going
to look at all right what does it
actually mean for me to be able to
obtain it? This amount of revenue is in
project revenue. This amount of revenue
is coming from retainers. This amount of

Here's my current client retention. What
does that mean from a percentage of of
of revenue am I going to need to be able
to have for my deliverable team as the

## 169. The Winning Strategy to Keep Clients Happy and Grow Your Creative Agency

I would start the conversation with
understanding where they are right now
and collecting the data to begin with.
And so from a sales perspective, you
should be understanding where they are
right now, where they're looking to go,

which they're going to measure the
success of this particular initiative,
and how are you going to measure the
success
yourself. Hey everyone. In today's

foundation of everything you do. Whether
it's retaining clients, growing their

the impact of the work that you're doing
is not only essential to demonstrating

go about initiatives to understand what
it's more about. We're going to talk
about why it's so crucial to
highlighting your results in a sales

## 170. These 5 Habits Will Supercharge Your Creative Agency Growth in 2025

In the book Atomic Habits, Jame Clear
says, "Success is the product of daily
habits and not really just a
once-in-a-lifetime transformations." And
I can say that from my own experience,
this is absolutely true. Things like
marketing, funnels, and sales pitches
can ultimately get you quick results.
But if you can develop good working
habits over time, you will become more
productive and make more money while
ultimately working less, which I assume
is your goal if you're watching this
video. So, I'm going to talk you through
the five most important daily habits
that have made the biggest impact on our
agency clients and their results. So
many agency owners ultimately let the
kind of day happen to you. They don't
really have a plan. They're don't really
have an understanding of how to decide
what is important now and what needs to
happen later. And being able to have a
clear understanding of how to allow for
you to be able to be programmatic
ultimately about your day. So, being
able to take a to-do list and put it in
your calendar, break it down into the
things that you should not be doing, the
things that you should be doing, the
things that are going to have impact in
your life and in your agency today, and
be able to plan it out very closely
detailed into what you should be doing
next and exactly how much time you have
allocated. And largely, when you have a
plan, you're more likely to be able to
have success. When you just go into the
day with absolutely no plan, you're more
likely to get lost in the process. Far
too often we think that we need to
sacrifice ourselves and that in order
for us to be able to have success, we
have to do more things and ultimately we
are busy and we are thinking that we
have to just sell two more clients and
that if we are able to do that then we
can have what we need, financial
resources in order to be able to hire
the person that we need. But realizing
that ultimately as we bring on those two
new clients, we're going to be more
underwater than we are today. And so
what I want you to be able to think
about here is that in order for you to
be able to have the success, you need to
focus more and do less. If you are
working on lead generation, you don't
want to have four different channels.
Focus on one, maybe two at a push and do
less that ultimately allow for you to
have the maximum amount of impact. And
this is true across every single thing
that you're doing in the business. The
vast majority of people, including
myself at a time, had the belief that I
had to do more in order to have more.
But the reality was is that I needed to
do less in order to have more.
It is really truly impossible to know
how things are moving along unless we
have a clear tracking progress. There
was an example in the book atomic habits
where James Clear is talking about and I
thought this was a great articulation of
how to think about this. What he
describes is a person in a room. The
room is below freezing. it is 30° and
that your objective in the room is to
run around as quickly as you possibly
can to warm up with friction, warm up
the room and to start melting some ice
on the table. You're running around
vigorously, not knowing ultimately that
you are increasing the temperature.
There's no temperature gauge. And so
you're running around, running around,
running around, not knowing that you
actually got the room with friction to
32° just at the point of freezing. And
if you did it just a little bit more,
you would know that you were actually
getting to that objective of what you
wanted to do. But because you do not
have a mechanism to track your progress
to understand the impact that you are
having, many people ultimately give up.
So I want you to focus on the things
that you need to accomplish, measure

## 171. This 2% Adjustment Doubles Profits for Creative Agencies

So, the easiest way to grow an agency is
to focus on the tiny tweaks that have
the biggest impact. And after helping
hundreds of agencies achieve their
growth goals, I've realized that there's
one type of change that truly stands
out. Where a 2% change can literally
double your revenue. So, in this video,
I'm going to explain what that change
is, how you can apply that change to
your agency, and at the end, I will show
you how I helped an agency make an
additional $2.5 million in revenue using
this exact strategy. But in order to do
that, I need to very quickly explain the
concept of inputs and outputs. So what
are they? Inputs are the behaviors, the
actions that you're doing in order to
achieve a result. And the outputs are
the results of that input. So for
example, let's say that you are going
and doing email marketing. So the input
could be the number of emails that
you've sent. And the output depending on
the campaign could be the number of
clicks or the calls that you booked as a
result of that campaign. Or for example,
let's say that you're working out in the
gym. And so the input could be the
number of reps that you're doing and the
number of exercises that you're doing at
the gym. And the output could be the
amount of muscle gained or the amount of
weight loss that you experienced. So at
this point, you might be wondering, why
does this matter at all to me, Robert?
And what does it mean for my agency? So
the tweak I'm referring to is going to
be found within your marketing and sales
data. So, in order to understand how to
make the right tweak, we need to
understand your data because identifying
your inputs and outputs will allow for
you to be able to determine which of
your sales and marketing efforts are
delivering you results. And you'll know
which input you need to adjust so that
you can get the outcome that you want to
have. And what also is really important
here is that you'll also be able to see
the patterns and so the pros and cons of
what action you took is having to the
output. So, let's use an example that
directly relates to agencies to show you
how big the result can be from
identifying and tweaking an output. So,
let's suppose that you run an agency
that generates 30 new clients per year
at roughly $80,000 each, which would
gross you around $2.4 million in total.
So, to generate those 30 new clients,
you must generate 120 proposals every
year. To produce those 120 proposals,
you need to generate 400 marketing
qualified leads. you probably need
somewhere around 8,000 total prospects
or leads in your marketing funnel to get
to those 400 MQLs. So imagine that you
did a tweak of just 2% of improvement on
this. So now you're qualifying 7% of
your prospects. Right now you need 560
MQLs. And so now you've got 180
proposals compared to the 120 that you
had previously. And so now you're
closing 27% versus 25 of your clients,
putting you at 49 clients and 3.9
million in revenue that year. So this 2%
improvement that you made in this funnel
allowed for you to be able to increase
your revenue by $1.5 million. I'm sure
all agency owners would welcome a boost
like this. But ultimately, how do you
get to that 2% improvement, right? So,
in order to do this, you're going to
have to be able to get under the hood of
your marketing and sales operations and
identify what the inputs are so that we
can dramatically improve the outputs.
So, let's say for example, right, that
the problem that you're having is that
you have the right amount of traffic,
but you're not getting the right people
on the phone. So, your MQLs are kind of
failing you. And so what I often see in
this example is that the copy that
you've utilized to identify the
challenge of your ideal client is not
actually hitting the mark. So someone
that has just started their business is
going to have a completely different
challenge than someone that's doing 10
million a year or 50 million a year. And
so the challenge that you need to be
articulating, the copy that you need to
have in front of them needs to be
different. And not only are you
attracting the wrong person, but you're
also dissuading the person that actually
needs you and that has the revenue to be
able to afford you. Because if you're
talking about a lower level problem,
you're attracting the person at that
level, but you're also dissuading the
person that is your ideal client because
they're like, "Oh, I've already solved
that. I don't need this person." So, the
thing that I see happen in a lot of
instances is that agency owners look to
just the top of the funnel to be able to
look at how do I increase revenue. So,
let's say that the goal is for you to be
able to make a 50% improvement in your
revenue this year. And so, in order to
do that, what most people think is the
lever is I need to generate this much
more traffic or I need to send this many
more emails, 50% more emails in order
for you to be able to get there. So,
let's 2% improvement, right, is so much
easier than that 50 or 100% improvement.
So, at a 1% you're usually, for the vast
majority of you, if you have a 1%
improvement in multiple touch points in
your sales and marketing funnel, are
able to have a 50% increase in your
revenue. At a 2%, you're able to have a
100% or near 100% increase in your
revenue. And there's lots of things that
you can do to allow for that to be able
to happen, but it is incredibly easier
to be able to make a small improvement
than to make such a dramatic
improvement. And if you want to make
sure that your own agency is as
profitable as possible, I'd recommend
downloading our free agency analyzer in
the description below. So, I want to

dramatic adjustment in the way that they

increasing their price point to $120,000
