## 173. This Simple Growth Strategy Scales Your Agency so Fast It Feels Cheating

Most agency owners think that growth has
become incredibly complicated. They have
to run more ads, they have to do more
outreach, they need more content, they
are going to need more budget. But
agencies growing fast today are using a
strategy so simple it can almost feel
like they're cheating. They are not
spending more, they're just finally
using what they already have, their
existing happy clients and partners.
So in this video I'm going to
show you exactly how to build a referral
program that turns your best clients
into your best promoters for free. So
why do most agencies not actually not
have a referral program or system? They
just believe that referrals are always
just going to happen naturally, so they
have no structure, no system, no
consistency. And so it's actually
leaving one of the most a cost-effective
growth strategy on the table because the
reality is is that when you have people
brought in from trusted recommendations,
they are so much better of a lead than
you could have done from any type of
marketing. And so why is this actually
true? Is that warm leads actually
convert at a much higher rate. They
actually have a higher threshold of cost
associated. They're a lot less price
sensitive. You know, they clients
actually come in with you already with a
built-in amount of trust before the
first call even happens, so that
actually makes projects a lot easier to
deliver on. And these referred clients
often stay longer and they end up
spending more with you. And so they're
already primed to say yes. And so it
creates this like really meaningful
quick oriented structure, so the amount
of the sales cycle is a lot shorter.
They're just primed to work with
you in a more ongoing reoccurring basis.
So let's talk about what the actual
steps look like. The first step is
actually define what existing referral
sources you have today. It's a little
bit time intensive, not overly so, half
a day, maybe less, but just going
through all the opportunities that
you've had in your door over the
last 12 months. If you have more time,
go ahead and do 24 of where did
everybody that came through your door
come from? Did this come from an
outreach? Did this come from a referral?
Did this come from a partner network?
Who was it that actually came through
the door? Not whether you sold, not just
the clients that you sold, but every
single opportunity, every proposal,
every sales conversation that you
ultimately had. Who did they come from?
Make a list of when that happened and
who they were introduced by, making sure
that you have an incredible list of what
these what your actual network looks
like.
At the end of it, you actually have an
understanding of where you're starting
from for this system. Then, the next
step is to build your system.
And the little suggestion here is that
there's a book called Referrals Without
Asking by Stacey Brown Randall. I would
check it out. Incredibly informative
book that will go through how to build
some of these oriented pieces of your
system as well. So, the thing that's
really important here is that you need
to be staying in touch with folks. The
reality is is for you and me, as time
progresses, we end up meeting more
people, you end up getting a little bit
further away, and you just forget about
people. And so, you need to stay in
touch with people. You need to meet them
for lunch. You need to maybe send little
gifts, you know, on their birthday. It
can be a quick, you know, happy birthday
oriented video to them. It could be
something that is completely random.
Hey, just thought about you today
because of X, Y, and Z reason. It could
be a handwritten card that you send out
when you actually get connected with
someone. And one of the things that I
have found incredibly useful and just
really nice is actually celebrating wins
of where things ultimately are. The
thing that's really important that I
want you to recognize here is that a
referral actually isn't about you. I'm
going to say that again. It actually is
not about you. The reality of why you
actually get a referral is that someone,
a friend, is talking to a colleague, is
talking to someone that they are having
a relationship with, and they have a
problem. That person that they're
talking to has a problem that they need
to solve. So, the person that is
actually sending you the referral very
commonly is just actually doing it out
of a nicety for the person that they
care about. And so, you
>>
>> you are being entrusted with the
relationship they already have. And so,
it's incredibly important to recognize
that. It actually isn't about you. You
just happen to solve the problem that
this person ultimately has. And so, one
thing that I find really useful is
actually celebrating the wins that are
actually going on, keeping this person
up-to-date around how things are
actually progressing because they're
trusting their own reputation and their
relationship with you. So, acknowledge
that, understand that, and take that
with the respect that it deserves. But
one thing that I have really
enjoyed is like as an example, a
branding agency client of mine, when
they launch a new brand, they actually
print the logo, the the graphic work of
what it is that they're producing, and
send it to both the client as a
celebration, as well as the person that
they were referred by. It allows
for both of them to actually celebrate
this milestone in the relationship, and
actually being able to actually keep
these relationships actually moving, and
it creates for this additional dopamine
hit because the reality is is that when
someone makes this referral, they're
helping a friend, and it makes
them feel good for having done so. And
so, when you actually elevate that that
dopamine hit for them, you're actually
just repeating it and actually making
them want to do it more. The next step
is actually growing your network. So,
depending on where you were in step one
of how many people do you have, if you
have even five, it's actually a
reasonable referral network to start,
but do continue to grow that network.
One of the easiest ways that I find to
do this is there may be YouTube
channels, podcasts, publications, where
people are actually targeting your same
ICP. You should be reaching out to those
people. They might be accountants, it
could be lawyers, could be consultants,
could be all sorts of people that work
and provide services to your client. The
ideal person would be someone that works
with them immediately before they are
going to need to work with you, but
build relationships with those people.
You can send them a quick message of,
"Hey John, Vanessa, whomever, you know,
I was taking a look at some people in
the space. You look like you have an
incredible reputation. I promise that
there's no sales pitch coming. We both
work with XYZ type of client. We don't
compete in any way. I thought it would
be useful for us to get to know each
other. There may be some opportunity for
us to collaborate." Have that
conversation, get to know that person a
little bit, grow your network.
Incredibly vital. The reality is is that
as you continue to do this, there's a
meaningful compounding effect. As you
impress new clients, they have become
your advocate, your brand advocate. They
continue to end up on that growing
referral list, the list of people that
are referring to you, and you are
continuing to stay in touch with them,
continuing to stay top of mind, and
you're continuing to allow for them to
feel that immense amount of dopamine
hit, that positive feeling for actually
solving someone else's problem, and you
continue to make that happen over and
over again. The reality is is that as
you start to build these systems, your
referral program, referral system, is
going to allow for you to be able to
stop feeling like you're starting from

leads that are coming in that are
replacing that cold outreach. That
growth becomes a lot more predictable.
It's not actually accidental. You're

of people actually making introductions
on a regular basis. And these
best clients are coming from your best

program fills your pipeline. But if

executive producer and founder of the Ox
Co. Danny has spent over two decades
producing high stakes campaigns across
broadcast, digital, and experiential
with brands like Nike and Hulu. After
years inside big holding company
agencies, she launched the Ox Co. in
2017 to champion a new agency model,
helping small and midsize shops being
able to grow smaller, collaborate
better, and deliver bold creative
without the overhead and burnout of the
old huge conglomerate agency system.
Today, she's here to talk about how
agencies can rethink competition,
embrace fractional expertise, and why
building a community and real world
brand experiences may be the next big
shift that we have in the agency space.
Welcome to the show, Danny.

## 185. Why Clients Don’t Choose Your Creative Agency (It’s Not Your Work)

You're not losing prospective clients
because your work isn't good enough.
You're losing clients because your
prospects don't understand the value
behind the work. If your pitch feels
complicated, or if your explanations
feel long-winded, or your prospects keep
saying things like, "Let me think about
it." Or, "I'm not sure that this is the
right time." It's rarely a strategy
problem. It's almost always a value
expression problem. So, prospects aren't
rejecting you. They just don't get what
your work actually does for them. So, in
this video, I'm going to break down the
simple value expression framework that
makes clients understand your impact
instantly. Once you use this, prospects
will stop hesitating and start seeing

can start getting some new clients. So,

specifically within that specific
segment or that type of work. And the
reality is that it's not typically that.
It's predominantly clarity. So you
haven't translated the creative work
that has an impact to their business. So
the reality is is that people are

All
right. So, most agencies think that

I think that's really just the baseline,

that clients who stick around for the
longest often do so because they it's
how they feel about working with you or
working with your agency. So, in today's
episode, we're going to talk about how
to go beyond the deliverables and
intentionally build a client
relationship that surprises, delights,

your agency apart. These are the things
that don't show up in the scope of work.
This is what is going to make the
difference though in how clients
ultimately see you and perceive you. So
the reality is is that us as a species,
we are predominantly a very emotionally
based creature. And as my Angelo has
said, people will not remember what you
did, but they'll remember how you made
them feel. And that's not what you
ultimately delivered. So what you
delivered is what you did, but how you
went about it. All the things that
happened around it is how you made them
feel. Did you make them feel seen? Did

as a human? Were you friendly? Did you

relationship. And surprising and

to make them feel seen, appreciated, and

## 189. Why Creativity Won’t Grow Your Creative Agency

So, many creative agencies like to
believe that they win because the work
is better. And sometimes, early on, that
might even be true. That better work can
get attention. It can create
credibility. It can even win accounts.
But, at a certain stage, creativity
stops being a competitive advantage. And
when agencies continue to optimize their
output, their creative output, growth
ultimately stalls. The top 1% of
agencies don't grow because they're more
creative, they grow because they
understand the business that they're
actually in. And then they end up
shifting their focuses accordingly. So,
let's talk about that for a moment. The
predominant thing here is that agencies
believe that creativity is the primary
creative and competitive advantage. They
assume that better work automatically
drives more growth and better growth and
bigger accounts and all of that sort of
things. The reality is that creativity
will attract a lot of creative people.
And that will be an incredible means by
which for you to attract talent, which
is an incredibly important component of
the business. But, the reality is that
clients evaluate risk, not just
aesthetics. Aesthetics are important.
They need to be creative, it needs to be
impactful, it needs to be all of those
things. But, just pretty and great
creative in and of itself is not the
thing that clients are looking for. The
appreciation of the craft is there, but
it is not more important than all of the
components that go into a purchase
decision. And so, here's why I'm hitting
these points. So, if the buyer valued
creativity at the same depth as you,
they would actually just be a creative.
They would be a designer. They would
have spent more of their life there. The
decision-makers on the brand side, on
your client side, are prioritizing the
business impact and ultimately risk
mitigation in what's actually happening
from the work that you're producing. And
so, we have to understand that world,
that equation between the two. Is how
exactly does the creative output of the
thing that we're producing, which does
have business impact, how do they
actually have direct correlation? So,
competing on creativity alone, frankly,
will commoditize you. Unless you have
this incredibly huge name like
Pentagram, competing on insight is going
to elevate you. Because the reality is
is that's what exactly what they're
looking for. In this incredibly complex
market, they're looking for insight.
They're looking for directionality.
They're looking for how exactly can they
have a competitive edge in the existing
market? And so, the reality is that the
more that an agency is competing on
insight, strategy, narrative clarity,
risk reduction are the ones that
ultimately win. Because what clients are
ultimately asking throughout, whether
it's a scoping process or in the
creative process, is why this direction?
How is this going to actually have the
impact that we're looking for? How is it
that this specific insight is going to
support the direction that we are headed
in the thing that we are looking to
accomplish? How does this directly tie
to a business outcome so that we are
able to renew this type of activity next
year? That we're able to continue to do
this marketing activity. That we're able
to continue to invest into brand. And
then, what makes this option less risky
than this option? And how are these
alternatives actually playing against
each other? And what are the likelihoods
of that potential outcome? The reason
why I'm sharing this with you is that
the more of that we start to think as a
creative agency and less about creative
output, but more about strategic
thinking and strategic framing, the more
that our work has this direct
correlation between the connectivity
between both. And the reality is is that
greater creative work is founded in
really impactful strategy. And without
the two combined together, both are
ultimately going to fall flat. And it's
both of them are actually important. The
reality is is that clients understand
one more than the other. And if they
were, like I said before, if they were
the creative, they would be doing the
work themselves. And the insight that
you're able to provide in the strategic
direction, in the research that you've
done, in the insights that you're able
to provide because of your expertise, it
actually makes the decisions that either
the person at the organization or the
owner that you're talking to or the
C-suite person makes the decision that
they are looking to make defensible,
which is incredibly important for them
because it makes them feel comfortable
in the thing that they're doing. And
when you have the higher amount of
defense and understanding and rationale
and risk mitigation, and opportunity
creation, the more that you actually are
able to justify your premium price. The
more that you have insight in the
creative work that you're producing, the
metrics that it actually produced in the
outcome, the reality is that the more
that you're able to do that, the more
authority that it creates for your
agency. And the more authority that your
agency has, the more leverage that you
have in negotiation, the more deals that
you have hitting you in in in coming
into the front door, and the more people
that come knocking at your door in the
first place. And so, here's the the
shift that I want us to be thinking
about is that the early stages of being
an agency, the biggest growth constraint
is how do we actually get this work
done, right? Is we're still figuring our
stuff out, whether here's the kind of
work that we do, here's the our
aesthetic style, here's the things that
are actually happening, here's how we
engage with clients. So, it's first
constraint is just getting clients,
having any visibility, and basic levels
of proof that we're able to say we're
going to do something and actually
produce those things. The next stage is
actually having to have clarity around
the way that you're positioning the work
in the market. So, here's the plethora
hundreds of thousands of agencies in the
United States alone, and how exactly is
your agency positioning yourself amongst
the rest? Obviously within the segment
that you're working with, but how do you
position yourself amongst the rest?
Then, how exactly do you go about your
offer structure? How exactly are you
positioning the process by which you
follow? Some kind of variation of the
things that are going on in the market,
but how exactly are you systematizing
the way that you go about things? And
then, the way that you then sell and
intake their problems, output direction
that allows for you to have meaningful
sales system. And then now, the
meaningful growth stage that is allowing
for you to have not only meaningful
sustainable growth, but maybe you've
potentially starting to get into to
scale is client retention, which is
entirely predicated on result driving.
It is delivery infrastructure, it is
margin control, it is leadership depth.
And now, here's the thing that you you
need to recognize between each one of
these stages is that the agencies that
continue to optimize purely only on
creative output is that that growth
constraint has shifted really in each
one of those different stages and energy
gets misallocated. Not saying that you
don't want to be investing in your
creative output, you do, but it just
shouldn't be the number one pillar of
focus for you. But if it is that that
key focus, the thing that you have as
the number one metric is that come hell
or high water, this is the most
important thing that you need to be
doing and you should be doing great
work. Don't get me wrong. Your growth
will stall. That output will improve,
but your revenue ultimately doesn't
because you're not actually tying it
back to the thing that's really
important. How did it have the impact to
the business that you're working with?
How did you go about mitigating the
problems and risk factors that were
associated? How do you go about making
sure that you're able to measure the
components that are going on that allows
for you to be able to solve the problem
within the reality or boundaries, the
limitations to what that business had.
And then you have loads of different
creative ways that you're going about
it. And here's the thing is that there's
the comparison between solving the
problem purely that you like the
creative output versus the problem that
the market has and that you have. And so
what agency owners are often solving for
is creative refinement, pushing the
work, aesthetic upgrades, portfolio
improvement, which again, those are all
great things to have. But as a creative,
those are the things that allow for you
to look at your work and then feel
validated in the thing that your life's
pursuit has been. But the reality is is
in order for you to be able to be
remunerated for it, to be compensated
for it, you have to be solving for a
couple of other things, too. Pipeline,
positioning, retention, pricing. And
those pieces are all immediately
impacted by not only the fidelity of the
work you're producing, but the impact
that that work had to the business that
is paying you to do so. And that is
predicated on the strategy, that is
predicated on the insight, that is
predicated on the direction, right? The
whole idea of measuring twice and
cutting once. Making sure that you're
doing that meaningfully and that you're
embedded in the actual thinking of the
organization. And when this happens, the
creative work feels more productive
because it is. The system is going to
really work in a meaningful way and it
attaches the strategy to the creative,
right? The thing that you care most
about, the work that you have refined
and perfected and care a meaningful
amount about, that it is probably still
having the impact, right? Because it is
incredibly brilliant, but you have to be
able to have the attachment between the
two that allows for it to be more
meaningful. And that emotional
attachment that you have to something,
that decision-making distortion, if you
will, right? Like you you want to make
sure that there's the emotional
commitment, the pragmatic commitment to
it, too. And the greater that you have
as a leader, the more mature of a leader
that you become, you start solving for
the constraint that you have in the
business, not just the comfort zone that
you ultimately feel that you've been.
And the reality of the world is is as a
leader of a business, we are often the
limitation to its own growth. And how do
we actually grow up beyond the place
that we are as a leader? So, if you're
struggling with closing in your
business, the reality is that
positioning is likely a problem. You
have client churns, or you're having
business fall out and going out the back
door as much as it's coming in the front
door, your margins aren't where it
should be, your profitability isn't as
isn't there, your revenue is a lot a lot

pipeline that's continually coming in,
your team is feeling a bit chaotic in
the work that is actually being
produced. What I want you to think about
is what is the meaningful bottleneck
that I have right now? Which one of
these is the predominant thing that's
the problem? And then solve for that.
Let's actually start to solve for the
problems that we are facing. And then
don't just double down on your craft,
because I I
do fundamentally believe that you have
to be great at what you do. But that's

want to build up now, you want to grow,
we are going to have to grow beyond
that. You are assumed now that you can

let's talk about why strategy and

baseline, execution is expected at this

you're going to produce what you listed
in the scope of work and it is going to

the level of insight that you're able to

architecture of what it is that you

allow for you to make those types of

what the constraint is and what output
needs to be, so that not only have you
improved upon your craft, but you're
able to meaningfully grow beyond that.
Not just how do we make better work, but
how do we have impact to the business
that we are in at this stage, not only
for ourselves, but for the clients. It
allows for you to have meaningful focus
and realignment. Your systems are able
to improve better. Your authority is
going to strengthen, and you're able to
have a meaningful growth to the way that
you are perceived in the market. If
creativity isn't what differentiates you
at scale, then what does? It's how you
frame insight. It's how you reduce risk.
It's how you control the narrative in a
sales conversation. Because once the
constraint shifts from craft to
positioning, the next battle happens on
how do you sell it. So, if you want to
understand how strategic agencies handle
sales conversations without
over-explaining or having to defend
their work, go watch my video on how to
crush sales objections, because growth
doesn't come from better slides. It
comes from better control. I'll see you
there.

---

## 191. Why Most Creative Agencies Aren’t Actually Profitable

everyone. So, a lot of agency owners
ultimately believe that profit is what's
left over after their expenses, but
that's ultimately the mindset that keeps
them just a bit stuck. So, if you're
only profitable when you're doing the
work yourself, or when when one big
client ultimately sticks around, you
don't really have a business, you have a
job, and even a job that isn't very safe
and secure. So, in this episode, Darren
and I wanted to be breaking down exactly
how to go into this kind of profit first
thinking really makes it how it would
work in practicality for an agency. So,
we'll talk about what makes an agency
model profitable and how you do it by
design where most agencies kind of just
go off the rails and why the scaling
without having a profitable foundation
is a trap that is going to break you and
your business. So, the format that we
want to kind of go into it is thinking
about this like initial kind of profit
first thinking and what exactly is that?
Uh I love to have these kind of sessions
where we get to just kind of deep dive
into the mind of an agency owner uh and
kind of elaborate on these different
topics. Right? So overall what is profit
first thinking?
>> So the mindset of the profit first and I
just want to give credit where credit's
due. Profit first is um a book that was
written by Mike McCowitz. So profit
first thinking is where you have profit
ultimately is one of the first
priorities. So you have a percentage
that you've designated specifically to
profit and it's taken out immediately.
It is not what's left over. It is not an
afterthought. It is actually the first
thing that ultimately happens. And so
the idea frankly is a lot around the
idea of Parkinson's law is that humans
will utilize what available resources we
have available to us. And so if you
remove those available resources, profit
to begin with, you are not going to
actually end up eating away at your
profit by increasing your expenses and
ultimately having a profit first
mindset.
>> So how is this different from the way
that most agencies currently view money?
>> I think it's not just for agencies. I
think it's generally how most people
view money. It's like here's the bucket
of what I have and let me spend it and
hopefully there's some profitability
left over is really the how it all
ultimately functions. But the
practicality of it is that so often
there's no real constraint placed around
a business. And like we have these
conversations pretty consistently. In
fact, we got an email earlier today from
a client of, hey, what budget can I
spend this money from? Like I want to go
buy this thing. Where can I pull this
money from to do so? One, I think it's a
good thing that this question is being
asked. And fortunately, we had the
foresight to know that there was going
to be a need for this budget. So, we
actually had a budget specifically set
aside for this need for the client. but
actually having constraint around it. So
what ends up happening for the most part
is that there's no real framework by
which anything is done within the
business. There's no means by which to
make a decision, a buying decision. And
so it's incredibly important to
recognize that you need to build your
business to be designed for it to be
profitable in from day one. So making
sure that you have a target margin that
you're headed for that you can then
reverse engineer from there your pricing
model, your delivery model, the way that
you go about investing and bringing
people on. Are you hiring someone
full-time? Are you bringing on a
contractor? Like what exactly is the
model that you are following here? And
one of the things that becomes this like
really massive trap for so many
agencies, and I see it happen anywhere
between like that $500,000 line to that
$1.2 million in revenue line a year is
that up until that $500,000, their model
that they currently operating under kind
of works because they are the primary
delivery person. They may have
contractors that they brought in here
and there. They may have even hired
someone on full-time to do so. But once
they get to that stage where they can no
longer do all of it, they end up having
to hire people. And every single hour of
someone working extra, doing going above
and beyond, having scope creep, it ends
up actually costing them money. And one
of the number one questions I get from
an agency in that phase of business is
why am I making less money now? I'm
almost twice the size I was, but I'm
making less profit now than I was
before. And that's because they didn't
actually build and reverse engineer
their model to make sure that that model
worked in the first place.
>> Interesting. I do hear that a lot as
well when people start to hit that that
point where it's like, okay, cool. I am
currently doing 250,000 in in revenue,
but I'm taking home 96% of it cuz I'm
doing all the work. But it's like, now
I'm at 500K in revenue. I expected to be
at like $400,000 in revenue, but now I'm
at 150. What's What's going on here? How
is this not translating to me bringing
in double? Yeah, the math is not a math
thing. So, it sounds like a lot of this
is done in the planning stage. So, what
are what are the kind of key elements of
having that profitable agency model?
>> The reality is is that we're a
service-based business, right? So, the
most expensive thing that we ultimately
have is the cost of labor, whether that
labor is you or that labor is someone
else that you've hired or is a
contractor, what have you. One of the
number one things that I'm thinking back
to a a client in outside of New York
City that when we first were working
with them, they were asking they were

a a typical number that so many agencies
ultimately end up having, but it's like
this $140 number that they have as their
blended rate across all the different
functions within the business. And so
then I one I'm not a fan of the blended
rate because I just don't think that it
makes sense from a basic business
fundamental perspective. But what the
blended rate tends to miss is not every
hour within a workday is productive
time. We then end up missing cost of
benefits. We end up missing cost of
overhead. We end up missing cost of
insurance, rent, etc., etc., etc., etc.
And so the primary thing that we have to
be looking at is what are all the
elements within the business that
actually are the components that are
necessary in order for us to deliver
upon the model that we're following. So
here's the framework. Here's the
process. Here's the thing that we are
going to deliver. And so what are all
the different components? It's like the
idea of I've got this Apple Pencil here
in front of me and I have plastic. I've
got the the components inside of it.
I've got a chip inside of it. I've got
the battery inside of it. I've got all
these different components, these
different elements that need to go into
it. And what so many agencies miss is

manufacturing oriented model is you have
to think about all the different costs
associated being able to understand all
right for me to know that I'm going to
take this product and I'm going to
manufacture it. This is the cost of
delivery but I also have to get it from
the manufacturing plant into the hands
of the customer. I have to then market

well. So thinking about it is not just
the actual cost of delivery but
overhead, profit, marketing, sales that

layers are often missed in actually
building that base model. So, one of the
things that we do with pretty much every

is go into and uncover their initial

those elements taken into account so

profitable or at the very least if
they're going to make a choice to be
less profitable in this project that
they do it with all the information.

## 195. Why Your Creative Agency Is Always One Project Away From Failing

Most creative agencies are one bad month
away from a crisis. Not because the work
is bad, not because clients are unhappy,
but because the entire business is built
on a model that resets to zero every
single month. So, if you run a
project-based agency, you already know
the feeling. You close a great project,
the client is getting the work, the team
delivers, the client is happy, and
you're straight back to hunting for the
next one. And so, you can't see past 90
days, and the cliff is always there. So,
in this video, I want to talk about why
that happens, what it is actually
costing you, and how the most stable
creative agencies in the world have
fixed it. So, let's first just define
the 90-day cliff. So, the reality of
project-based model is that revenue just
frankly just resets every single month.
And you may have multiple months within
the given project, but the reality is is
the same. Is that you don't end up
having visibility past 90 days. And this
is true for large mid-size agencies that
are in the 50 to 75 employees to the
boutiques that are one to three to five
employees. And so, the reality is is
that they just cannot see past that
90-day time line. So, every decision
that is made is made under this this
shadow of what's next. Can I afford
this? What's going to happen? This cliff
is always there. So, then it ends up
creating this dynamic where you're
afraid to make hiring choices and
investment choices because you know that
there's a cliff. And even in the good
months, you're afraid of what that means
in a future month because well, maybe I
haven't seen an opportunity come down
the pipe. But then you end up having
more opportunities come a week later.
And so, it's just this constant cyclical
place where you feel that level of
anxiety. The reality is that there is a
good side to project-based work as well.
So, there's always a motivator. Hunger
drives hustle. It drives motivation. It
drives momentum. And it ends up forcing
the team to stay sharp. It forces the
team to stay competitive. It forces the
team to push above their weight. And the
reality is is that there's no time for
complacency because the pipeline is
always moving and there's always
something to be doing. There's always a
new thing to be selling. There's always
a next stage of what you need to to
doing. And frankly, some agencies just
truly thrive on this energy. So, I want
to be very clear that while I'm going to
go through this, if your agency is just
very much focused on this project-based
oriented environment, and the reality is
is that some industries just thrive more
on this type of dynamic, it may not be a
fit for you to go more on the recurring
side. But, let's let's take the inverse,
right? The bad side of project-based
work is that that constant stress bleeds
into your leadership decisions because
you're in this feeling of famine,
really. It's that you're not really sure
when you're going to be able to bring in
the next client, when you're going to
get that next yes that will allow for
you to be able to afford your expenses,
your payroll, making sure that you have
that going on. So, you're constantly
reactive to hiring, to pricing, to
bluntly, most things. You're reactive to
most things. It's hard to plan, it's
hard to invest, it's hard to grow, it's
hard to know where you're going next,
what's what's coming around just that
next corner. And so, one slow month can
threaten the entire operation where
you're going to have to go through a
layoff, you're going to have to make
some meaningful cutbacks. And the
reality is is that when that happens,
team morale suffers when uncertainty is
the norm, especially if they end up
feeling it. As a leader, it sometime can
be hard to make these segmentations or
separations from the stress that you
ultimately feel. And if the team ends up
feeling it, even in the example of like
if you've had to do a layoff, even more
so then that they understand that things
are unstable, and it ends up creating
more tumultuousness for your team as
well, which really they, generally
speaking, are not adapt to be able to
feel. And so, the reality is is why do
most agencies end up stuck in this
model? Is that retainers can often feel
a lot harder to sell. Owners don't know
how to package the ongoing value and how
to actually go about selling a retainer
model. Clients end up pushing back is
that we're not going to need that every
month, we're not going to need to
engage, let's just start with a smaller
project, that sort of thing. And so,
there's no system in place to convert
that one-off client into a recurring
oriented engagement. And there's a
comfort in the familiarity of where you
currently are, even if it's painful and
you really don't like it. As in it may
be something that you've just come
become accustomed to. And so let's talk
about what that retainer model actually
looks like and how you can go about it.
So, the reality is is that clients are
not buying monthly content. They're
buying monthly progress and you have to
make sure that you understand the
distinction between it and that your
packages need to be built around the
client's problem, not your deliverables.
And exactly how it goes about taking
them from point A to point B. And the
best way that I like to actually put
together these types of packages is by
thinking about how quickly someone wants
to get there. As in the reality is is
that each one of these different
packages will get you to where you want
to go. Just one version is going to get
you there faster and you need to be
making sure that you know what their
pain points are and how you're going to
go about solving for what they are
experiencing and not just thinking
through exactly what the service is. So,
pricing is important to make sure that
you understand exactly how to go about
thinking through what exactly that
outcome is going to be so that you can
meaningfully attribute the value that
you are providing them with that outcome
is and so that you are able to
meaningfully structure the way that
you're communicating the value of this.
And the entirety of your ability to do
and close these types of deals is by
being sure that you understand exactly
how to communicate that. And the reality
is is that going about this kind of
winging it ends up killing your close
because you don't actually have that
sale appropriately structured that
allows for you to be able to have them
understand and and be able to fully
quantify the impact that it's going to
have. So, let's talk about converting
your existing clients and existing
engagements into retainers and bluntly
it should be one of the easiest sales
you've ever made because they already
trust you. They already know you. They
already have a clean understanding of
exactly who you are. So, there's a whole
lot less of a sale that's really needed
and the reality is this for new clients,
you need to qualify them, they need to
qualify you, you're going to have a
discovery call, all those sorts of
pieces. And the reality is is that you
don't need to have that. The way that
you should go about this is that based
on the way that you actually go about
setting up the structure of your
retainer model and there's loads of
different ways about setting them up.
But the reality is is that as you solve
one specific problem that they have,
what they have come to you for that
initial project, the reality is is that
business is not without its challenges
for its next stage of growth. And what
is the client looking to accomplish over
the course of the next three, six, nine,
and maybe even 12 months? And how do you
actually fit into that growth journey
for them? And so making sure that you're
having a conversation about where
they're headed next, what is it they

you have had this relationship with them
for several months makes you incredibly
well positioned to have that
conversation, to allow for you to really
meaningfully understand where they are
so that you can meaningfully solve for
it. One of the important things that I
want you to think about here is that
what does change when you have these
ongoing relationships, these retainers,
is that you're able to see past the 90
days. You know exactly what your revenue
is. Obviously, there's still attrition,
it's not without some ups and downs, but
you're able to have a lot more security
and what it looks like 90 days from now.
So, you can now start to hire with
intention and not be as reactive. Your
leadership decisions can feel a bit more
clear and you're not operating out of
fear. And the motivator can stay because
you're focused on what exactly that
outcome looks like. And also, for every
new project that you're currently
bringing in, there's still an
opportunity for you to be able to sell.
And you're that motivator of the new
business sale is still there as well
because you're still have those clients
and those prospective clients that
you're still talking to. So, retainers
solve the revenue cliff, but they only
work if your team can consistently
deliver month after month without
burning out. And that starts with how
you manage the people doing the work.

## 196. Why Your Creative Agency Is Stuck at the Same Revenue (Brutally Honest)

If your agency has been stuck at the
same revenue for months, maybe even
years, you probably think it's demand.
But here's the uncomfortable truth. Most
agencies don't plateau because of
demand. They plateau because their
pricing, hiring, delivery model, and
risk tolerance are perfectly optimized
for the revenue that they are currently
at. You didn't accidentally get stuck.
You built a structure that holds you at
exactly where you are. So, in this
video, I'm going to break down why that
happens and what exactly needs to change
if you want to be able to break through
that glass ceiling. Let's start with why
agencies plateau at that same revenue.
So, the same pricing structure, same
client profile, same team capacity, same
acquisition volume, same founder
involvement is the plateau. That
stability becomes kind of reinforcing,
and the owner becomes the glass ceiling.
The structure around us is the thing
that creates that see of sameness. In
order for us to accomplish something
new, we have to do something different.
And the reality is is that most of us
are continuing to do the exact same
thing over and over and over again,
assuming that somehow it is going to
ultimately end up changing. And our own
leader mindset is that first we need to
change in order for the business to
reflect it, especially if you are a
single owner founder. And it's
incredibly important that you allow for
this shifting dynamic within the agency
to then start to reflect that that
changing future.
The optimization trap that I see, and
why this is created, is that your
pricing matches your current delivery
capacity. And so, the reality is what
this ends up creating is that growth
becomes a financial risk. So, if your
current capacity in every single
delivery component within the agency is
fully matched to your current pricing
and exactly what's currently going on,
you don't have any space or capacity to
be able to hire new people if someone is
slightly below their utilization,
then it actually creates a meaningful
financial risk for you to go and hire
someone new to allow for you to be able
to get to that next stage of growth. And
so, then your team size matches the
current workload. So there's ultimately
then no space to grow. So if you have
the exact same amount of team members
for the exact same amount of workload
that you currently have, then you are
not able to bring on new clients.
Barring obviously that there's some
opportunity within contractors allowing
for you to be able to grow and being
able to allow for things to adjust
slightly. So there's some opportunity
there and some variability. Your
marketing budget matches last month's
revenue. The reality of what your
current operational budget is fully
spent to your current situation. So
you're not able to build a war chest.
You're not able to invest appropriately
if you need to lean a bit more into your
marketing. You need to lean a bit more
into hiring. You need to lean a bit more
into this specific area that you don't
actually have the cash reserves or war
chest that allow for you to be able to
do so. Which leads to the next piece is
that risk tolerance matches your
existing comfort level. And I want to be
very careful here is that I'm not saying
that you need should be taking on an
inordinate amount of risk. You
shouldn't. But it actually creates an
area of complacency within the agency.
And reasonably risk is normal. And you
need to understand exactly what type of
risk tolerance are you ultimately end up
feeling. And I see very frequently, so
many times I've seen this where agencies
will grow to a certain size
and then because of the numbers, the
amount of zeros that are involved, the

nervous, ends up making the wrong types
of decisions, and ends up creating for
their own ceiling. And the final piece
is that everything is calibrated to
maintain, not to expand. So that's how I
want you to be thinking about all of
this is that if you have set up the
system to be exactly maintaining, like
we do still want sustainability, but we
also want sustainable growth. And you
need to be able to create the space, the
capacity, the opportunity for that
growth. And without it, you're going to
end up staying in exactly the same
place. And so why is demand ultimately
not the problem? What I imagine you
might be saying is like, this is all
well and good, Robert, but the reality
is is that I don't actually have the
deals that allow for me to be able to

exact same market, and there's always
available demand in every market, even a
down market. And here's just to clearly
articulate this, right? In 2008, there

but the reality is is that there were

there's those that are growing in the

fairness across the entirety
of the economy. Some won, some lost.
And that's true in this market, even in

internal thinking. Demand just didn't

enacting it, the adaptation to the new

Want to go through a couple of these

the wrong level. And here's the core of
what becomes the problem here.
Is that under-priced services attract
lower-tier clients. Those lower-tier
clients create higher operational strain
because they are very demanding. The
reality is is that for smaller
businesses, some of the investments that
they're making is a lot of money for
them. Whether it is a lot of money or
not, it is a lot of money for them.
And so because it's a lot of money for

strain creates problems for strategic

actually becoming your ceiling. And so
there's a couple of pieces that are
necessary in here. One, it's incredibly

value that you're providing. And so, you
have to do a comparison to what that
looks like. What is it meaningfully
creating from an ROI perspective? Is
that fair for you and for them? Because
it it needs to be in polarity. You
should be largely to a reasonable degree
at a higher end of that ROI echelon,
allowing for you to deliver at a higher
value than the current price. Doesn't
mean that you need to be vastly
underpricing, and I'm not suggesting

ROI than your competitors. But, still
then, looking at what exactly does that
look like from a pricing perspective
that allows for you to be able to have
the financial capacity to be able to
grow. And then, thinking about capacity
as that ceiling. So, when we are hiring
reactively, it keeps you constantly in
this place of catch-up. And so, that
refusal to invest ahead of that growth,
before your revenue's locked in, it ends
up creating a problem. And so, if your
team can only handle $80,000 a month,
you're going to stay at that $80,000 a
month, but the reality is is this is a

what you were previously charging 80,
you now have $7,000 a month that you can

individuals that allow for you to be
able to grow. And so, it allows for you
to be able to create for that capacity
strain. So, I was just wanting to make
that cross comparison between the two.
And then, that founder-level bottleneck,

the risk tolerance that we currently

fulfillment. We are avoiding these

you continue to stick your hand in
places, it is going to constrain your

think about price raising, whether
that's for existing clients or new

in this allow for you to be able to make
meaningful change. Higher for that next
level before you feel ready, but with
the right financial metrics. You do want
to make sure that you have the
meaningful cash reserves to allow for
you to be able to afford that.
Increasing your acquisition
intentionally, allowing for you to
understand here is my cadence of what's

and then building delivery for higher
capacity. So, now that I have an

for me to look at making slight

You have to redesign the systems by

## 198. Why Your Creative Agency Isn’t Charging Enough (And How to Fix It!)

How is it possible that for the same
piece of work, some creatives are able
to charge $50,000 whereas others

of work that much better? It is actually
due to three much more simple reasons
that have very little to do with the
work itself. And the good news is is
that any creative person can start using
them today to start charging what they
are worth and start making the money
that they deserve. In case you don't
know me, my name is Robert Patton and
I've helped over 100 creative agencies
to charge what they're worth and
dramatically increase their profits. The
primary reason that you are not able to
charge your worth is that you are
selling your services wrong from the
first place. You are not focusing on the
outcome, but you're actually selling it
like you would sell a pen. And you don't
sell pens. You sell your services to
achieve an outcome. So let's say that
you are a web design agency. And rather
than actually looking at what the client

accomplish, you focus on the features.
What are the modules that they want?
What are the pages that you need? How

is it going to look like? Do you want

everyone the exact same way. Every

They have different priorities and they
make decisions differently. And if you
can't read that, adjust, then your deal

closers, they think differently. They
read the client fast and adjust how they
communicate so that the prospect

there's a cognitive dissonance. The
exact same structure to the
conversation. You need to think about

than thinking about here's the questions

using the same language we are talking
in the exact same pace. The reality as

to actually communicate appropriately.

type. So, start with visionary. Here's

## 209. Your Agency Revenue Is Capped Because of This

If you feel exhausted running your
agency, but your revenue or profit isn't
necessarily bad, this video is for you.
Because burnout is not about just
working too many hours. It's about
spending too much of your energy in the
wrong place. And what most agency owners
don't realize is this. Burnout doesn't
just make you tired. It quietly lowers
your decision quality, reduces your
strategic thinking, and caps your
growth. So, in this video, I'm going to
show you what burnout actually is
costing you as an agency owner, and how
to fix it by rebalancing your energy,
not just your calendar. So, let's start
with burnout being about balance and not
just like this idea of time. So, the
vast majority of people ultimately are
talking about burnout being just too
many hours. And you can absolutely work
too many hours and and create burnout.
So, I'll put that aside for now, but I
ultimately think that it's there's a
bigger dynamic that's ultimately going
on. You can burn out at 40 hours a week,
you can and thrive at 70. And I'm sure
that you've had that situation happen in
your own business, too. The reality of
how I actually see burnout, and the most
consistent way that I see it happening,
is that burnout is more a prolonged
energy drain. That you're misaligned in
the things that you're doing or
misaligned in your focus. You're
misaligned in how you are actually
feeling within the business. So, you
burn out when the tasks that you were
doing is that you're not uniquely
qualified to do, and it's actually
causing a drain in every single time you
do it. You end up spending your time in
these like frustrating loops, things
that you are in a working competency or
working frustration in the way that
you're actually working and in the way
that you're functioning. And so, you end
up operating more reactively rather than
strategically and doing things that you
truly enjoy. So, I fundamentally believe
that burnout is an energy imbalance
problem and not just a calendar problem.
So, how can we look at making an
augmentation to the focus of our time
and attention and where our mind is
being spent versus just looking at it
from a dollars and cents number of hours
perspective. So, let's talk briefly for
a moment about what burnout is actually
costing. So, the reality of the world is
that when you are in burnout or edging
towards burnout, your decision quality
is the worst. You have lower clarity,
you're lower quality hires, lower
quality offers, you're selling at from a
point of frustration. So, you're doing
things differently, your partnerships
are not coming in from the right place,
you're not focused on the right things.
Your decision quality just is flawed,
plain and simple. Your strategic
thinking becomes more difficult because
the idea of continuing to be in that
same kind of place for 6 to 12 months or
even continuing to be in business in 6
to 12 months feels like like a difficult
thing. And so, it changes the way
they're even looking at strategic
thinking, whether that's for your client
or for you, but the dynamic completely
shifts. It ends up creating a revenue
ceiling. So, those two things end up
leading to the symptom of a glass
ceiling for you. So, you are optimizing
ultimately in this point for just
maintaining because the idea of
expanding feels like more of you that
needs to be sacrificed. More of you that
you're going to have to deposit into the
business and you don't have any more to
deposit. So, it ends up changing the way
that you show up, which then also
creates for this authority component
both externally as well as internally.
So, it ends up causing a cultural drain.
So, when you are in this place of
decision quality being less, you're
you're frustrated and strategically not
thinking well and not feeling clear in
what you're doing, you end up showing up
as a leader as exhausted. So, you're
focusing entirely on short-term
decision, kind of managing things as
much as you possibly can, completely
disconnecting from things that you
should be involved in, completely
involving yourself in things that you
shouldn't, and you end up in this like

## 214. Your Freedom Through Setting Client Boundaries - Episode 180 - The Agency Blueprint Podcast

Setting client boundaries isn't about
saying no. It's really about creating
clarity and fostering a more productive
relationship by ultimately defining how
you engage, when you're available, how
quickly you'll respond, and you can
ultimately free up your time while
giving clients the structure that they
need to succeed.

improve your client relationships in the

symbiotic way with their clients and
